responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Considerations of Early Attempts in Co-Operation Between Religion and Psychiatry

Symposium No. 5. (New York: Group for the Advancement of Psychiatry, 1958. Pp. 55. $0.40.)

H. Home

H. Home

H. Home  

It is difficult to see what purpose a Symposium of this kind is intended to serve. The protagonists are psychiatrists on the one hand, and religious leaders drawn from various denominations on the other. With one notable exception, the contributors do little more than state the practical terms on which they feel co-operation is possible. There is general agreement that psychiatrists should not try to do the work of clergymen and vice-versa, and that both have a role to play in society. So far so good, but discussion at this level merely avoids the fundamental conflicts which everyone knows exist and which are of real interest. Only Professor Hofmann of Harvard seems prepared to face the full implications of a thoroughgoing debate.

71

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Phyllis Greenacre, M.D. (New York). 'Experiences of Awe in Childhood.' Pp. 9–30.

Awe reactions in childhood are most frequently associated with the phallic stage. Such reactions as are described are screen memories. Penis awe occurs in both sexes, but is less intense and traumatic in girls, perhaps because in the boy there is less discrepancy between the body self-image and the image of the man. The greatest impression is made when the phallus is seen in a state of excitement.

The awe reaction entails fear and intense admiration with some degree of exalted envy. When the focusing is chiefly on the organ and does not involve admiration of the man, the reaction is complicating to the oedipal relation, and persistent attempts to possess such an organ may lead to repetitive receptive homosexual experiences and/or the development of stubborn competitive fantasies with pretentious but passive attitudes in reality performance. Where the fixation is on the ejaculatory process, a self-delusion beginning in latency that urination is a seminal ejaculation may develop. Urethal erotism is marked and disturbances of potency follow.

The two contrasting images of the phallus often persist into adulthood. The inner psychic situation may produce a small penis complex. The reaction to the phallus may be duplicated in the total self image and lead to character disturbances. The awe reaction is variously associated with inspiration, creativity, and religious feelings.

1Published by International Universities Press, New York, and Imago Publishing Co., London.72

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Heinz Hartmann, M.D. (New York). 'Notes on the Reality Principle.' Pp. 31–53.

Dispositions for future ego functions whose growth later influences the pleasure and reality principles exist even before birth. Freud assumes that the tendency to gain pleasure present from the beginning predominates at an early stage over the tendency to avoid unpleasure. Both together cannot fully account for the institution of the reality principle in the sense of postponement of discharge and toleration of unpleasure; we have to assume that the development of ego functions enters the process as an independent variable.

The pleasure-unpleasure balance, that is the sum total of pleasurable and unpleasurable elements in a situation now, includes a consideration of the future. Quantitatively, identical pleasure-unpleasure balances may produce different reactions according to whether the accent is on pleasure gain or the avoidance of unpleasure. This will always remain a variable both developmentally and individually. Anticipation of the future includes judgements on the relations of cause and effect, both as to what happens in outer reality and in the child's mind.

Further, structural differentiation induces a state of affairs in which earlier sources of pleasure lose their pleasurable qualities. In this domestication of the pleasure principle by the formation of the superego not only the reality tuned functions of the ego but also its organizing function play a part. The direct use by the ego of the pleasure principle for the mastery of outer and inner reality becomes at this point important. In the case of the danger signal, an unpleasant feeling is intentionally reproduced by the ego for that purpose. The pleasure-unpleasure balance is changed when an instinctual desire is renounced by the gratification deriving from parental approval, as well as by the pleasures inherent in ego activities (sublimations) themselves.

Throughout life the influence of objects may work for or against objectivity; the acceptance of 'conventional knowledge' may be adaptive without being objective. This influence is less in the area of perception (though the processes by which sensory data are integrated are accessible to subjective modification). It appears principally in concept formation, habits of thought and emotion, and the use of language which opens the way both to objective thought and the preservation of stereotypes. In the same way the formation of the superego acts as a motivation to the objective appraisal of the inner world as well as to self-deception. Clearly the preponderance of one adaptive ego function may mean the weakness of another, in itself equally adaptive. These complexities of the reality principle are best thought of in terms of a variety of independent ego functions and their interactions and of various states of intrasystemic equilibria.

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annal) 11, 195611

Seymour L. Lustman, Ph.D., M.D. (New Haven). 'Rudiments of the Ego.' Pp. 89–98.

Inherent autonomic endowment is thought of as one of the nuclear apparatuses from which the primitive ego emerges, upon which subsequent formative influences impinge and with which they interact in the ultimate development of body and mental ego. This is seen as in keeping with Freud's inherited ego factors, and seems to corroborate Hartmann's concept of an 'undifferentiated stage' and of a 'conflict free sphere of ego development' as well as to support his formulation of ego autonomy.

Concerned with the general effect of inherent autonomic endowment on personality development, the author undertook an evaluation of sensitivity of erogenous zones in thirty-two neonates, using the reaction of the autonomic nervous system as a measure of sensitivity. There seemed to be suggestive experimental evidence that the oral area is the dominant erogenous zone in the neonate while oral sensitivity did not exclude sensitivity in other areas.

Individual differences in response to stimulation of the lips varied between non-reaction and hyperreaction. Non-reactors were not necessarily phlegmatic, but possibly too immature to respond. These varying degrees of sensitivity and autonomic reactivity constitute the primitive sensation levels from which the subsequent perception of oral activity develops. This is at first in terms of the autonomous factor in the conflict-free sphere of ego development (perception). It is conceivable that sensitivity and autonomic reactivity participate in id impulses, a hypothesis according with the concept of the 'undifferentiated stage'. Environmental influences, including the complx factor of the mother's personality, will also be experienced according to elements inherent in the endowment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Elizabeth R. Zetzel, M.D. (Boston). 'An Approach to the Relation between Concept and Content in Psychoanalytic Theory (with special reference to the work of Melanie Klein and her followers).' Pp. 99–124.

In addition to the task of clinical validation of analytical findings, theoretical formulations in psycho-analysis need to distinguish between and correlate propositions mainly related to the content of the unconscious mind and propositions of an abstract conceptual nature regarding the nature and function of the mental apparatus. This thesis is illustrated in the following ways. First abstract formulations divorced from meaningful content may be compatible with divergent points of view, as illustrated by a comparative study of recent papers by Hartmann, Kris, and Loewenstein and by Anna Freud with formulations of Mrs. Klein. The area chosen for comparison relates to the types of conflict aroused by aggression and the means by which the aims of aggression are modified. Second, failure to make adequate distinction between concept and content may lead to semantic confusion as illustrated in Mrs. Klein's premise of an active death instinct and her suggestion that anxiety derives from fear of the death instinct; contentions in which her elaborations of content are not necessarily controversial but which are highly speculative in themselves. It is also suggested that the abstract, objective conceptual approach (of Hartmann, Kris, and Loewenstein) for instance, may have significant limitations in a science based on essentially subjective concrete data. The thesis of the conflict-free autonomous ego is set alongside Susan Isaacs' definition of fantasy as 'the mental expression of instinct', in the light of which the concept of neutralized energy would appear relatively unacceptable. While Hartmann emphasizes neutralization as a means of liberating instinctual energy from its unconscious sources, Isaacs contends that ego freedom depends on unconscious fantasies which endow the overt activity with unconscious value.

It is obviously essential, if Melanie Klein's work is to be correlated within the main body of analytic theory, that she should indicate more clearly how and in what manner true reality testing and secondary process thinking can be understood in terms of her basic premises; how and in what manner maturation plays a role in different stages of development. On the other hand it appears that valuable though Hartmann's conception of conflict-free autonomous ego functions may be from a conceptual and descriptive point of view, it is nevertheless desirable that these formulations be correlated more closely with detailed description as to the relationship between neutralization and specific phantasy content. This correlation of concept and content is significant not only from a theoretical but from a clinical point of view.

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

David Beres, M.D. (New York). 'Ego Deviation and the Concept of Schizophrenia.' Pp. 164–235.

A more precise instrument than the general concept of ego strength is needed in the study of ego deviation and schizophrenia. Such an instrument is the detailed analysis of the separate ego functions. This paper makes the specific ego functions, their development in childhood, the resultant ego deviations and the relation to the concept of schizophrenia the focus of consideration. The interrelationship of functions in an approach to the human psyche as a unit, however, is not overlooked. Sections are devoted to the relation to reality, regulation, and control of instinctual drives, object relations, thought processes, defence functions of the ego, autonomous ego functions, and the synthetic function.

1Published by International Universities Press, New York, and Imago Publishing Co., London.74

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

L. Bryce Boyer, M.D. (Berkeley, Calif.). 'On Maternal Overstimulation and Ego Defects.' Pp. 236–256.

Freud chose to call the high perceptive thresh-hold of organisms a stimulus barrier. As its efficiency diminishes with the child's growth, object relations develop. Before the nursling can adequately perceive stimuli, the ideal mother serves as a supplementary barrier against traumatic internal and external excitations. Infants whose maternal protective barriers are deficient develop ego weaknesses and inadequate differentiation of ego and id. If the stimulus barrier or the maternal protective barrier is deficient, or if the drive strength of the child is unusually increased, ego development may be disturbed. If in addition the mother adds traumatic stimuli, ego development may be abnormal.

A fragment of a case of a catatonic woman provides information which can be interpreted to mean that her stimulus barrier was broken by the mother's adding external stimulations and failing to relieve inner tensions, by using the baby to solve her own problems and specifically through continual auditory excitation.

A tentative conclusion is adduced that under certain conditions of mother-child relationship in infancy, the accentuation of auditory stimuli may cause an unusually strong bond between the Gestalt of being mothered and the auditory sensorium, that is, the development of a strong link between oral and auditory sensorial spheres.

1Published by International Universities Press, New York, and Imago Publishing Co., London.75

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Annemarie P. Weil, M.D. (New York). 'Some Evidences of Deviational Development in Infancy and Early Childhood.' Pp. 292–299.

This paper deals with certain early phenomena encountered in infants and prelatency children which, considered against normal maturation, may be prognostic of the wide range of disturbances which vary from near psychosis on the one hand to all degrees of ego disturbance on the other. It seems that the development of such children lacks integration at all times. They show peculiarities and unevenness of their general maturational patterning, of their physiological functioning and of their psychological apparatus. They show delay in ego development and frequently deviations in the expression of aggressive and libidinal drives. This is often associated with an overload of tension evident from an early age and with various manifestations of anxiousness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Rudolf Ekstein, Ph.D., and Judith Wallerstein, M.S.W. (Topeka). 'Observations on the Psychotherapy of Borderline and Psychotic Children.' Pp. 303–311.

An earlier paper described the fluctuating availability of different ego organizations in the above type of child, ranging from psychotic manifestations to advanced achievement appropriate to chronological age. The present paper describes the implications of these observations for psychotherapy.

In the therapy of neurotic children, interpretations are addressed primarily to the more mature ego achievements. Such interpretations often result in panic and the precipitous disruption of contact in the borderline. Or they may succeed in superimposing a pseudo-secondary process upon a shaky foundation as a concession to an outer demand for social adjustment and conformity.

An example is cited where the therapist, mistaking the level of ego organization from which the child's material arose, interpreted a displacement from the transference as with a neurotic child. The resulting breakdown of ego functioning is accounted for as follows: 'In general the mechanism of displacement represents a relatively advanced achievement in the hierarchy of defences. For it is associated with the differentiation of self from the outer world and its efficacy as a defensive manoeuvre can be said in large part to be predicated upon the stable cathexis of ego and object boundaries. For the psychotic child the ability to use displacement as a defence frequently signals a significant progress because it is associated with a growing capacity to maintain object cathexis.'

A second example is cited where the therapist interpreted 'within the regression'; that is, the interpretation rested upon a temporary willingness to assume that the patient's grossly distorted perceptions reflected outer reality, because they accurately reflected his inner psychological reality and the state of his ego, which had temporarily lost the capacity to differentiate inner and outer reality.

'By returning with the child to ego stages where contact is almost disrupted and by reliving early experiences, primitive transferences are evoked or maintained which can serve as the foundation for new and more mature identifications. This maintenance of the therapeutic relationship, often made possible by interpreting within the regression, thus lays the foundation for the new development of identificatory processes rather than the superimposition of an imitative façade. And it is by the continual repetition of such experiences that the secondary process can emerge and extend its dominance.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Elizabeth R. Geleerd, M.D. (New York). 'Clinical Contribution to the Problem of the Early Mother-Child Relationship. Some Discussion of its Influence on Self-destructive Tendencies and Fugue States.' Pp. 336–351.

Material is presented from the analysis of three patients at the point where early mother-child relationship was uppermost. In all cases sibling and oedipal rivalry was manifest in a strongly oral fashion and libidinal and aggressive longings gave rise to the wish to devour. Additionally, there was a partial ego regression to a point where the distinction between self and object was lost. The wish to sleep in one patient and to obtain oblivion through drink and drugs in another occurred in such a manner as to confirm Lewin's thesis (1950) that suicidal fantasies and sleep are closely linked to early infantile fantasies of falling asleep at the breast. In a third patient the violence of her anger made it immaterial whether she killed herself or the analyst. In this patient profuse daydreaming in childhood manifested an identification with her withdrawn mother and could be understood as a partial regression to the earliest phase of mother-child relationship.

The author refers to Freud's theories of suicide as set out in Mourning and Melancholia, and suggests her material may indicate that the presence of suicidal wishes is more closely allied to primary narcissistic states regressively reanimated than to secondary narcissism and guilt. The material also casts light on fugue states in which the patient loses his sense of space, time, and personal identity. The fugue may be a partial suicide, a compromise between living and dying. In one patient, periods of non-attentiveness alternating with rages in which she had suicidal fantasies suggest that she was considering the alternatives of the fugue state or suicide.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Louis A. Gottschalk, M.D. (Cincinnati). 'The Relationship of Psychologic State and Epileptic Activity. Psychoanalytic Observations on an Epileptic Child.' Pp. 352–380.

The psycho-analytic treatment of a 10-year-old epileptic boy is presented and reviewed. Treatment revealed that either a meaningufl external situation or a self-initiated series of thoughts aroused in the patient memories of recent and past experiences having to do with dependent, sexual, and aggressive interchanges between key people of the past or recent present, with commensurate affect. The psychological compromise between these conflicts was experienced as a compulsion to go to a window screen and look through. If this was not interrupted, a 'screen' spell (epileptic) invariably started, which, following some psychotherapy, could be modified by verbal communication with the patient. Presumably, memories and thoughts (cortical activity) and the emotional components (subcortical activity) of these ideas modulated 3 c.p.s. spike and wave activity that in itself was not sufficient to cause a seizure. Under other (unknown) circumstances a seizure with highly integrated motor activity could ensue, associated with an altered state of consciousness from which the subject could sometimes be aroused. Sometimes, for no known reason, the relatively localized epileptic cerebral discharges would become diffuse, manifesting themselves as a grand mal seizure. A necessary condition for this sequence of events was a specific anatomical structure and neuro-chemical basis without which the behaviour associated with the psychological state would probably have been otherwise.

The therapeutic problems encountered in this patient illustrate the therapeutic value of psycho-analytic psychotherapy in the management of certain types of epileptics and the value of such studies to our understanding of the interplay of the neural and experiential bases of emotional disorder.

1Published by International Universities Press, New York, and Imago Publishing Co., London.77

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

William G. Niederland, M.D. (New York). 'Clinical Observations on the "Little Man" Phenomenon.' Pp. 381–395.

The author describes and reviews case material from two analyses in further elucidation of the 'Little Man' phenomenon representing an isolated segment of the ego (Kramer, 1955). The phenomenon seems to follow and compensate for the loss of infantile omnipotence by the erection of a magical separate structure in the ego which still remains ego-syntonic. In one case 'the dwarf', an angry creature without bone or muscle, ugly, deformed, dirty, and big-headed, represented the patient functioning as a shady and successful business man identified with Alberich of the Niebelungenlied. Living within the patient's anus, the dwarf stood for his triumph over the threat of castration and a safeguard against it. The patient had suffered severe nutritional disturbances and, following rickets, had defective bone structure in the chest and head. As a child his head seemed overlarge. He identified with animals he had seen slaughtered in an abattoir and had observed excreting in the death throes. The retention of the dwarf in his anus represented an assurance against death.

A second patient, who experienced a thoracic deformity as 'a hole in the middle of his body', defended himself with the help of an amoeba fantasy which allowed him to withdraw his pseudopods at will, recreate them again, and to encapsulate himself so as practically to disappear. Isolated character qualities corresponded to this fantasy. This patient also suffered from infantile diarrhoea in which his body seemed to melt away. The amoeba fantasy, a variant and perhaps a forerunner of the 'little man', defended against the dreaded sensation of bodily dissolution. In both patients reality testing was intact except at such time as the 'dwarf' and 'amoeba' segments took over. In contrast to the Schreber case they functioned therefore in a non-psychotic way. Essentially traumatic in origin, they formed part of severe character disorders and can be viewed as an attempt to perpetuate infantile omnipotence and as a special solution of the castration threat.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Eleanor Pavenstedt, M.D. (Boston). 'The Effect of Extreme Passivity Imposed on a Boy in Early Childhood.' Pp. 396–409.

Over the eight years in which he was observed, this child was subjected to influences from a highly dominating and punishing mother and a somewhat supine father. Traumas included severely restrictive bodily interference from birth onwards. During his childhood the mother repeatedly miscarried, and finally produced the more-desired girl child. Symptoms included sporadic identification with inanimate things, rotary movements, retarded speech and learning, and severe overt anxiety. Evidence suggests that hyperactivity and rebelliousness followed a normal start in life. The determining factor in his final reduction to passivity seems to have been a severe dysentery at 21 months when bodily interference and restriction was intensified for a period of six months. This episode seems to have been decisive for his final psychotic disorientation because its timing affected a highly erotized body zone, in a particularly stormy instinctual period; before the development of speech, when body integrity and body image are especially vulnerable; and when aware that he can assert himself, the child was not yet able to exercise self-control. His fear of drifting off into limitless space was the expression of his slender object relationships, threatened by primitive sadomasochistic fantasies; his fear of isolation drove him back into erotized subjugation to his mother. The picture was later complicated by a bisexual struggle in which he lacked adequate objects for identification.

1Published by International Universities Press, New York, and Imago Publishing Co., London.77

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Joyce Robertson. (London). With Comments by Anna Freud, LL.D. (London). 'A Mother's Observations on the Tonsillectomy of her Four-year-old Daughter.' Pp. 410–435.

The Observations are in the form of a diary which the mother kept from the time operation was decided on and which covers preparatory and post-operative events as well as the period of hospitalization which the mother shared with the child.

In this very normal little girl anticipation aroused formidable anxieties deriving from separation and castration problems, the latter chiefly concerned with the threat of anaesthesia. Fears of dying and bleeding to death, clearly related to fantasies of pregnancy as well as superego anxieties in which the hospital took on the aspect of a prison and the operation of a major punishment, also appeared. Phobic defences alternated with turning passive into active, regressing increasingly towards primitive defensive manoeuvres, temporary psychosomatic symptoms, temper tantrums and attempts at denial of internal and external reality.

The period of hospitalization was marked by defences against passive anxieties, again connected chiefly with anaesthesia. These continued to influence her sleeping for a short time after the return home. The main post-operational problem concerned her ambivalence to her mother.

Anna Freud in her commentary stresses the importance of the quantitive factors in the normal child's precarious emotional balance. Anxieties are mastered by the ego if they remain below a certain threshold. Mastery is seriously jeopardized when threats from reality join cause with threats arising from internal sources. 'What we call "courage" in ordinary language is no more than the individual's ability to deal with external threats on their own ground and prevent the bulk of them from joining forces with the manifold forces lurking in the id.' This consideration may serve as an operative one in distinguishing the role of the mother described in some detail in the diary from that of the therapist. The author gave only such help to her child as enabled her to meet the operation on the level of reality and thus supported her in dissociating it from her inner fantasy life.

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Martha Wolfenstein, Ph.D. (New York). 'Analysis of a Junvenile Poem.' Pp. 450–470.

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas  

The author considers the poem of Lady Jane Grey written when Housman was 15 years old. She shows the contribution of various life experiences to the adolescent fantasies out of which this poem emerged, and which were to find repeated expression in Housman's later poetry. Common fantasies like that of the sadistic primal scene, the mutual involvement of love and death and love and birth and dying to save, assumed peculiar intensity in Housman because, amongst other circumstances, the connexion between birth and death was confirmed for him by his mother having died on his twelfth birthday. Other fantasies show a more idiosyncratic quality, as when father and mother are imagined as putting the brother to death before they make love. While this may be seen as a variant of 'A Child is Being Beaten', the more fatal violence and the connexion between the brother's fate and the love act seems distinctive. Another exceptional fantasy involves the delegation of the role of oedipal hero to a brother. A major factor in the formation of these original fantasies would seem to have been Housman's intense involvement with brothers who replaced him before and during the oedipal period. This protracted struggle with feeling appears in later poetry when he wishes to comfort the 'luckless lads', who likewise lost the mother whose possession he so envied them.

[[RunningHead]]

2nd Latin-American Psychoanalytic Congress

This Congress was held at São Paulo from 25 to 31 August, 1958, under the auspices of the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society (São Paulo), and it was attended by 315 members, fifty-nine of whom were members of the I.P.A. The following guest speakers addressed the Congress: Dr. William H. Gillespie (London): 'Psycho-Analytic Concepts of Instinct and Ego Development as illustrated by Problems of Sexual Perversions; Dr. Paula Heimann (London): 'Early Childhood Development'; Dr. Melitta Sperling (New York): 'Psycho-Analytic Treatment of a Case of Ileitis.' There were four Symposia: (1) Methods of Validation in Psycho-Analytic Investigation, with Dr. Willy Baranger and Dr. Ignacio Matte Blanco as main speakers; (2) Early Childhood Development, with Dr. Walderedo Ismael de Oliveira and Dr. Arnaldo Raskowski as main speakers; (3) Development of Psycho-Analytic Technique, with Dr. Enrique Racker and Dr. Decio S. de Souza as main speakers; (4) Psycho-Analytic Training, with Dr. Werner Kemper, Dr. Adelheid Koch, Dr. Arminda Pichon Riviere, and Dr. Sacha Nacht as main speakers.

79

Charles Frederick Menninger Award

It was announced at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association that the above award has been given to Dr. Heinz Hartmann 'in connexion with the publication of an English translation of his classical essay, "Ego Psychology and the Problem of Adaptation", and in recognition of his research on problems of ego psychology which stemmed from that original work.' (Published by International Universities Press, New York, as Monograph Series Number One of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, translated by David Rapaport.)

responseSet.title: 21st International Psycho-Analytical Congress
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

21st International Psycho-Analytical Congress

The 21st Insernational Psycho-Analytical Congress will be held in Copenhagen at the Congress Hall, Falkonercentret, 9 Falkonerallé from Sunday, 26 July, to Thursday, 30 July, inclusive. Communications referring to the Scientific Programme should be addressed to Dr. Paula Heimann, Hon. Secretary, Programme Committee, 32 Eamont Court, Eamont Street, London, N.W.8. All enquiries about registration and accommodation for this Congress should be sent to Thorkil Vanggaard, M.D., President, Danish Psycho-Analytical Society, H. C. Andersens Boulevard 51, Copenhagen, Denmark.

Provisional Scientific Programme

Monday, 27 July

9.15 a.m.: Opening by the President, I.P.A.A. 10.00 a.m.–12.20 p.m.: Plenary Session. Four papers. 2.30 p.m.–5.30 p.m.: Simultaneous Session with four individual papers (five sections).

Tuesday, 28 July

9.15 a.m.–12.20 p.m.: Plenary Session. Free Discussion of 'Metapsychology of Pleasure', by Raymond de Saussure (published in this issue and in Revue Française de Psychanalyse, Tome XXII, No. 6, 1958, pp. 649–673).

2.30 p.m.–5.20 p.m.: Simultaneous Symposia: (1) Psycho-Analysis and the Disturbances of the Digestive Tract (Main Speakers: Franz Alexander, Angel Garma, Melitta Sperling); (2) Psycho-Analysis and Ethology (main speakers: John Bowlby, I. Charles Kaufman, Charles W. Tidd); (3) Entwicklungen im therapeutischem Anwendungsgebiet der Psychoanalyse (main speakers: Anna Freud and Barbara Lantos, K. R. Eissler, Wilhelm Solms-Rödelheim); (4) Psychotic Objectrelationship (main speakers: D. C. de Sousa, Serge Lebovici, Margaret S. Mahler).

Wednesday, 29 July

9.15 a.m.: Business Meeting I.P.A.A. (for members and associate members only).

Thursday, 30 July

9.15 a.m.–10.30 a.m.: Simultaneous Sessions with two individual papers (five sections).

11.00 a.m.–12.20 p.m.: Plenary Session. Symposium on Depressive Illness (introduced by Elizabeth R. Zetzel, Chairman; main speakers: S. Nacht and P. C. Racamier, Clifford M. Scott.

2.30 p.m.–3.45 p.m.: Symposium continued. Discussion from the floor.

responseSet.title: Technical 'At Homes'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Technical 'At Homes'

An important aspect of the Congresses of the International Psycho-Analytical Association has always been that they provide an opportunity to meet colleagues from other societies working in different parts of the world, and to have informal discussions with them about different problems arising from their work as psycho-analysts. The Sub-Committee on Congress Design has suggested, and the Programme Committee would like to implement this suggestion, that on the Monday and Wednesday evenings of the Congress, after dinner, an attempt should be made to arrange, as an experiment, what the Sub-Committee has called Technical 'At Homes'. Details will be announced in the Circular to those who have registered for the Congress and during the Congress in Copenhagen.

152

responseSet.title: Clinical Essay Prize
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Essay Prize

Lothar Rubinstein

Lothar Rubinstein

Lothar H. Rubinstein, Honorary Scientific Secretary
Institute of Psycho-Analysis
63 New Cavendish Street
London, W.
 1.

Lothar Rubinstein  

Members and Associate members of the International Psycho-Analytical Association are reminded that competitors for the Clinical Essay Prize must send in their work to the Hon. Scientific Secretary of the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 63 New Cavendish Street, London, W.1., by 31 May of the year in which they wish to enter the competition.

The conditions governing the competition are the following:

A prize of (not exceeding) 30 guineas is offered.

Requirements for the Essay

The essay shall consist of a clinical record of a case investigated by psycho-analytical methods. It should illustrate clearly the events and changes in the mental life of the patient and their relation to external environment. In awarding the prize, the Judges will pay attention to acuity of observation and the clarity with which the facts are stated. If the writer wishes to draw theoretical conclusions, he must bear in mind the necessity of making the evidence for such conclusions carry conviction.

It is recommended that the length of the essay should not exceed 20, 000 words.

The Essay shall not have been published in any book, journal or other form of publication and shall not have been read to or formed the subject of discussion at any formally constituted meeting of psycho-analysis.

Date of Sending in Essays: Language: Format, etc.

Essays must be submitted on or before 31 May in any year, in the English language. They must be typescript on quarto paper with ample left-hand margin. They must be in triplicate and be sent to the Hon. Scientific Secretary of the Institute. All copies of essays submitted become ipso facto the property of the Institute (or its successor) while it has the appointment of the Trustees for the Prize Fund.

No Award

If no essay of merit worthy of a prize is submitted in any year, no award shall be made for that year.

Joint Award

In the event of the Judges regarding the essays of two or more competitors as of equal merit, they may divide the prize money into equal parts and award it to such competitors jointly.

responseSet.title: Psychiatric Research Reports of the American Psychiatric Association, No. 8. Research in Affects: (Washington, 1957.  Pp. 186.  $2.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychiatric Research Reports of the American Psychiatric Association, No. 8. Research in Affects

(Washington, 1957. Pp. 186. $2.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

Parts of this symposium are of considerable interest to psycho-analysts. It starts with the description of rumination, i.e. regurgitation in infants, which is described as a psychosomatic syndrome. Careful observations of four infants suggested that a disordered relationship between parents and baby played an important part in the etiology of the syndrome. The infants which displayed the syndrome are thought to have the capacity to compensate for frustration with substitute visceral gratification. Another chapter is concerned with an infant who was born with an atresia of the oesophagus. She was fed through a gastric fistula. The mouth was in this case not used for the intake of food. This rare experiment of nature is being studied by a team of psycho-analytical trained workers. They found the mouth's rôle as source of gratification greatly reduced. This had a marked effect on autoerotic behaviour in general. Another study is concerned with the problem of unconscious versus conscious affects within and without the psycho-analytic situation. Their relationship to defence mechanisms and methodological difficulties of the study of affects with the help of tests is discussed. The rest of the volume deals with experimental studies with neurophysiological methods.

349

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Patient and the Mental Hospital. Contributions of Research to the Science of Social Behaviour

Edited by Milton Greenblatt, Daniel J. Levinson and Richard H. Williams. (Glencoe, Illinois: Free Press, 1957. Pp. 658. $6.00.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This volume is a carefully edited record of a work conference which took place in 1954 with the support

349

of the National Institute of National Health financed by the American Government. The participants, some of whom came from this country, discussed all aspects of life in the mental hospital. The Chestnut Lodge studies by Stanton and Schwartz have by some psychiatrists been regarded as of limited application only, in view of the unusual character of their case material and of the hospital in which it was studied. No such qualifications apply to this book, which deserves to be read carefully by doctors, social workers, and senior nurses working in mental hospitals. It is certain to give them a fuller understanding of their work and to make them better at it.

350

responseSet.title: Psychiatry and the Criminal: By John M. Macdonald.  (Springfield, Illinois:  Charles C. Thomas, 1958.  Oxford:  Blackwell Scientific Publications, 1958.  Pp. xvi + 228.  42s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychiatry and the Criminal

By John M. Macdonald. (Springfield, Illinois: Charles C. Thomas, 1958. Oxford: Blackwell Scientific Publications, 1958. Pp. xvi + 228. 42s.)

Hyatt Williams

Hyatt Williams

Hyatt Williams  

Dr. Macdonald has written a book which well merits both the first and second titles he has chosen. The second title, 'A Guide to Psychiatric Examinations for the Criminal Courts, ' describes most accurately what the book is about. The style is lucid and attractive, just sufficiently anecdotal to stimulate interest without causing any break in the sequence of ideas and themes.

Chapter 1, 'The Origin of Criminal Behaviour, ' is the most interesting part of the book from a psycho-analytical viewpoint. In this section, the author manages in a very small space to condense a great deal of information about sociological and biological factors in the aetiology of criminal behaviour, before going on to consider the psycho-logical and psychodynamic factors in more detail. One of the merits of the book is that the author culls information from many fields of endeavour, and he has so integrated the material that there is no incongruity. Everything has been well metabolized. Due regard is paid to the contribution which has been made through psycho-analysis to the understanding of crime and the criminal. In particular reference is made to Freud's paper 'Some Character Types met with in Psycho-analytic Practice—Criminals from a Sense of Guilt'. It was of this particular paper of Freud's that Jones wrote that most of the modern work on the psychology of criminals and delinquents stems therefrom. Reference is made also to the work of Aichhorn, Alexander, and other workers in the field of the psychopathology of delinquency. Bowlby's work on the psychopathology of delinquents in their family setting is also mentioned.

The section dealing with the interviewing of the defendant is essentially practical and reveals the great experience of the author in Court work. It is primarily a factual and sensible guide to others who wish to or must work in this field.

The rest of the book is more psychiatrically orientated, and the various syndromes are dealt with systematically, from that associated with the name of Ganser to the clinical mixture known collectively as 'Psychopathic Personality'. Here again the author introduces the reader quite painlessly to a number of interesting and relevant facts.

responseSet.title: Group Processes: By Bertram Schaffner (ed.)  (New York:  Josiah Macy, Jr. Foundation Publications, 1957.  Pp. 328.  $4.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Group Processes

By Bertram Schaffner (ed.) (New York: Josiah Macy, Jr. Foundation Publications, 1957. Pp. 328. $4.00.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This is a record of the third conference on group processes. It deals with the following four subjects: interpersonal influences within the family, interpersonal persuasion, maternal-neonate interrelationships, and Chinese Communist thought reform. The

350

points of view from which these problems were approached were chiefly those of sociology and social psychiatry, some of which were informed by psycho-analysis. Rôle theory played a considerable part in the discussion of the first two topics. Interpersonal persuasion was studied in the mental hospital. In the discussion of the third subject animal observations were introduced. The last topic takes up one-third of the book. It is based on interviews with ex-prisoners who underwent systematic brain-washing in Chinese prison camps. The chief contributor to the discussion, the Harvard psychiatrist Lifton, prefers the term 'thought reform' to 'brain washing' which is of Chinese origin. The main psychological events which lead up to the prisoner's complete capitulation are annihilation of identity, establishment of guilt, and total conflict with the environment. Strong influences from all parts of the human environment, including the fellow-prisoners, contributed to the final surrender.

351

responseSet.title: A Short History of Psychotherapy: By Nigel Walker.  (London:  Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1957.  Pp. 185.  25s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Short History of Psychotherapy

By Nigel Walker. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1957. Pp. 185. 25s.)

H. Hardenberg

H. Hardenberg

H. Hardenberg  

This is a most stimulating and refreshing book. 'A short account of the development and theory of psychotherapy' would be a more accurate title.

The author's approach is that of a naturalist who arranges and labels his specimens on the bench and then critically and sympathetically looks at them and wonders how they work. Inevitably this produces a rather flattened look in the specimens. For example, in describing psycho-analysis, major and fundamental features, such as the distinction between psychic and external reality or the value of dream interpretation, are stressed no more than any other of its features. Nevertheless it is sometimes useful to see how acute differences between psychotherapeutic systems look to an external observer, and we see the Freudian, Post-Freudian, Jungian, and Adlerian approaches displayed before us. Not content with this, there is a glance at group psycho-therapy, child psychotherapy, and psycho-somatic medicine.

The advantage of this book is that the author has some new ways of examining his material. He is clearly trying to understand as well as describe, and to see whether some new words—such as techniques, sub-techniques, facilitants, etc.—will help.

What is needed, and perhaps will be supplied in the next edition, is an examination of objectives, in this case health and ill-health. The history of the changing concepts of being well and of being sick, of the changing rôles of doctor and patient, of analyst and analysand, together with the changing expectations from therapy during the past fifty years, needs still to be written. And to give body to this history something must be written of the changing methods and requirements of the training of psycho-therapists.

The author rightly dislikes psycho-therapy sheltering under the name of art or science. It is, he says, a technique—or as Trotter would say, a practical art—and, as such, may be examined without undue mystery or awe. Nevertheless techniques gradually lead to a respectable body of knowledge and theory which can be brought into the general body of the biological sciences.

P. Lomas

P. Lomas  

The importance of the study of the family as a whole is becoming increasingly recognized, and this book is an expression of it. The basis of the conference reported here is a study of the contemporary family especially in its relation to health education. The various contributors include psychiatrists, sociologists, and anthropologists, as well as health educators. Throughout the book emphasis is laid on the fact that it is profitable to study the gestalt of the family, and to think in terms of sick or healthy families as well as sick or healthy individuals.

Particularly valuable is the chapter by Dr. Nathan W. Ackerman, who describes the individual's loss of personal identity—which is the mark of our time—and its result. 'The family is called upon to make up to its individual members in affection and closeness for the anxiety and distress which is the result of failure to find a safe place in the world.' The family in its turn, however, is lost and insecure, owing to rapid social change, and cannot meet this challenge. The various family defences which result, e.g. static patterns of conformity, or ceaseless social activity, and the consequences to the individual members, are described with great clarity and insight.

Despite some excellent chapters this book is not a successful whole. Perhaps this is the fate of most symposia. One notable defect is that several of the contributors have succumbed to the prevailing American fashion of a clipped and bloodless style which manages somehow to be long-winded yet condensed to the point of ambiguity.

352

responseSet.title: Crestwood Heights: A Study of the Culture of Suburban Life.  By John R. Seeley, R. Alexander Sim, and Elizabeth W. Loosley.  (New York:  Basic Books, 1956.  Pp. xv + 505.  $6.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Crestwood Heights

A Study of the Culture of Suburban Life. By John R. Seeley, R. Alexander Sim, and Elizabeth W. Loosley. (New York: Basic Books, 1956. Pp. xv + 505. $6.50.)

A. Shapiro

A. Shapiro

A. Shapiro  

This book is a sociological study of a professional, middle-class suburb of a city in Canada. The name Crestwood Heights is a thin disguise for the suburb of a provincial capital. The book differs from other sociological studies of communities belonging to the Western civilization by being part of a project launched by the National Council for Mental Hygiene (Canada). The project owes its origin to a determined attempt to study the causation of high post-war psychiatric morbidity and to initiate a research programme of preventive treatment. It thus has a very strong therapeutic orientation. A community with a high level of education and materially well-off was chosen as a subject for the study because it was considered that adverse environmental conditions would be a variable best eliminated from the study, and also because such a community was of national importance as it was one from which would come a disproportionate number of leaders of opinion and others responsible for the fate of intelligence in the next generation. Crestwood Heights had also built up a very progressive and efficient educational department which was interested in Mental Health problems. The original project was primarily directed at the study of children in their educational setting, but it was soon realized that the findings would only be significant if concerned with the sociological aspect of the community life in which the whole of the project took place.

The major part of the volume presents the material of the survey and is followed by a further part in which that material is analysed and assessed.

353

responseSet.documentRef: Lomas, P. (1959) The Person in Psychology: Reality or Abstraction: By Paul Lafitte.  (London:  Routledge, 1957.  Pp. x + 233.  25 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  40:355
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Lomas, P. (1959) The Person in Psychology: Reality or Abstraction: By Paul Lafitte. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. x + 233. 25 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 40:355
responseSet.documentRefXML: Lomas, P. (1959) The Person in Psychology: Reality or Abstraction: By Paul Lafitte. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. x + 233. 25 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 40:355
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195940355AThe Person in Psychology: Reality or AbstractionBy Paul Lafitte. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. x + 233. 25 s.)PeterLomas
responseSet.title: The Person in Psychology: Reality or Abstraction: By Paul Lafitte.  (London:  Routledge, 1957.  Pp. x + 233.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Person in Psychology: Reality or Abstraction

By Paul Lafitte. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. x + 233. 25 s.)

Peter Lomas

Peter Lomas

Peter Lomas  

This book is even wider in scope than its title suggests, being an investigation of the question: 'What is the proper study of psychology?' Dr. Lafitte, who is a lecturer in psychology, comes out emphatically with the answer: 'The Person in his real, ordinary life situation.' 'The choice for psychology, then, ' he writes, 'is between working with easily verified but largely trivial or irrelevant propositions and working with complex propositions in the field of common experience, which are fully as empirical as any but much harder to verify.' This is a point of view which a psycho-analyst would readily endorse, but it is a delight to find it expounded with the erudition and force of reasoned argument of which Dr. Lafitte is capable. He emphasizes the uniqueness of the person, who is the cause of his own destiny and who can only be studied during action in real life by an observer who is himself to some degree involved. Much of Dr. Lafitte's basic approach shows the influence of existentialist thought.

The latter part of the book contains a discussion of the nature of psychological laws. He criticizes the fashionable mathematical analysis of fragmentary behaviour, and also the unjustified inferences that are often made from this to real behaviour.

Dr. Lafitte has an excellent hand of cards but tends at times to overplay it. for instance, although he is undeniably right in pointing out the danger of assuming that the psychology of the person can be explained in terms applicable to the physical sciences (psycho-analysis is criticized for this), he seems to underestimate the immense heuristic value which analogies from other fields of science can often have.

This book is an important one. It is an enlightened and powerful argument against the arid and artificial type of enquiry that constitutes so much of present-day psychology, and it contains constructive thought on the paths which might more profitably be open to the psychologist in the future.

355

responseSet.title: The Idiom of Contemporary Thought: By Crawford Knox.  (London:  Chapman and Hall, 1956.  Pp. x + 206. 18 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Idiom of Contemporary Thought

By Crawford Knox. (London: Chapman and Hall, 1956. Pp. x + 206. 18 s.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

Consisting of two parts which the author calls essays, this book deals with a very fundamental problem, namely, the place of man in his biosociological and cosmological context. The answers to the questions raised during the attempts of the author to develop his theme reveal the need for a synoptic integration of the various scientific disciplines, so that something can be gleaned from the somewhat compartmentalized studies which prevail today.

In the first essay the author reviews the problem from the viewpoint of the physical sciences, after which he ventures beyond the boundaries of formal science into the territory of mystics, the thought processes of primitive man, and the study of aesthetics. His search for the ultimate Being of all things, which indeed he regards as being God, leads him to postulate something outside spatiotemporal relationships.

355

responseSet.title: Fear: Contagion and Conquest: By James Clark Moloney, M.D.  (New York:  Philosophical Library, 1957.  Pp. 140. $3.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fear: Contagion and Conquest

By James Clark Moloney, M.D. (New York: Philosophical Library, 1957. Pp. 140. $3.75.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

The main thesis which Dr. Moloney puts forward with his characteristic vigour is that tension in mothers is communicated to their young children by means of gesture, expression, muscle-tone, and anxiety expressed in a whole multitude of physical contacts. That these methods of communicating tension states apply to neurotic mothers and their children is well known, but the author states emphatically that non-neurotic mothers who are themselves realistically anxious owing to external stress situations, communicate tension and anxiety to their young children in whom the communicated tension state becomes fixed and chronic so that henceforth there is a more flimsy adjustment and a higher breakdown rate. The author cites, as his main example, the Okinawans, who in their island homeland enjoy a highly satisfactory early childhood with pleasant nurture experiences, and who later have not only tolerant social structures in which the individual is allowed to bloom, but also a very low mental breakdown rate. The migrant Okinawans of Hawaii, however, who live cheek by jowl with the Japanese who despise them, despite apparently good early nurture conditions have a much higher than normal breakdown rate for Hawaii, which itself has a higher breakdown rate than Okinawa. Repatriation of Okinawans to their homeland has been fraught with so many complications, and has taken place so recently, that any restoration of favourable prognosis in mental and emotional health has not yet been validated. However, Dr. Moloney cites as an example of the resurgence of a healthy society the second generation Israelis, whose sturdy manliness, energy, and independence he ascribes to the joyful attitude of immigrant parents who had found a home, a soil, a feeling of belonging, and a sense of freedom. One feels about this part of the book that the enthusiasm of the author for proving his main thesis has led him into viewing the Israeli youth somewhat through rose-tinted spectacles. Qualitatively I think his conclusions are convincing, even if quantitatively he has somewhat overstated the case.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Disorder and Crime

By W. Lindesay Neustatter, M.D., B.Sc., M.R.C.P. With a Foreword by John Maude, Q.C. (New York: Philosophical Library. Pp. 248. $6.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

This is a book intended for non-medical readers who are involved or interested in how the psychiatric aspects of crime impinge upon the legal It is also of interest to doctors who wish to learn something about the problem from a non-specialist viewpoint. The style is lucid and there is a great deal of practical commonsense scattered throughout the book. The author has a kindly approach to his subject, but, though basically humanitarian, he frequently oscillates between sympathy for the criminal and sympathy for the victim. In places certain trite and platitudinous statements, usually the last words upon a subject, seem irritating and give the impression that the author was afraid either to take himself and his views entirely seriously, or—what is more probable—to confess in public that he does so.

Repeatedly one gets the impression that a sensitive and accurate observer fails to follow up the implications of his observations because due weight has not been given to the vital role of psycho-analysis in the elucidation of the psychopathology of the criminal.

356

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hospital Treatment of Alcoholism

By Robert S. Wallerstein and Associates. Introduction by Karl Menninger. (New York: Basic Books, 1957. Pp. 212. $5.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

Psycho-analytic treatment of chronic alcoholism having proved disappointing, the authors devised a comparative experimental study conducted at the Veterans Administration Hospital at Topeka, which is linked with the Menninger Foundation. Four treatment methods were employed and their results compared; they were antabuse therapy, deconditioning treatment with an emetic, group hypnotherapy, and closed ward milieu therapy. Antabuse therapy proved the most effective, deconditioning treatment the least useful. The authors attempt an interpretation of this finding on psychodynamic lines. They regard the externalization of control through treatment, which distinguishes antabuse therapy from the other treatments, as an important therapeutic factor.

358

[[RunningHead]]

Medicine in a Changing Society

The New York Academy of Medicine Lectures to the Laity. No. XVIII. Edited by Iago Galdston. (New York: International Universities Press, 1956. Pp. 166. $3.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This is another series of lectures for the sophisticated layman. They admirably convey the profound changes which medicine has undergone during the last few decades as the result of revolutionary developments in natural and social science. Franz Alexander writes in a philosophical vein about the biological significance of the antithesis of adventure and security which pervades our society. John Rose writes about changes in family life and their influence on mental health. W. Malamud discusses the effects of brain changes on the personality. Horsley Gantt evaluates Pavlov's contribution to the study of behaviour. Lastly, Iago Galdston, in a fascinating historical essay entitled 'Salerno and the Atom', traces the development of comprehensive medicine back to the famous school of Salerno.

358

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Experimental Psychology and Other Essays

By I. Pavlov. (New York: Philosophical Library, 1957. Pp. 653. $7.50.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This selection of Pavlov's writings gives a good insight into the thinking of the great physiologist whose work has become something like a religion in the Communist world. This should not detract from its immense importance for the study of behaviour. Pavlov was unable to view behaviour in other than physiological terms. He was violently opposed to psychology adopting a language and methods of its own. This to him was dualism and idealism. Sherrington, who doubted whether mental phenomena can be translated into physiology, was pilloried as an animist!

358

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.

Martin H. Stein. 'The Cliché: A Phenomenon of Resistance.'

This paper deals with the cliché in the ordinary sense of the term. It also discusses the place of jargon as it appears in the service of resistance. A phrase can be recognized as an expression of resistance when it becomes stereotyped and loses the meaning it once had. A number of examples of this phenomenon are given. The author points out that some interpretations belong to the same category. The cliché acts as a barrier to communication and is thus a resistance in psycho-analytic therapy.

362

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3: Bertram D. Lewin.  'Education or the Quest for Omniscience.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3

Bertram D. Lewin. 'Education or the Quest for Omniscience.'

This paper was first delivered before the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis as the first Alexander Lecture in November, 1957. Lewin draws a parallel between the beginnings of psycho-analysis and that of the universities. History shows that learning has always had as one of its main sources the desire for omniscience within either the teacher, the pupil, or both. Threats to this omniscient phantasy have generally been met with violent reactions. A parallel with psycho-analysis is offered by the Renaissance movement of humanism. The humanists of that period were unorganized, but like the first psycho-analysts they introduced a type of knowledge unwelcome to the established order of learning. A further parallel can be made between psycho-analysis and the development of the experimental method. The universities, the seat of established knowledge, did not attract scientists even when they were not unwilling to utilize their services.

362

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3: Henry M. Fox.  'Effect of Psychophysiological Research on the Transference.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3

Henry M. Fox. 'Effect of Psychophysiological Research on the Transference.'

This is a report of an investigation whose aim was to correlate psychological and physiological data observed during psycho-analytical treatment. The patient chosen for this study was characterized by the fact that fluctuations in his emotional state tended to evoke quite obvious physiological effects. Urine volume, creatinine content, urinary 17-hydroxycorticoid and uropepsin determinations were carried out on daily 24-hour collections of urine. Review of the clinical material suggested that the biochemical findings corresponded roughly to phases of the transference. For example, unconscious transference resistance in the early phase of the analysis corresponded to rapid fluctuations in the level of 17-hydroxycorticoid. The effect of the investigation upon the patient's transference phantasies was given special consideration.

363

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3

I. Arthur Mirsky, Robert E. Miller, and John V. Murphy. 'The Communication of Affect in Rhesus Monkeys: 1. An Experimental Model.'

In this experimental project conditioning techniques were employed to discover the effect upon rhesus monkeys of affective disturbance in one of their own kind. The experiment indicated that the sight of another monkey either in a state of apprehension or in pain gave rise to an affective response which had already been extinguished by previous deconditioning. The authors suggest that the type of behaviour manifested by the experimental animals may be associated with the concept of empathy.

363

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3: Arnold H. Modell.  'The Theoretical Implications of Hallucinatory Experiences in Schizophrenia.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3

Arnold H. Modell. 'The Theoretical Implications of Hallucinatory Experiences in Schizophrenia.'

Ten hospitalized patients provided the clinical material for this investigation. The author's aim was to examine existing hypotheses in the light of the clinical data. After presentation of his clinical material he concludes, firstly, that the hallucinatory experiences recreate certain aspects of infantile relations to the parental objects so that an internalized object is now perceived by the patient as an external object. The patient is identified with both subject and object—he takes both active and passive roles. With respect to the nature of the relationship between the self and the hallucinated object Modell separates those instances where direct sexual gratification is achieved from those other instances where the hallucinatory objects have assumed non-eroticized attitudes. In the former group he noted the prominence of genital stimulation in both male and female patients often leading to genital orgasm. Such phenomena when they occur in psychosis may be more easily understood if it is appreciated that the regression which occurs in the ego does not necessarily correlate in a direct fashion with regression of the libido.

Modell observed that male patients heard predominantly male voices whose content concerned passive homosexual wishes. Female patients heard male and female voices with content which indicated both active and passive sexual wishes towards both parents. The material favoured the interpretation that such sexual content was secondary to the narcissistic regression which occurs in the disease. There was no confirmation of the usual theory that the relationship of the voices to the patient was a hostile one. Most of the patients perceived the relationship with the voices as beneficial. This could be thought of as the outcome of an ego regression to a stage when the child relies on the parent to perform certain functions that he later acquires himself. From a metapsychological standpoint an ego identification is reconverted into an object relationship.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3: Eugene B. Brody.  'Superego, Introjected Mother, and Energy Discharge in Schizophrenia:  Contribution from the Study of Anterior Lobotomy.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3

Eugene B. Brody. 'Superego, Introjected Mother, and Energy Discharge in Schizophrenia: Contribution from the Study of Anterior Lobotomy.'

This paper presents a number of theoretical propositions about the interrelations of energy discharge

363

and psychic structure in schizophrenia. The investigation consisted of a study of eleven schizophrenic patients before and after leucotomy. The patients were seen daily for two to three months before the operation and similarly for one to two months after leucotomy. They were also seen three to six weeks at three and twelve months later.

The clinical data indicated that there was an increase in the frequency of aggressive discharge for the leucotomized patients compared with the intact schizophrenic. This suggests that aggression against objects becomes available as a means of adaptation to the environment. A reduced level of ego-defensive activity was reflected in the post-leucotomized patients in an increased freedom of action, diminished obsessive fears, and lessened sexual anxiety. The author suggests that this lessened need for ego defence is due to increasing quantities of neutralized aggressive energy becoming available for ego function. It is also likely that the disruption of the defensive structure by leucotomy allows of an increase in the speed as well as the quantity of energy discharge.

The mother was the focus of both aggressive and libidinal conflicts in the patients prior to leucotomy. As in the case of aggression it was possible to infer a reduction, after leucotomy, in anxiety and guilt concerning incest wishes and in the perception of the mother as engulfing and castrating. The sexual conflicts of the female patients were related rather to problems of maternal identification than to incestuous wishes for the father. In general there was a less inhibited phantasy life and a greater trend towards apparent sexual gratification after leucotomy. Beneficial changes in sleep rhythm were also noted after operation.

From a structural standpoint the introjected mother undergoes a drastic change after leucotomy. The diminution in power of this introject leads to a freer distribution of psychic energy within the mental apparatus. In other words the schizophrenic superego functions as a foreign body within the psychic system. The author postulates that there is a wish in the schizophrenic patient for oral union with the mother. During the disease this wish must be repudiated because of the dangers of destruction and loss of identity. The leucotomized patient can achieve this wish and thus bring about new means of energy discharge. The symbolic union with the previously feared mother is comparable in structural terms to the fusion of ego and superego.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 4: Bonnard, A.  'Pre-Body-Ego Types of (Pathological) Mental Functioning.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 4

Bonnard, A. 'Pre-Body-Ego Types of (Pathological) Mental Functioning.'

This paper is concerned with the perceptual-affective organization of the first months of postnatal life, prior to the development of the ego. Such modes of mental functioning disappear from sight as development proceeds, but they can be detected in those individuals whose mental growth is disordered. Three case histories are used to illustrate the author's theory. She believes that the tongue has a decisive role in early infantile life. It is the first medium of sensory and proprioceptive awareness. In patients of the type described the tongue retains its infantile significance. These patients also display the fusion of self and object which is appropriate to the pre-ego phase. This process leads to confusion with such mental mechanisms as projection, projective identification, and talion fear. Symptoms can be thought of as a defence against fusion with the object and loss of identity. These considerations have important implications for technique—particularly in the sphere of interpretation.

364

No. 4</arttitle><artsub>Keiser, S.  'Disturbances in Abstract Thinking and Body Image Formation.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958. No. 4: Keiser, S.  'Disturbances in Abstract Thinking and Body Image Formation.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958. No. 4

Keiser, S. 'Disturbances in Abstract Thinking and Body Image Formation.'

A failure to accept the reality of certain body parts that can be known only from deduction was the affective basis for disturbance in abstract thinking in a number of patients. These patients denied the existence of the vagina because it could not be seen. In one case this refusal to acknowledge the reality of the vagina exerted a repressive force against the abstract conceptualization required for body image formation. In this patient a direct connexion could be made between his inability to develop a harmoniously integrated body image and the pronounced disturbance of his reasoning processes. The paper concludes with a review of the literature on ego development as it pertains to the present subject.

365

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1: S. H. Posinsky.  'Instincts, Culture, and Science.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1

S. H. Posinsky. 'Instincts, Culture, and Science.'

The Freudian theory that civilization requires a massive repression of the instincts, and leads to neurosis, is found to be a massive overgeneralization, too vague to be meaningful. A sound theory of culture requires instead increasingly refined concepts, capable of verification.

The difference between scientific and social values in our own culture puts psycho-analysis in a difficult position. As a science it must be amoral and asocial; but as a therapy it must accept the same social limitations as does medicine. In this conflict of values it is essential that the two aspects of psycho-analysis should be conceptualized apart, and perhaps in time separated.

365

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1: Thomas S. Szasz.  'Psychoanalysis as Method and as Theory.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1

Thomas S. Szasz. 'Psychoanalysis as Method and as Theory.'

The expansion of the field of study, and the advent of 'dynamic psychiatry', have rendered the term psycho-analysis too broad to be strictly meaningful. By contrast psycho-analysis as a method of observation ('the analytic situation') remains the same. Szasz suggests that the term psycho-analysis should be used only to denote an expanding area of science, or 'theory', while different psychological operations be clearly designated by different expressions.

366

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1

Joseph C. Solomon. 'Fugue in a Four-Year-Old.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

A father angrily rebuked his son for pummelling him, which had formerly been a permitted libidinal pleasure. The son responded with a short fugue in which he fantasied himself about to be eaten by a lion. This fantasy restored him to his family because it counteracted his unbearable anger with his father. The author speculates that repeated experiences of this kind can contribute substantially to the development of clinical anxiety and phobias.

367

Lustman, Ph.D., M.D. (New Haven).  'Psychic Energy and Mechanisms of Defense.'  Pp. 151–165.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Seymour L. Lustman, Ph.D., M.D. (New Haven).  'Psychic Energy and Mechanisms of Defense.'  Pp. 151–165.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Seymour L. Lustman, Ph.D., M.D. (New Haven). 'Psychic Energy and Mechanisms of Defense.' Pp. 151–165.

In an earlier paper (1956) the author presented experimental evidence to support the concepts of an undifferentiated phase of ego-id development with already functioning inborn apparatuses which operate in a conflict-free sphere. Evidence is now adduced concerning auditory perception in week-old babies. The only circumstances in which startle response to a bell stimulus was not elicited were during active nursing. Sucking is not purely reflex but involves discriminatory perception of some sort. Considering that the newborn has no voluntary control over the manifestations of its awareness, the absence of awareness with reaction in the same infant under different circumstances is highly significant. Both sets of observations and experimental data from other sources are linked in the following conclusions, which are discussed in detail.

The primitive but differentiated perception of the neonate is dependent on already existing psychic energy, which is highly mobile and fixed in quantity. This high mobility permits of its total investment where necessary, and this is most likely to occur at either end of the pleasure-pain continuum. The necessity to attend to and cope with excitation from within will take precedence over excitation from without. The total investment of energy in processes from within will preclude the possibility of energy investment in processes from without. It is postulated that this lack of available energy forms an inborn primary defence mechanism which is called the defence of imperceptivity. It is discernible in later life in crises and catastrophic experience. It is further postulated that other ego functions such as language, thought, motor activity, may be so involved by more extensive chronic depletion. With chronicity during developmentally crucial periods, severe ego restriction such as 'environmental retardation' may result.

It is felt that the psychic energy of the neonate does not function in a manner to suggest derivation from libidinal and aggressive drives. Since mechanisms of neutralization are unlikely to be available to the neonate, it is assumed that there may be other sources of neutral energy. It is postulated that there is an undifferentiated stage of instinct development that is functional, and exists as a functional matrix corresponding to the undifferentiated phase of ego-id development. This energy appears to be primitively neutral and available to the ego to operate its primitive functions.

'The Use of Prediction in a Longitudinal Study.'  Pp. 175–189.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Marianne Kris, M.D. (New York).  'The Use of Prediction in a Longitudinal Study.'  Pp. 175–189.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Marianne Kris, M.D. (New York). 'The Use of Prediction in a Longitudinal Study.' Pp. 175–189.

In the past seven years, the Child Study Centre, Yale University School of Medicine has attempted systematic predictions concerning the development of mother-child relationships and of critical areas in child development on the basis of prolonged observation which was initiated with the mother's pregnancy. It is held that prediction helps to expose areas where knowledge is scanty, and where it is adequate and reliable, and enforces re-evaluation of data and of assumptions as to what is relevant. The study also includes progressive analysis of the process of prediction. 'We try to focus on specific problems: What kind of predictions are being suggested by any given material; at what point are these predictions expanded; when do new areas of prediction suggest themselves; when are general predictions replaced by more specific ones; what predictions could have been made in the past had we properly evaluated clues. What can be predicted at any given time, that is the range of prediction, is to us more significant than the correctness or incorrectness of any specific prediction' (Kris 1957).

The present paper is an attempt to evaluate the process of prediction and highlight some of its problems. Predictions as to areas of future conflict have been based for instance on the assumption that the maternal defences would be of paramount influence whereas in fact the operative influence proved to be a break-through of the original impulses defended against. The early recognition of non-pathological aspects of behaviour in both mother and child and the assessment of their influence presented difficulty. 'We have been more accurate in predicting areas of conflict … than we have been in predicting conflict-free functioning and the use of normal defenses.' The positive effects which the baby's maturation would have on the mother's adverse attitudes and the effect of the influences of the team on both mother and child are difficult to forecast. The nature and intensity of a particular child's drives as well as what facets of the equipment maturation will bring to the fore also defy prediction. While the intervention of reality in the shape of environmental happenings cannot be known in advance, it may still be possible to attempt to forecast and assess in advance the impact of such typical influences.
