responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: William G. Niederland, M.D. (New York).  'The Earliest Dreams of a Young Child.'  Pp. 190–198.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

William G. Niederland, M.D. (New York). 'The Earliest Dreams of a Young Child.' Pp. 190–198.

The dreams of a child as reported by his mother are considered against the background of current events in the family life and in the child's day-to-day experience. The first two occurred at 17 and 20 months respectively and were in the nature of nightmares which the child could not verbalize. They could be understood in the light of frightening events of the previous day which at the time the child had appeared to enjoy. A further nine dreams from the age of 2½ to 4 could be understood at their deepest level to refer to actual and feared births and serve as a kind of longitudinal history of childhood dreams focussing landmarks in the child's development, and his mastery of traumatic events culminating in the sexual conflicts of the oedipal period. Comments are offered on the symbolic content with special emphasis on the part played by water both in the dreams and in the child's life experience. There is some evidence that the child was aware of foetal movements in the mother even at 20 months. At one point the child's comment 'The baby is inside you, around you, all around' suggested that the mother was experienced not only as the carrier of the baby but as equated with the baby. Such an impression may have become concretized by the perception of the mother's bodily changes and of foetal movements.

370

'Inconsistency in the Mother as a Factor in Character Development:  A Comparative Study of Three Cases.'  Pp. 209–225.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Anne-Marie Sandler, Elizabeth Daunton, and Annaliese Schnurmann (London). With an Introduction by Anna Freud, LL.D. (London). 'Inconsistency in the Mother as a Factor in Character Development: A Comparative Study of Three Cases.' Pp. 209–225.

Clinical material derived from the analyses of three children of different families who showed marked similarities in behaviour and personal appearance led the three therapists concerned to certain ideas about the role of inconsistency and unpredictability of maternal behaviour and the influence of the child's own anxiety on character development. Each child's anxiety, whether deriving from id or superego, was pathologically reinforced by real fear of the mother, who combined in her behaviour to the child both seductive and aggressive attitudes. The anxiety felt by the child was a combination of neurotic anxiety and real fear based upon the mother's unpredictability. These sources of anxiety constantly interacted, for the mother's inconsistencies and seductions reinforced the instinctual wishes and led to increased anxiety. The mother's aggressive behaviour similarly reinforced the prohibitions of the superego which was in any case faulty because of the mother's inconsistencies.

The situation was further complicated by projection outward of the internalized picture of the mother which carried with it the full weight of the child's own libido and aggression. All children tried to build up stable defences which were constantly undermined for want of a solid reality orientation upon which a stable ego could be built. The smallest provocation led to acute anxiety which it was attempted to deal with by identification with the aggressor. Insufficient defence led to regression under stress, a state of affairs which was reinforced by constant seduction.

The heightened anxiety of these children found discharge in the main through bodily excitement. This seemed to be the only form of pleasure which was consistently permitted and therefore could become conflict-free. Perhaps the most important factor was that in this way the children through the body-phallus equation satisfied their own and the mother's desire for a penis.

371

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12 1957

Erna Furman (Cleveland). 'Treatment of Under Fives by Way of Their Parents.' Pp. 250–262.

The scope of this undertaking exceeds the mere giving of advice to a parent concerning the vicissitudes of a particular developmental period, though it is much more restricted than the analytic treatment of either mother or child. The author illustrates the range of problems to be dealt with by this technique from case studies undertaken at University College Nursery School (Cleveland) which admits children already manifesting developmental disturbance. The criterion for treating the child through the parent is that the problem shall as yet not be completely internalized. Criteria for maternal suitability to undertake this work are difficult to ascertain or to apply in early psychiatric interviews. Amongst the less easy to ascertain in initial interview are those mothers who, while appearing insightful and generally balanced, have problems in areas which overlap those of the child's, or who share the child's defence mechanisms, precluding them from successfully sharing in the work.

371

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Bela Mittelmann, M.D. (New York).  'Motility in the Therapy of Children and Adults.'  Pp. 284–319.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Bela Mittelmann, M.D. (New York). 'Motility in the Therapy of Children and Adults.' Pp. 284–319.

Skeletal motility is a significant means of pleasure-seeking and of reality-testing and integration throughout life. It is one of the dominant functions in those areas, beginning with the second year of life for several years. This period is further characterized by increase in self-assertion along with continuation of dependence on the environment, increase in motor aggression, imitative identification, predominantly motor communication with the environment, readiness to translate impulses into action, and certain affectomotor (expressive) patterns. In addition, skeletal motility is significantly connected with all other physiological and psychological needs and impulses, particularly with self-evaluation and self-determination. Among its characteristic vicissitudes are motor anxiety—for example fear of high places—anxiety and rage in response to restriction, ready inhibition of new skills in response to anxiety and pain and disapproval, and as a result, fear of motor retribution and abandonment, and guilt and self-injury. Conflict solutions are: incomplete (and primitive) (symbolic) action, reaction formation, increase or diminution in motility, and regressive action.

Motility is one of the significant areas of function to be systematically explored in therapy. Its disturbances may arise from traumata directed at the motor function directly or through the expression of other motivational conflicts through the motor area. The utilization of motor data, comprising observable manifestations, motor memories, and dreams contributes to the effectiveness of treatment in both children and adults. While in children as a rule the observable manifestations predominate, in most adults the predominantly verbal communication and interpretation of these data are effective. With some patients, motor behaviour, gestures, symptoms, motor elements in dreams and phantasies, and motor memories need to be interpreted and carefully worked through. In disturbed schizophrenics the interpretative utilization of symbolic action is indispensable.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Margarete Ruben (Los Angeles). 'Delinquency, A Defense Against Loss of Objects and Reality.' Pp. 335–355.

An unusual case of wayward behaviour and stealing in a latency boy adopted from infancy is reported. The earliest episode took place at three years following separation from a beloved maid. The object stolen was the ring of the adoptive mother, who was an anxious, withdrawn and preoccupied person. Later at nursery school the child expressed his unhappiness by stealing the teacher's key ring. Episodes thereafter increased in frequency and importance. The analysis was hampered by constant acting out; the parameters by which the analyst restricted this interplay with the environment are described with the subsequent revelation of the child's need for a symbiotic relationship. Preoccupation with inanimate objects brought to the sessions proved to be repeated attempts at recovery from the early trauma and deprivation by the establishment of 'transitional' object relationships, but were used chiefly as a barrier to relationship with the analyst. The choice of the objects stolen was determined less by their symbolic significance than by the child's expectations that they held omnipotent palliation for his helplessness and fear of abandonment. The significant interpretation was that he had to steal to hold on to objects in the world. He needed to manipulate dangerous weapons in order to differentiate between his hostile omnipotent phantasies and outside dangers. Not trusting his hostile feelings towards loved objects, he turned to inanimate things and played make-believe with them as a means of warding off and simultaneously expressing his ambivalence.

The traumatic event of early object loss and his failure to bridge painful experiences with new and better ones were reinforced by the parents' punishment, and a traumatic tonsillectomy. A seduction by an older sister at five years and observations of parental intercourse through a keyhole completed the damage to his narcissism and led to the sexualization of his castration anxiety. Early indications of his attempt to assume the active role were submerged in the senseless thieving which represented an activity designed to provoke punishment, leading in turn to the unconsciously desired passivity.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Lisbeth J. Sachs, M.D. (New York).  'On Changes in Identification from Machine to Cripple.'  Pp. 356–375.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Lisbeth J. Sachs, M.D. (New York). 'On Changes in Identification from Machine to Cripple.' Pp. 356–375.

The onset of treatment of a 5-year-old boy, following tonsillectomy, was marked by convulsions and a devitalization of his body in catatonic-like state. In the analysis identification with death-dealing guns and machines coincided with 'playing dead' games. Passivity on the part of the analyst precipitated regressive trends while her structuring of the session towards 'playing baby' who was fed and fondled produced an arrest of these symptoms and progressive features. The child first presented her with switches to 'turn off' his schizophrenic activity and later undertook control himself. The identification with the machines was a projection of his aggression and an attempt to control it by immobilizing himself ('playing dead'). Ego functions including integrated speech were given up to maintain this control. Dying was introduced to prevent the acting out of murderous impulses directed against his mother and himself.

At a later stage of the analysis in response to threatened break-up of the family, separation from his mother, and loss of a friendship, the child identified with crippled men and adopted in play the feature of blindness. The machine as a projection of powerful and aggressive bodily structure was replaced by an actual body which experienced separation as damage and destruction, a feature probably related to the experience of separation and tonsillectomy.

At a turning-point in the analysis, the child indulged in an ecstatic and exhausting dance, and said 'For you I would do anything'. Later he wrote, 'I want to live, to live because I love my doctor and she loves me.' The dance represented a revitalization, a primitive overcathexis of the body, which could now be permitted.

The author describes certain parameters in the technique adopted and the account is illustrated with numerous drawings.

373

Charles Frederick Menninger Award

Phyllis Greenacre, M.D., New York City, psychoanalyst and Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at Cornell University School of Medicine, has received the 1959 Charles Frederick Menninger Award of the American Psychoanalytic Association. The Award is given annually by the Association for outstanding contributions to the theory and practice of psycho-analysis.

responseSet.title: Cost of Training Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cost of Training Analysis

Mr. Kenneth Robinson (Labour Member of Parliament) asked the Chancellor of the Exchequer why, when a psychiatrist underwent a training analysis to increase his therapeutic skill, no part of the cost of such analysis was allowed as a deduction from income for income-tax purposes. Mr. D. H. Amory replied: 'If the question relates to psychiatrists in salaried employment assessed under schedule E, the cost of personal analysis is not regarded as satisfying the test laid down by law, which is that expenses are deductible only if incurred wholly, exclusively, and necessarily in the performance of the duties of the employment.—Mr. ROBINSON: Is the Chancellor aware that the purpose of this analysis is solely to make a man a better doctor? Is he aware that many younger psychiatrists are spending as much as £500 a year—perhaps a third of their gross salary—on these courses in the interests of their patients?—Mr. AMORY: I do not disagree with what the hon. Member has said about the importance of this training, but, as he knows, the test in the case of employed persons under schedule E is very strict, and these courses, deserving as they are, do not seem to amount to an essential qualification for employment.

375

responseSet.title: The Index of Psychoanalytic Writings
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Index of Psychoanalytic Writings

Three volumes of the Index of Psychoanalytic Writings have now been published, and the fourth volume is expected to be in the hands of subscribers by the end of November 1958. Volume Five, the subject index volume, will follow shortly thereafter.

We would like to urge that corrections and additions to the Index be sent in as soon as possible so that they can be included in a special section to deal with such additions and corrections.

Please bear in mind that the terminal period of the Index of Psychoanalytic Writings is the year 1952, except for authors who have died since that time. We would like the bibliographies of these authors to be complete, and will therefore include writings of theirs which were published after 1952.

Kindly send additions and corrections either to International Universities Press, 227 West 13th Street, New York 11, New York; or to Alexander Grinstein, M.D., 18466 Wildemere Avenue, Detroit 21, Michigan, U.S.A.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.: Jose Barchilon.  'On Countertransference "Cures".'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.

Jose Barchilon. 'On Countertransference "Cures".'

Counter-transference cures differ from transference cures in that the former apply to a process which takes place wholly within the patient's mind during the process of an unconscious repetition of his childhood phantasy and experiences. In counter-transference cures there occurs in the therapist an unconscious wish of a libidinal nature for the patient's return to health. Simultaneously he obtains some vicarious satisfaction from this. Examples are given and reference is made to the literature on counter-transference.

361

Greenson.  'On Screen Defences, Screen Hunger and Screen Identity.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.: Ralph R. Greenson.  'On Screen Defences, Screen Hunger and Screen Identity.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.

Ralph R. Greenson. 'On Screen Defences, Screen Hunger and Screen Identity.'

A special type of defence—screen defence—is to be found in certain patients who have a disturbance of their sense of identity. The concept of screens was originally limited to memory, but Fenichel and others broadened it to include perceptions and effects. Greenson follows this trend by describing what he terms 'screen identifications'. The screen is a means of defence against mental pain and the 'screen character' makes extensive use of this mechanism. The behaviour of such individuals is characterized by an urgency and imperative quality. Clinical details of such patients are described—for example, defects in memory and self-image are frequently encountered. Periods of gratification at oral and phallic levels as well as in adolescence contribute to this particular syndrome. Screenhungry patients must be differentiated from psychoneurotics by the method they employ to deal with the anxiety arising from instinctual demands. The psychoneurotic utilizes phobic mechanisms while the screen character is counter-phobic.

361

Szasz.  'The Role of the Counter-Phobic Mechanism in Addiction.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2: Thomas S. Szasz.  'The Role of the Counter-Phobic Mechanism in Addiction.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2

Thomas S. Szasz. 'The Role of the Counter-Phobic Mechanism in Addiction.'

The author suggests that addiction represents a re-enactment of a danger situation—for example, fear of pregnancy, of helplessness, of bodily damage in connexion with pregnancy. These dangers are not avoided by means of phobias, but instead there is an attempt to master the anxieties. This is the essence of the counter-phobic attitude. Gratification follows from the knowledge of having successfully overcome the danger and having emerged unharmed. Failure to employ this mechanism as a result of therapeutic intervention or other cause may lead to disintegration of the ego.

362

'Early Physical Determinants in the Development of the Sense of Identity.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 4: Greenacre, P.  'Early Physical Determinants in the Development of the Sense of Identity.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 4

Greenacre, P. 'Early Physical Determinants in the Development of the Sense of Identity.'

The body image is the core of the incipient ego and the later self image. The aim of the paper is to isolate those physical influences which lead to the establishment of the body image. The body areas which are of greatest importance in contrasting and establishing individual recognition of the body self and that of others are the face and the genitals. The incorporation of the body takes place primarily through touch and vision. This build-up of body image takes place in accord with the body surface rather than a real internalized incorporation through the mouth. Early images of the sex organs will be influenced by the extent to which awareness of one's own organs is fused with that of the organs of another individual of the same or of the opposite sex. The sense of one's own body is constantly reinforced by the association with others of predominantly similar appearance.

364

R. 'Abstract Thinking and Object Relations:  With Specific Reference to the Use of Abstraction as a Regressive Defence in Highly Gifted Individuals.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 4: Rosen, H. R. 'Abstract Thinking and Object Relations:  With Specific Reference to the Use of Abstraction as a Regressive Defence in Highly Gifted Individuals.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 4

Rosen, H. R. 'Abstract Thinking and Object Relations: With Specific Reference to the Use of Abstraction as a Regressive Defence in Highly Gifted Individuals.'

This paper deals with some of the phenomena to be found in the analysis of gifted individuals who have the capacity for abstract thinking developed to a high degree. Abstract thought is the most advanced form of the secondary process. The formation of concepts is dependent upon the withdrawal of object libido. This libido is used to cathect images, words, and finally concepts. The main categories of abstract thinking can be ordered in terms of an increasing withdrawal of object cathexis—namely, classification, quantification, and spatial relationships. The author presents a case report which shows how the three elementary categories of abstraction can be used as a defence as well as being employed in the process of adjustment to the environment. Rosen suggests that in the highly gifted individual the intermediate stages of object cathexis which occur in the development of the average person's concepts are not omitted but are dealt with pre-consciously in the manner of the lightning calculator. Such intermediary stages, while close to the primary process, are also available to the secondary process. Such a proximity to the primary process facilitates the mobility of cathexes necessary during the stages of concept formation.

365

responseSet.documentInfoXML: <artinfo arttype="ABS" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ABS" id="IJP.040.0366A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1959</artyear><artvol actual="40">40</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">366</artpgrg><artvar>A</artvar><arttitle> The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1</arttitle><artsub>Samuel Novey.  'The Meaning of the Concept of Mental Representation of Objects.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1: Samuel Novey.  'The Meaning of the Concept of Mental Representation of Objects.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1

Samuel Novey. 'The Meaning of the Concept of Mental Representation of Objects.'

For our purposes in psycho-analysis, the concept of mental representations of objects cannot be defined in terms of a simple sensory perception system. In order to include the useful concepts of unconscious objects and of the superego we must have a more complex but more precise model of the perceptual system and hence also of the ego. It is essential to distinguish clearly between the psychic perceptual system and the physiological sensory apparatus, since the latter does not by itself determine the perceptual experience. Conscious perception can occur only after a physical sensory experience has been worked over in the unconscious; in this respect sensory stimuli from without are equivalent in kind to stimuli arising from within the organism. A conscious perceptual experience is bound by the principle of secondary revision (elaboration) in the waking state as well as in dreams.

To understand the problem of perception, we must suppose that the ego has multiple functions, which must include both simple sensory perception and the internal representation of objects. The cathexis of 'external objects' occurs in the ego and not in the world of time-space. Secondary narcissism is a particular kind of investment of a part of the ego, of that part perceived as the self. The withdrawal of cathexis from 'external objects' is thus not accomplished by withdrawal of cathexis from object to ego (Freud), or from object to id or physical self (Federn), but is an energic shift from one part of the ego to another.

The hypothesis of multiple part-egos infers that they will have various relations to each other in the ego-perceptual system. Federn's 'reality perception' usefully defines the idea of coincidence, or lack of it, between perceptions in time-space and inner perceptual experience. It rightfully differentiates reality testing from the inner convincing sense of reality that one can have without finding any of the criteria for the reality of the time-space world. The inner view of the self is only one part of the ego and coincides with 'external reality' to a limited degree at best. That part of the ego that is internally not consciously perceived or is looked upon as 'not self' may nevertheless coincide with time-space reality. (Author's Summary.)

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1.: Gert Heilbrunn.  'Comments on a Common Form of Acting Out.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1.

Gert Heilbrunn. 'Comments on a Common Form of Acting Out.'

The paper deals with those patients who seize upon a current reality which seems insurmountable to them and make it the exclusive theme of their associations. This hypercathexis of a reality situation draws its intensity from two sources. One is that the situation represents an important, if not the most vital, element of the patient's neurosis—an infantile wish appropriately displaced on to reality. The other is the fear of relinquishing the substitutive or expected gratification of this wish.

366

'The Childhood of the Artist.  Libidinal Phase Development and Giftedness.'  Pp. 41–72.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Phyllis Greenacre, M.D. (New York).  'The Childhood of the Artist.  Libidinal Phase Development and Giftedness.'  Pp. 41–72.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Phyllis Greenacre, M.D. (New York). 'The Childhood of the Artist. Libidinal Phase Development and Giftedness.' Pp. 41–72.

The author emphasizes the tentative nature of the following ideas. The talented person is probably 'endowed' with greater sensitivity to sensory stimulation, an unusual capacity for awareness of relations between various stimuli, an empathy of wider range, and an intactness of sensorimotor equipment to allow the building up of motor discharge for expressive functions. In the area of object relations the gestalt of the talented infant may be widened to include not only the primary object but more peripheral related objects able to arouse similar sensory responses. She adopts the term 'collective alternates' for the range of extended experience which may become attached to the main focus of object relationships. In fortunate circumstances this 'collective alternate' is enriched by identification in the process of growth or projectively identified with some outstanding personality. The impetus for creative expression is a 'love affair with the world', partaking less of the artist's narcissism than has been thought and more of a collective relationship. The artistic product aims at being a perfectible love gift. In the infantile years such collective relationships may have diminished the effect of critical situations in individual relationships.

The oedipal phase is marked more by an extension of ideals than by the development of a prohibitive conscience. The desexualization of the oedipal object is seldom achieved and the temporary resolution of the complex may be due largely to a lessening of biological pressure. The fact that the oedipal object is retained may be evident in the personal sex life with consequent castration anxiety. In the creative life it appears in the 'collective power' to whom the creation is directed and in the identification with this object which palliates castration anxiety.

Libidinal phase development is marked by overlapping as in the perversions, though the confusion and disorganization typical of the perversions is obviated by discharge through the talent. Such personalities show more mobility of psychic energy and greater flexibility in changing instinctual aim and object. It is possible that no process comparable to sublimation therefore occurs. The pressure to create which reaches the momentum of a drive may derive less from neutralized aggression than from unfolding growth pressures, deriving from a somatic nucleus of the earliest development of the primary autonomous ego.

Loewenstein, M.D. (New York).  'Some Thoughts on Interpretation in the Theory and Practice of Psychoanalysis.'  Pp. 127–150.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Rudolph M. Loewenstein, M.D. (New York).  'Some Thoughts on Interpretation in the Theory and Practice of Psychoanalysis.'  Pp. 127–150.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Rudolph M. Loewenstein, M.D. (New York). 'Some Thoughts on Interpretation in the Theory and Practice of Psychoanalysis.' Pp. 127–150.

Interpretation as a tool in the report of investigative procedures is distinguished from interpretation functioning in a therapeutic situation. This distinction is relevant to the regularity with which case reports reiterate similar conceptual content when the individual therapeutic procedures are rich in difference. Interpretation may be approached from a triple viewpoint: interpretations as statements of general explanatory concepts; as statements about the results of psychological investigation of a given person; as used in the individual therapeutic analysis. Comments which are for the most part directed to the third aspect and only differentially to the others, cover consideration of the process as it arises in the analyst, perceptiveness of clues, not confined to the primary process and the nature of the interaction between patient and analyst in a wide sense. 'Preparatory work' resulting in the analysis of defensive structures is illustrated indicating the effective stimulation of linkages between various strata of the patient's mind awakening the flow of primary process connexions. The impact of the analyst's selection of words is specially considered as it effects the regrouping of the patient's material. There is a complementary process being achieved for the patient by effective preparatory work which enables him to offer meaningful clues to the analyst often also condensed in specially selected words.

Patients who make use of analytic jargon in the session make use of the cognitive function devoid of its personal expressive counterpart. The analyst's use of jargon in the session reveals a shift of the cognitive function from the patient's individual problem to more general phenomena. While analyzing we work with these concepts as a framework, but in interpretation we retranslate them into the patient's personal experience. Every analysis can and perhaps ought to be conducted as though the theory were never completely taken for granted.

369

Geleerd, M.D. (New York).  'Some Aspects of Psychoanalytic Technique in Adolescence.'  Pp. 263–283.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Elizabeth R. Geleerd, M.D. (New York).  'Some Aspects of Psychoanalytic Technique in Adolescence.'  Pp. 263–283.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Elizabeth R. Geleerd, M.D. (New York). 'Some Aspects of Psychoanalytic Technique in Adolescence.' Pp. 263–283.

Technical adaptation to the needs of adolescents is considered in so far as it concerns three age groups: early (12–14), middle (14–16), and late (16–18) adolescence. The first period is marked by group identification and rejection of the adult. Repression of the oedipal conflict is intense and some regression sets in. Treatment is not easily accepted except where problems are painful; material is scanty and phantasy life difficult of access. The second period, marked by intense fear of the drives and readily accessible phantasy, but as well by real need for the adult as a helper and interpreter of life, provides easier contact for the analyst, but some danger of precipitating chaotic reactions. In the third period work approximates to adult analysis, with occasional shifts towards earlier periods.

The following technical differences are discussed. Transference reactions but not a true transference neurosis coexist with a relationship of need for the analyst as a real person. Interpretation of the latter factor may undo the treatment aim, though negative feelings should be consistently interpreted. A greater amount of help in learning to test reality is needed. A greater effort has to be made to increase the ego's tolerance of pathogenic conflicts. A consistent and systematic analysis of all defence mechanisms is not possible and in some instances is contra-indicated. The adolescent resorts to many temporary defences in an endeavour to deal with conflicts which in their intensity are passing and immediate analysis may well surrender him to the id. Certain defences which distort reality must, however, be taken up. Because of the continual eruption of changing id processes working through is almost always inadequate and limited.

Case material is used to illustrate technical modifications and to emphasize the need for perceptiveness as to their meaning in the analysis and in the total development of the child.

372

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Nathan N. Root, M.D. (New York). 'A Neurosis in Adolescence.' Pp. 320–334.

A case is presented of a girl who developed anxiety neurosis and masochistic depressive character neurosis, following her marriage at the age of 17. Just prior to the menarche this patient suffered the traumatic loss of her mother in an accident. The analytic process brought into relief the patient's need to mourn which she had unconsciously denied. The material is used to throw light on the normal maturational progress during adolescence as one of detachment from infantile libidinal positions involving the process of mourning. The mechanism of denial of both inner and outer reality may readily be called upon during this period with resultant damaging effects upon the maturational development of the ego.

372

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual), 12, 1957

Robert Plank, LL.D., M.S.W. (Cleveland). 'On Seeing the Salamander.' Pp. 379–398.

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas  

An episode in which the elder Cellini shows his son a salamander in the burning fire is linked with similar experiences of 'blissful' moments of surprised joy depicted in biography. These experiences are linked in their quality with mystical experience with or without religious content (which it is contended may be in the nature of a rationalization). All such experiences are ego-alien, come upon the self from 'elsewhere', and are tinged with awe which accounts for their frequently religious character. Pride in and triumphant assertion of the possession of a penis is the core of the phenomenon. The gratification arises because a conflict has been resolved by a lifting of the self to a higher plane, equivalent in the life of the child to the transition from the oedipal phase to latency.

In the literature the salamander is constantly linked with fire, a reversal of the fire-quenching properties of the penis. The revivified phoenix may symbolize the revivified penis after its state of flaccidity. The author quotes widely from biography and myth and suggests the unwisdom of confusing phenomena of this type with the frankly pathological. The psychologist who does so 'will undoubtedly catch some schizophrenics; he will scarcely see a salamander.

374

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Lili E. Peller. (New York). 'The School's Role in Promoting Sublimation.' Pp. 437–449.

The function of sublimation in promoting stability is discussed and certain problems affecting the individual under modern industrial conditions raised, notably the failure of much adult work to offer libidinal satisfaction of any type. What happens to children when the work of their elders is predominantly void of libidinal investment? The teacher's influence on the formation of ego ideal and on identificatory processes prompts some comment on modern educational procedure. The unreal 'matiness' existing in some progressive schools between teacher and pupil is held to interfere with this process. A wider choice of identification could moreover be afforded by grouping children in mixed age groups. On the other hand the tendency to stress group activities may overlook the important fact that it is the libidinal charge of an activity, not the inducement to sociability, which counts. 'The child who shares a collective activity with lukewarm interest profits less than the child deriving a deep gratification from a solitary pursuit.'

Self-direction is encouraged not only by opportunity to use freedom but also by guidance in anticipating danger and frustrations. The ability to decide and select may be encouraged by simplifying the environment. Walls covered with the large sheets of children's drawings may make children the prisoners of their own imperfections and add pointless confusion and disorganization.

responseSet.title: Neurotic Interaction in Marriage: Edited by Victor W. Eisenstein, M.D.  (New York:  Basic Books, 1956.  Pp. 352.  $5.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Neurotic Interaction in Marriage

Edited by Victor W. Eisenstein, M.D. (New York: Basic Books, 1956. Pp. 352. $5.50.)

E. Gostyn

E. Gostyn

E. Gostyn  

This book, written by a number of distinguished American psycho-analysts, is a stimulating and refreshing symposium, presented in readable and attractive form. Each essay however tends to leave the reader with a feeling of incompleteness. It is an aperitif rather than a comprehensive exposition upon a particular aspect of the subject of Neurotic Interaction in Marriage. One is left with the feeling that this all-star performance lacks something of the cohesion which a less eminent group might have achieved.

The subject is an important one, and, considering how high the incidence of neurosis is, it is surprising how many marriages do survive, and somehow manage to retain an atmosphere of mutual love and devotion. The essential thesis worked out and developed in the book is that neurotic persons take their neuroses with them into matrimony, and no considerable quantum of such neurosis is shed at the altar or purged at the registry office. Subsequently, the neurotic fits or tries to fit his or her unfortunate partner into the strait-jacket of the earlier objects of his or her past life. This poses difficulties, the overcoming of which depends upon a quantitative factor, the ability of the less disturbed partner to cope with the attitudes of the more disturbed one, and also to a possible cancelling out of neurotic difficulties.

351

responseSet.title: On the Significance of Psychic Factors in the Development of Peptic Ulcer: By Hojer-Pedersen.  (Acta Psych. et Neur. Scand., Supplement 119, Vol. 33.)  (Copenhagen:  Munksgaard, 1958.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Significance of Psychic Factors in the Development of Peptic Ulcer

By Hojer-Pedersen. (Acta Psych. et Neur. Scand., Supplement 119, Vol. 33.) (Copenhagen: Munksgaard, 1958.)

Angel Garma

Angel Garma

Angel Garma  

The author studied fifty-one duodenal ulcer cases in a Copenhagen hospital and a control group of fifty-one employees at the same place. The study consisted of two psychiatric interviews, the Rorschach and Raven tests, a graphologic study, and above all a 'special personality questionnaire … based upon subjective and objective criteria and … termed on the OBS test'.

Even before the outbreak of their ulcers, it was found that the patients suffered intense emotional tensions due to their increased conflict preparedness. They showed addiction to alcohol and tobacco and suffered from muscular tension. The ulcer always appeared as a consequence of emotional complications.

According to the OBS test, ulcer patients had qualities of leadership with both a strained ambition and activity, but with an inhibition of emotions, imagination, and sensibility, with a strong feeling of inferiority, introversion, fictitious optimism, and an intense rejection of homosexuality. Almost all of them had an abnormally strong parent-fixation. The graphologic study showed ambition, obstination, inhibition towards aggression, and introversion. The Rorschach test yielded almost no response and showed only a marked tendency for coarctation. All these personality trends in ulcer patients make it difficult for us to accept the author's affirmation that these patients suffered actual neurosis and no other kind of neurosis.

The author insists upon introversion, coarctation, and, above all, his patients' apathy. He considers apathy a very important factor in the genesis of peptic ulcer, and arrives at the following conclusions: 'Persons who have a great conflict preparedness during defence and/or inhibition as part of a chiefly dependent/independent perfectionistic (expansion-inhibited) personality, and who in the case of further stress use apathizing mechanisms in particular, may run the risk of developing a psychosomatic disease, for instance, duodenal ulcer. Provided, of course, that the somatic apparatus is susceptible, or possibly owing to previous and/or simultaneous influences is in a state of (possibly dissociated) hyperactivity of varying degree. As no other acceptable somatic causal factors were found in the series of ulcer-patients, chronic duodenal ulcer can in all probability be considered a psychosomatic disease in the strictest sense.' 'In comparison with the control series I have demonstrated in ulcer-patients nothing but a relatively more pronounced, apathized conflicts preparedness; in the present study I am unable to go in for any fundamental psychological specific findings in addition to this.' The author's enormous effort in order to understand the psyche of his ulcer patients is clearly evident, but his conclusions leave us dissatisfied because of their sparseness and ambiguity. Although a statistical study is valuable, if instead of performing a study with his questionnaire and other tests on a relatively large number of patients and control group he had taken the same period of time for a psycho-analytic treatment of only one patient, he would have been able to go much deeper.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Judith S. Kestenberg, M.D. (New York). 'On the Development of Maternal Feelings in Early Childhood. Observations and Reflections.' Pp. 257–291.

The question of vaginal sensation in early childhood is still controversial. The girl seems to fuse vaginal excitations with sensations from other zones, and to project vaginal tensions to the outside of her body. Unable to experience direct vaginal discharge and unable to master vaginal excitations by active exploration, she tends to externalize her wish for mastery. She finds a suitable substitute for the lack of an organ of discharge in the baby doll. Denial sets in when these mechanisms break down. Where vaginal sensations threaten to invade consciousness repression may ensue. At the end of the phallic stage during which the baby wish has become attached to the penis wish, the inside of the female child is recathected as the girl wishes to be penetrated and hopes to receive a baby. Further repression may ensue at the end of the oedipal stage.

Various fusions and externalizations of early vaginal excitations contribute to the changing image of the girl's inside, which is later used for the development of the image of the vagina as an organ. Successive oral, anal, baby, and phallic representations merge with the final more realistic image of the vagina. Each of these representations contributes to adult maternal functioning. The oral representation, modelled after the primal cavity, fosters the incorporation wish which initiates pregnancy; the anal image contributes to the retentive aspects of pregnancy; anal and urethral images may serve as a model for expulsion in delivery; the baby image enhances the mother's protective role; the early female-baby concept (akin to the transitional object conceived as a projection of the self), coupled with the later penis-baby image make it possible for a mother to accept female and male children. Fantasies concerning the clitoris may underlie the maternal concern with the steady growth of her child. The many times in which the girl gives up her hope for a baby during early development, may prepare her for the cycles of her adulthood and for ultimate resignation to her children leaving her.

responseSet.title: The Predisposing Situation to Peptic Ulcer in Children
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Predisposing Situation to Peptic Ulcer in Children

Elisabeth Garma

Elisabeth Garma

Elisabeth Garma
BUENOS AIRES, ARGENTINE

Elisabeth Garma  

SUMMARY

If care is taken not to overlook gastric contents, which are apt to be screened by oral and excremental ones, a rich symptomatology of complicated ulcer is often found in the analysis of children, usually, however, with no demonstrable organic lesion. This situation of predisposition to peptic ulcer is brought about by failure in the oedipal situation followed by masochistic oral-digestive regression, reactivating a cruel, predominantly maternal, superego that attacks the child's feeding instincts.

responseSet.title: Discussions on Child Development: Vol. 3.  The Proceedings of the World Health Organization Study Group on the Psychobiological Development of the Child.  Geneva 1955.  (London:  Tavistock Publications, 1958.  Pp. 223.  28s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussions on Child Development

Vol. 3. The Proceedings of the World Health Organization Study Group on the Psychobiological Development of the Child. Geneva 1955. (London: Tavistock Publications, 1958. Pp. 223. 28s.)

W. Gillespie

W. Gillespie

W. Gillespie  

When the first two volumes in this series were reviewed here (Vol. 38, 1957, p. 365) the general pattern and underlying plan was described. The group taking part in the discussions now under review was much the same as the previous group, but the guests this time were D. Buckle from the World Health Organization, Erik Erikson, Julian Huxley, and Raymond de Saussure. The present volume differs from the previous two in much the same way that a concerto differs from a symphony; this time we have two virtuoso soloists whose contributions dominate the book, namely Margaret Mead and Erik Erikson.

The first section deals with the childhood genesis of sex differences in behaviour. This is discussed by Margaret Mead with a wealth of fascinating anthropological material, her aim being to elucidate what can be regarded as universal sex differences, and how much of what is often so regarded must be ascribed to cultural influences. She adduces a great many data which are clearly relevant and indicate the great importance of the latter factor; but she by no means denies the intrinsic differences, and does not advance any dogmatic thesis. She lays much stress on the part of the mother in teaching young children of either sex what is their sex rôle. As in previous volumes, many interesting and important points come up in the widely-ranging discussion, e.g. Mead's authoritative statement that the idea of a truly matriarchal society is pure fantasy; or the suggestion that the concept of the latency period is not applicable to the development of girls.

The rest of the book, its greater part, is chiefly concerned with presentations by Erikson. First he describes a method of play construction which he used in examining a series of 12-year-old children, with comments on the typical sexual differences as well as on other points revealed by this method. The next section is devoted to the exposition and lively discussion of what Erikson calls the syndrome of identity diffusion, with special reference to problems of adolescence in a changing society. Erikson gives a vivid clinical picture of the various facets of this problem and shows how it needs to be related to social as well as to individual psychological factors. The discussion focused, among other things, on the special vulnerability of periods of rapid change in the individual and in society, adolescence being, of course, one of the most important.

[[RunningHead]]

Peter and Caroline

A Child Asks about Child-birth and Sex. By Sten Hegeler. Drawings by Gerda Nystad. Foreword by Professor W. C. W. Nixon. (London: Tavistock Publications, 1957. Pp. 29. 7 s. 6 d.)

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft  

In the early decades of this century many psycho-analysts would have endorsed the high hopes entertained in 'advanced' circles of the prophylactic effect of the sexual enlightenment of children. If children, it was believed, had their questions answered straightforwardly and honestly and were spared the prudery and hypocrisy that had been the lot of previous generations, they would grow up free from the guilt and inhibition about sexual matters which was the most striking manifestation of neurosis. However, as psycho-analysis developed, it became apparent that it was all much more difficult, that guilt was, in part at least, the result of pregenital aggressive fantasies which charged the libidinal fantasies of the oedipal phase with guilt-engendering sadism, and that the sexual misconceptions of childhood, which seemed to play such a large part in the development of neurosis and perversion, were not attributable to ignorance or inaccurate information, but were particular manifestations of the specific system of fantasies and defences evoked in the child by the totality of his psychic experience. Furthermore, the suspicion arose that fantasy itself had its value and that harm could be done to a child's imaginative development by premature confrontation with the facts of adult sexuality. As a result, analysts became sceptical as to the value of sexual enlightenment per se and tended to see the problem of infantile sexual curiosity as only one aspect of the larger problem of providing children with a milieu in which they can grow up with sufficient security and integrity to welcome the advent of sexual maturity in adolescence. In On the Bringing Up of Children, edited by the late John Rickman, Nina Searl's answer to the question 'Should the child's questions about sex be answered?' is in the main advice to parents on how to discriminate between questions arising from interest and those arising from anxiety, and on how to avoid in their total attitude to their children the Scylla and Charybdis of over-stimulation and sensual deprivation.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3: David Grauer.  'How Autonomous is the Ego?'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 3

David Grauer. 'How Autonomous is the Ego?'

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

Following upon a review of the ego autonomy concept Grauer criticizes Hartmann's theory of neutralization of instinctual energy. He suggests that a better case for ego autonomy can be made out if autonomous ego operations are considered as dependent on the ego's own energies rather than on those transferred to it from the drives. Federn's concept of medial ego libido provides a better foundation for the autonomous ego functions of perception, thinking, defence, constructive and adaptive activities. Complementary ideas on this topic of autonomy which have been proposed by Weiss, Menninger and Angyal are also discussed.

364

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 4: Hendrick, I.  'Dream Resistance and Schizophrenia.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 4

Hendrick, I. 'Dream Resistance and Schizophrenia.'

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

This paper consists of a fusion of two papers presented by the author in the 1930s. Their interest lies in the fact that he advances a theory of schizophrenia which is very similar to the views which Hartmann has described more recently. Hendrick pointed to the failure of the ego in schizophrenia to deal with excessive quantities of aggressive energy. He regarded dream resistance as similar in its mode of operation to schizophrenic thought. In both there is a prolific outpouring of sexual phantasies which mask violent hostility. In schizophrenia the libidinal processes attempt to neutralize the destructive tendencies within the organism. The libidinal phenomena observed in psychoses are not, therefore, primary manifestations but are the result of attempts to maintain the integrity of the ego by means of neutralization of aggression.

365

responseSet.title: Change of Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Change of Editor

Willi Hoffer M.D.

Willi Hoffer

Willi Hoffer  

The conclusion of this volume marks also the termination of the Editor's second five-year term of office. Dr. J. D. Sutherland, who has been appointed to succeed him, is a graduate of Edinburgh University, Medical Director of the Tavistock Clinic, and a former Editor of the British Journal of Medical Psychology. In 1955 he succeeded Dr. Ernest Jones as Editor of the International Psycho-Analytical Library.

Everyone will agree that the Editorship of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis entails responsibilities of international scope, and the manner in which they are discharged will influence the course of psycho-analysis as a science as well as the development of a healthy tradition among practising psycho-analysts. The editing of a Journal such as this cannot be a one-man job, and the retiring Editor therefore strongly feels the need of thanking those who in the past ten years have co-operated with him in maintaining the standards which Ernest Jones as Founder and first Editor set himself to achieve.

I am particularly indebted to the late Dr. John Rickman and to Dr. W. Clifford M. Scott, from whom I learned much during the period of our joint editorship from 1947 to 1949. From 1949 onwards, when I had become Editor-in-Chief, Dr. Marjorie Brierley has patiently and tirelessly given me a helping hand in assessing and improving manuscripts for publication; her judgement and advice have always been constructive and the thanks I have received from many authors are due to her. Dr. Robert P. Knight, who joined the Editorial Committee in 1952, has proved an indispensable link between American psycho-analysts and the Editorial Committee.

I am glad to say that at no time has any discord interfered with the Editorial Committee's smooth work. As I have done, Dr. Sutherland will do everything possible to maintain unity of purpose in the service of psycho-analysis as a science and as a professional organization.

It has always been most stimulating and rewarding to correspond with the various contributors of original papers, book reviews and of abstracts, and I shall greatly miss this pleasure in the days to come. I ask them to accept my thanks for their co-operation, patience, and understanding of the Editor's position. My sub-editor, the administrative staff of the London Institute of Psycho-Analysis, the publishers, Messrs. Baillière Tindall & Cox, and our printers, The Whitefriars Press, also have my thanks for their co-operation and their regard for the Editor's ambitions. I offer my best wishes to Dr. Sutherland for the future of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Aphasia, a Critical Study

By Dr. Sigmund Freud. Authorized Translation with an Introduction by E. Stengel, M.D. (Vienna), M.R.C.P. (London). (London: Imago Publishing Co., 1953. Pp. 105. 12s. 6d.; New York: International Univ. Press, 1954. Pp. 105. $3.00.)

H. Van Der Waals M.D.

H. Van Der Waals

H. G. Van Der Waals, M.D.
Topeka

H. Van Der Waals  

Almost seventy years after its publication, Freud's

339

essay 'Zur Auffassung der Aphasien' in Stengel's excellent translation still makes good reading for everyone who once has been or is still interested in problems of aphasia and cerebral localization. At the time Freud wrote this monograph (1891) the first great discoveries on cerebral localization (Broca, 1861; Fritsch and Hitzig, 1870; Wernicke, 1874; Munk, 1877–1880) were still recent enough to have kept alive the great expectations aroused by them. Wernicke, taking his starting-point from his investigations in the field of aphasia, never slackened in his attempts to interpret psychic disturbances as neurological symptoms. His pupil Kleist, in spite of the frustration Wernicke suffered in these endeavours, continued these efforts for some further decades.

Freud's essay opens with a highly readable and enlightening criticism of the 'classical theory' of aphasia (Wernicke-Lichtheim), in which he concludes that aphasic disorders cannot be understood as mere problems of localization, pointing out that Wernicke and Lichtheim also had to resort to functional factors for the explanation of aphasia. Especially his critical examination of cases of so-called transcortical motor aphasia convinced Freud that it could not be attributed solely to a circumscribed lesion of tracts or centres, but that functional changes also had to be taken into account. Freud therefore advances the hypothesis that the centres of the speech apparatus react as a whole with an alteration of function to partial lesions, causing a reduction of excitability, and refers to Bastian, who distinguished three states of reduced excitability of a centre.

Freud's attempt to arrive at a new concept of aphasia certainly forms the most important part of his essay. This concept greatly influenced the thinking of later investigators; in the light of later acquired knowledge we can only admire Freud's perspicacity and sagacity. To localize the physiological correlate of an idea in a nerve cell, as did Wernicke and Meynert, was unacceptable to Freud. In his opinion this physiological correlate could not be anything static, but had to be something in the nature of a process starting at a specific point of the cortex, from there spreading over the whole cortex and along certain pathways. Following this line of thought, Freud rejects the assumptions that the speech apparatus consists of distinct centres separated by functionless areas, and that ideas (memories) serving speech are stored in certain parts of the cortex called centres, while their association is provided exclusively by subcortical fibre tracts. Freud sets against these assumptions the view that the speech area is a continuous cortical region within which associations underlying the speech functions are taking place; these associations are, as Freud says, of a complexity beyond comprehension. In advancing such a theory Freud, of course, has to take care of the apparent proof by morbid anatomy of the existence of speech centres, especially the areas of Broca and Wernicke. Freud does so in a convincing way; his concept of the organization of the central apparatus of speech is now generally accepted (Storch, Kleist, Goldstein).

responseSet.title: The Psychodynamics of Family Life: Diagnosis and Treatment of Family Relationships: By Nathan W. Ackerman, M.D.  (New York:  Basic Books, 1958.  Pp. 379.  $6.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychodynamics of Family Life: Diagnosis and Treatment of Family Relationships

By Nathan W. Ackerman, M.D. (New York: Basic Books, 1958. Pp. 379. $6.75.)

Gillian Elles

Gillian Elles

Gillian Elles  

In this book Dr. Ackerman describes the growth of a point of view that germinated some twenty-five years ago. In order to help disturbed patients he feels that it is vital to view the individual disturbances in the larger setting of their meaning to the family. Therefore in his first section he sets out his conceptual framework which enables him to do this. His framework is a synthesis of psycho-analytic personality theory with recent works of American social scientists.

He continually draws attention to the fact that character disorders are seldom found in isolation, and that mental illness limited to a single family member is a rarity. Therefore he sets any sick process as simultaneously occurring intra-psychically, interpersonally, and as a distortion in the social processes of the environment itself. The emphasis he puts on this last form of illness seems to be one of the important messages of the book. He highlights the cumulative effect of mental illness within the primary group where the distortion of the usual social processes adds in a dramatic way to the difficulties of the primary patient. Dr. Ackerman's conceptual framework allows the sick process to be seen and treated inside and outside the primary patient. Equally it can show in detail the health-maintaining processes latent or functioning in and around the primary patient. The play between these two processes he feels to be of considerable diagnostic importance. He shows that it determines how the individual deals with his emotional problems, how the family use them or are crippled by them. Finally he demonstrates the way in which the emotional content of these current family experiences then qualifies the individual's capacity to deal with the original personal conflicts.

Having established his method of approach, Dr. Ackerman uses the following sections to illustrate the clinical advantage that ensues from a wider diagnostic appraisal. The therapist can establish a responsibility to the family or to the individual. Either alone or with his team he can systematically treat both the primary patient and the disturbance within the family.

responseSet.title: The Control of Human Behaviour
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Control of Human Behaviour

Mortimer Ostow M.D.

Mortimer Ostow

Mortimer Ostow, M.D.
RIVERDALE, NEW YORK.

Mortimer Ostow  

I. INTRODUCTION

The sexual reproductive mechanism requires that two mature animals influence each other's behaviour at least briefly, and the flocking of gregarious animals requires that each individual respond to the influence of the group. Hence the ability to exert and to respond to intraspecific influence must be primitive properties of all sexually reproducing and gregarious animals. Upon this biological foundation is based an intricate system of devices whereby humans exert a continual influence one upon the other.

In all activities in which two or more people are engaged in some relation to each other, a more or less mutual influence prevails. Only when he is working on the soil, on the earth's resources, and on material things (or on the scientific study of these) is man temporarily not participating in an exchange of influence with another person, though when even these activities are performed in solitude, they usually have an indirect goal determined by the individual's relation to other people. It is possible to follow in the history of civilization accounts of methods and goals of influencing human behaviour. These are seen in the areas of group organization such as government, religion, ethics, and morality; in areas of personal gratification such as family life and affectionate relations; and in areas of the individual's relation to the group such as commercial activity, teaching, and personal and professional service.

The gratifying effects of some of those forms of influence are enjoyed and cultivated now as they have been in the past. Other modes of mutual influence are grossly distressing and have been deplored since the beginning of historical time. War and tyranny are the chief of these, and personal destructiveness and hatred follow close behind. Today, however, we are appalled as we see atomic energy impelling sword as well as ploughshare, and electronic regulation directing spear as well as pruning hook. Limitless communication assembles people as often in the groves of Baal as on the mountains of the Lord, for even in spirit man remains a carnivore. Physical sciences have to date only made him more efficient; they have given him no control over his nature. Leaders are bumbling along with intuition and common sense no more refined than in the Stone Age, though their power becomes daily more terrible.

responseSet.documentRef: Williams, A. H. (1959) Eros and Civilization: By Herbert Marcuse.  (London:  Routledge.  Pp. 277.  25 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  40:359-360
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Williams, A. H. (1959) Eros and Civilization: By Herbert Marcuse. (London: Routledge. Pp. 277. 25 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 40:359-360
responseSet.documentRefXML: Williams, A. H. (1959) Eros and Civilization: By Herbert Marcuse. (London: Routledge. Pp. 277. 25 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 40:359-360
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195940359-360CEros and CivilizationBy Herbert Marcuse. (London: Routledge. Pp. 277. 25 s.)A.HyattWilliams
responseSet.title: Eros and Civilization: By Herbert Marcuse.  (London:  Routledge.  Pp. 277.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Eros and Civilization

By Herbert Marcuse. (London: Routledge. Pp. 277. 25 s.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

The theme is the study of the philosophy of Freud's work, a philosophy which is implicit in all Freud's writings, but which becomes explicit from time to time. Civilization is based upon the permanent subjugation of human instincts, and it is questionable whether the suffering thereby inflicted upon individuals has been worth the benefits of culture. The problem is how to render the instincts safe and yet preserve pleasure. All too often there is a tendency to lose the pleasure and yet not effectively curb the instincts. The problem consists of an interplay between the two polarities, Eros and Thanatos. Both instincts, so to speak, are contra-cultural. The change from immediate to delayed gratification, from joy or play to discipline and toil, from receptiveness to productiveness, from internal security and the absence of repression to external security and curtailment by repression, are what happens when animal man becomes a cultured human being. The way in which civilized man became as he is can be traced both ontogenetically and phylogenetically, and this is the way in which the subject is considered by the author, who regards the effect of the civilizing process as one of ever-increasing constraint, with the growth of fantasy as an almost functionless method of internal escapism. Work and productivity are regarded as formidable constraints hardly worth while. Prometheus, the prototype of toil, is considered, together with his polar opposites, Orpheus and Narcissus, bent as they were upon lives of song and dream. Here begins the synthesis of the work. The author regards both these figures as symbols of a non-repressive, erotic attitude towards reality.

359

responseSet.title: Autobiographical Aspects of the Writer's Imagery
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autobiographical Aspects of the Writer's Imagery11

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer, M.D.
NEW YORK

Mark Kanzer  

SUMMARY

The imagery of the writer is inevitably determined by his individual experiences and outlook. It is consequently of potential value as a projective test of his personality, creative processes, and life history. Extensive use of the principle, influenced more or less unconsciously by psychoanalysis, has already been made by literary scholars. Their methods and findings are reviewed and aspects of interest to the analyst discussed. With increasing emphasis on the activities of the preconscious, the feats of the creative imagination of writers prove relevant to the interpretation of images both in dreams and during free association.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Jeanne Lampl-DeGroot, M.D. (Amsterdam).  'On Defense and Development, Normal and Pathological.'  Pp. 114–126.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Jeanne Lampl-DeGroot, M.D. (Amsterdam). 'On Defense and Development, Normal and Pathological.' Pp. 114–126.

The infant uses inborn and learned regulation and adaptation mechanisms to establish physiological homeostasis. The little child does the same to establish psychic equilibrium. The author considers that all pathological defence mechanisms seen in the neuroses and psychoses of latency and adulthood are the same mechanisms that served normal ego development in early childhood where their role of adaptation and regulation is stressed. This formulation has the advantage of emphasizing the continuum of physiological-biological and psychic processes in the normal and the abnormal. When at first with the help of an auxiliary ego borrowed from the mother the ego succeeds in using the various mechanisms from the aspect of their regulative and adaptive side, conflicts may be solved in a way what stimulates balance and growth. When the ego has to fight too strongly against the drives, for inner or external reasons, using all available forces for the maintenance of its still precarious organization, a lasting disturbance may result. (In this respect it is the ego's capacity to deal with the aggressive drives which is of paramount importance. Every conflict promotes free aggression which is possibly more unsuitable for neutralization than is libido and most dangerous to the developing ego when turned against the self.) We should speak of a real childhood neurosis only when the defensive functions of the mechanisms outbalances the regulative functions and interferes with ego growth.

368

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 1956: Ernst Kris, Ph.D.  (New York).  'The Recovery of Childhood Memories in Psychoanalysis.'  Pp. 54–88.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Ernst Kris, Ph.D. (New York). 'The Recovery of Childhood Memories in Psychoanalysis.' Pp. 54–88.

Dynamic processes in the recovery of memories are discussed having regard to technical innovations which followed the study of defence mechanisms (1936). The interrelation of reconstruction and effective therapy is more complex than was assumed in Freud's earliest theories. Reconstructive work may shelter relevant conflicts where biographical connexions are isolated from affect or otherwise hidden. On the other hand, detailed analysis of current conflict situations may highlight a hidden identity between past and present and open the way for genetic interpretation. The latter may point to archaic impulses representing preverbal ideations embedded in later experiences or establish an historical context linking the isolated elements in the patient's material, according as stress is laid on the endopsychic or environmental factors.

Problems in the reconstruction of archaic material are illustrated from specific cases; the danger of the patient getting control of an empty id vernacular to be used as resistance; the pre-essential lifting of anticathexis against derivatives spread over wide areas of behaviour; the distinguishing of repeated (strain) traumata from single (shock) events, as yet hardly understood; the fact that reconstruction is regularly concerned with thoughts and feelings which did not necessarily exist at the time of the 'the event', but which derive from the interaction of the infantile experience with later events. The recovery of childhood memories is part of the struggle between the ego and the id and also of a rearguard struggle which takes place within the ego itself. The latter reflects in some way the functional distribution of various defences in each personality, particularly the interaction between repression and more archaic methods, which in some personalities still remain central for the solution of conflict. In psychotic and transitional states the dysfunction of both sets of mechanisms sometimes leads to unusual memory phenomena. These facts may be relevant to the distinction between memories which suddenly emerge from repression during analysis and memories which had been preconsciously available before they entered the patient's communications. The closer we approach the area where repression has lost its power as central defence, the less can the transition from past to present become an indicator of progress. The core of the therapeutic effectiveness of memory recovery rests in the dynamics of the lifting of anticathexis. Then memories enter the stream of thought in associative causal connexions which stabilize their continued existence as part of the ego and prevent re-repression. Energy set free by the exploration of defence and repressed ideas should reinforce the capacity for neutralization and be discharged in social function.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mental Depressions and their Treatment

By Samuel Henry Kraines. (New York: The Macmillan Co., 1957. Pp. 555. $8.00. Also London: Macmillan, 56s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The origin of manic-depressive illness is unknown. We have only working hypotheses, none of which has so far thrown much light on the mystery. Heredity is no doubt important, and so are psychological factors. The thesis of this book is that 'the basic manic-depressive illness always has its origin in physiologic susceptibility which is subject to the releasing, modifying and prolonging influence of psychic factors.' This thesis is not new but still in need of confirmation. The author believes that dysfunction of the diencephalic-rhinencephalic-reticular systems, which have recently been much in the news, is an important etiological factor. At times he gives the impression that this is proved already. Endocrine factors are also regarded as significant in the causation of the illness. The presentation is discursive and often unnecessarily dogmatic. The uninitiated reader will find it difficult to distinguish between the established and the hypothetical. The book is not likely to impress those who lean towards the author's views, or those who do not share them.

350

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The 21st Congress of the International Psycho-Analytical Association will be Held in Copenhagen at Falkonercentret, 9 Falkonerallé Copenhagen, F., from Sunday, 26 July to Thursday, 30 July (Incl.), 1959

MEMBERSHIP OF THE CONGRESS

Membership of the Congress is open to the following groups:

a. Those whose names are included in the list of members of the International Psycho-Analytical Association published in the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, 1958, Volume 39, (5).

b. Members and associates whose names are not included in this list, but whose application form has been countersigned by the Secretary of his or her local Psycho-Analytical Society.

c. Students of psycho-analysis, registered at training centres recognized by the International Psycho-Analytical Association, whose application form has been countersigned by the Secretary of the local Psycho-Analytical Society.

d. Guests whose application form has been countersigned by the Secretary of the local Psycho-Analytical Society. Applications under this category may be submitted by the Congress Administration to the President of the International Psycho-Analytical Association for final approval.

e. Husbands or wives of members of the Congress.

CONGRESS FEE

The Congress fee of seven guineas or twenty-one U.S. dollars covers admission to all sessions as well as a copy of the programme. Neither the cost of excursions nor the Congress dinner are included. The Congress fee should be remitted before 15 March to HANDELSBANKEN, 2 Holmens Kanal, Copenhagen K, by cheque made out to the '21st International Psycho-Analytical Congress.' Application for membership of the Congress is not accepted before remittance of the Congress fee.

If a member wishes to cancel his/her membership of the Congress, half of the fee will be refunded if such cancellation takes place before 1 June, 1959. After this date no refund can be made.

Those who want to take part in the 1959 Congress are requested to fill in Form A and—upon remittance of the Congress fee—to send it to Thorkil Vanggaard, M.D., 51 H. C. Andersens Boulevard, Copenhagen V.

HOTEL ACCOMMODATION

The Congress administration has reserved a number of hotel rooms for members of the Congress. Members are free to make independent arrangements, but those who wish to avail themselves of the service of the Congress administration are requested to fill in form B and return it to Thorkil Vanggaard, M.D., 51 H. C. Andersens Boulevard, Copenhagen V, before 15 March. Owing to the very tight hotel situation in Copenhagen during the summer months it is urgently necessary to make hotel reservations not later than 15 March.

responseSet.title: News, Notes and Comments
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

News, Notes and Comments

INTERNATIONAL PSYCHO-ANALYTICAL ASSOCIATION

The Association's 21st International Congress took place at Copenhagen, Denmark, from 26 to 30 July, 1959, and was attended by more than 800 members and guests of the organization. The Danish Psycho-Analytical Society, under whose auspices the Congress was held, particularly its President Dr. Torkil Vanggaard, took every care to make the scientific and social programme a success. All participants without exception appreciate Dr. and Mrs. Vanggaard's and the Copenhagen Committee's attention to details. The official report in the Bulletin and the Scientific Proceedings will be published in the 1960 volume of this Journal.

At the Business Meeting the following were elected members of the Executive: President: Dr. W. H. Gillespie (London). Vice-Presidents: Grete Bibring, M.D. (Boston); Ruth S. Eissler, M.D. (New York); Anna Freud, LL.D. (London); Maxwell Gitelson, M.D. (Chicago); Willi Hoffer, M.D. (London); Dr. Jean Lampl-de Groot (Amsterdam); Dr. Raymond de Saussure (Geneva); Hon. Treasurer: Phyllis Greenacre, M.D. (New York). Miss Pear King, B.A. Hons. (London) was reappointed Hon. Secretary.

Dr. Heinz Hartmann has been elected Honorary President of the I.P.A.; Mme. Marie Bonaparte (Paris) was elected Honorary Vice-President at the 1957 International Congress in Paris.

The 22nd International Congress will be held in Great Britain in 1961.

THE ASSOCIATION FOR PSYCHOANALYTIC MEDICINE (NEW YORK)

Dr. Robert A. Senescu has been appointed Professor and Chairman of the Department of Psychiatry at the Medical College of Virginia, Richmond, Virginia.

THE EDWARD BIBRING MEMORIAL MEETING

The Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute held the Edward Bibring Memorial Meeting on 14 April, 1959 at the Boston Medical Library. Anna Freud, LL.D., spoke on 'Edward Bibring's Contribution to the Understanding of Psycho-therapy and Technique', David Rapaport, Ph.D. on 'Edward Bibring's Theory of Depression'; the President, Joseph J. Michaels, M.D. was in the chair.
