responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La Psychanalyse, 1953–1955

Vol. I, 'Sur la Parole et le langage', vii+291 pp.; Vol. II, 'Mélanges cliniques, ' 323 pp. (Presses Universitaires de France, 1956.)

Lilla Veszy-Wagner

Lilla Veszy-Wagner

Lilla Veszy-Wagner  

The first volume is a symposium dedicated to semiotic and linguistic matters and is greatly influenced by existentialist teachings (Jaspers, Heidegger, etc.). We even find a similar trend in the second volume, in the preliminary part of J. Lacan's seminar in a short story by E. A. Poe, trying to find a common ground for Heidegger's and Kierkegaard's philosophical principles and Freud's meta-psychological ideas as expressed in 'Entwurf einer Psychologie' (1893). It makes difficult reading by reason of the use of a different nomenclature from that accepted by psycho-analysis. Neologisms like 'chosification' ('thingification' if we had to translate it, I, p. v) or 'autruicherie' ('ostrichism', II, p. 34) do not help towards clarification. (J. Lacan's essays take up the greater part of Vol. I as well).

On the other hand, D. Lagache's paper: 'Sur le polyglottisme dans l'analyse', on bilingual analyses (in Vol. I), brings us back to the familiar analytic idiom of our profession. Choice and transition from one language to another reveals, according to Dr. Lacan's data of resistance, transference and phantasy life. Rejection of or clinging to the mother tongue has its significance, since it is the ego ideal which manifests itself in the chosen language (?), and the return to it means a return to one's childhood. The second, auxiliary language used could mean a defence or an attempt at approaching the analyst or, conversely, hostility or rivalry. The analyst's incomplete command of the patient's language is not regarded as a serious obstacle. The other topics dealt with to a greater extent are related to the subject-matter of death. Dr. Lacan's other paper is an excellent clinical essay, a case presentation on pathological mourning. He contradicts the theory that in mourning there is a conflict between the superego and the ego which ultimately identifies with the lost object, but he affirms that identification with the lost object appears as a means to solve the conflict between the ego and the maternal superego. The deceased represents a moral instance which is in favour of death (mortification) and against the mourner's wish to continue living. Identification with the deceased is an attempt to appease the guilt derived from the latter wish by installing death in the course of life in a masochistic fashion (by restrictions). The work of mourning is aiming at disengagement. A similar topic is discussed in S. Leclaire's paper on the rôle of death in the life of the obsessional neurotic. R. Lefort reports on a case of a new 'Wolf-man' who is transformed into a wolf. In F. Perrier's phobic case a patient is afraid of becoming attracted by the preverbal impulse which is not of a motor character but is a fascination which Perrier calls the 'process of passivation'. M. Schweich gives an account of recent investigations into the nature and treatment of schizophrenia. The clinical volume is completed by some other essays, a few translations, a review of E. Glover's technical papers (by C. Laurin), etc.

This is a well-written textbook for psychologists engaged in work with neurotic and psychotic patients. Unlike some other books of its kind, it does not unduly stress the rôle of the psychologist as a psychotherapist. There are excellent chapters on projection tests and the psychology of the interview. The book should prove of considerable help to clinical psychologists.

351

Mental Acts

(Studies in Philosophical Psychology, edited by R. F. Holland.) By Peter Geach. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. x + 136. 12 s. 6 d.)

H. Home

H. Home

H. Home  

This book discusses the logical status of concepts used to report mental acts, that is, people's thoughts and feelings. It is, therefore, a book about logic and not about psychology, which will doubtless get the review it deserves in the appropriate journals. Mr. Geach writes with clarity and precision, and meta-psychologists might do well to read him, in order to become aware of the kind of criticism their work must eventually be adequate to meet.

356

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Matrix of Medicine: Some Social Aspects of Medical Practice

Edited by Nicholas Malleson, M.D., M.R.C.P. (London: Pitman, 1958. 45 s.)

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard  

Now that this book exists, it should become a 'must' for all psycho-analysts to read, in order to enjoy the process of their own further enlightenment. The subtitle explains its wide terms of reference, without being able to do justice either to the span of information provided, or to the level of excellence of presentation by its sixteen contributors. Of these, the Editor is one, and his late mother, who was among the most admired and intrepid of doctors in this country, is another. The book could also be entitled 'The contributions of Medicine to contemporary civilization and vice versa'. No professional day in an analyst's life can go by without reference to some of the information or informed opinions brought together in this symposium. If, then, he acquires the knowledge, properly defined as vital, which this book so well provides, it will increase his sophistication, in the best sense of the word. One looks forward to changing editions, each one of which will provide a dated 'documentary' of social medicine, for those historians who will seek to record the civilizing trends and aims of the past. Among such aims must be included the spirit and intention which created this book.

357

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Edith Jacobson, M.D. (New York). 'Normal and Pathological Moods: Their Nature and Functions.' Pp. 73–113.

Moods are ego states characterized by generalized discharge modifications which temporarily influence the qualities of all feelings, thoughts, and actions. They are evoked by intense experiences causing high energetic tensions leading to an overflow of energy throughout the ego. These processes go along with generalized transference phenomena, a point which emphasizes the differences between moods and object-directed feeling states. The latter are characterized by libidinous or aggressive investments in specific objects. The moods transfer the qualities of the provocative to all objects and experiences, imparting a special colouring to the world and hence to the self. Since they permit gradual repetitive discharge with reality testing on many objects, they must be regarded as a primitive economic modality of the ego.

States of sadness and grief appear to develop as a contrast effect induced by the discrepancy between equally overinvested opposing memories and phantasies (e.g. of past happiness and present loss). The frustrated search for happiness lost makes the world empty, the self deprived and poor. In so far as the sad person cherishes his past, he will feel deprived but not worthless. The libidinous cathexis of self and object world is retained, though in a reduced form. Hence object relations and ego activities acquire a subdued quality. It would appear that the contrast effect, achieved by the factor of unexpectedness in gratification, plays a significant role in all states of gaiety and cheerfulness.

In so far as moods colour or overstate one aspect of reality and understate an opposing aspect, they involve denial and distortion. The nature of this denial differs in normal and pathological moods and provides significant differential-diagnostic criteria.

In general, denial affects both the self and the object world. It may be directed against the provocative event itself, against its impact or its meaning. The denial may operate directly against external reality, or affect primarily the inner imagery, and only by way of projection, external facts. Finally there may be denial of mood awareness, a narcissistic phenomenon to be distinguished from the denial of specific affects or moods involving both object and self, and deriving from the ego.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Gabriel Casuso, M.D., D.Psych. (London.) With an Introduction by Anna Freud, LL.D. (London). 'Anxiety Related to the Discovery of the Penis, An Observation.' Pp. 169–174.

A week after a 10-month-old child had stood alone for the first time, he discovered his penis with

369

signs of intense fear. This experience was repeated and gave way only to reassurance from the mother's touching his penis and linking it by gesture with his other body parts. A fortnight later, having already accepted his penis apparently as part of himself, he showed intense interest in his urination which followed a period of masturbation with pleasure. Though erections had been observed when his mother was engaged in cleaning him, masturbation did not produce observed erection. During the next two months pleasure in being washed and powdered by his mother produced outbursts of laughter and any interference with masturbation made him angry. He began to walk the length of the playpen on his first birthday.

At the age of 16 months, there has been no further sign of fear concerning his body, although fascination reappeared in connexion with the discovery of his knees and toes. Erections began to appear in connexion with masturbation at about 13 months. The author recalls that fear of the big bathtub preceded the first observation by about one month.

This observation is linked with a similar one by Loewenstein (1950), where however anxiety was less apparent. 'Perhaps the explanation for this difference could be sought along the lines of the timing (which is probably variable in each individual) of the discovery of the penis in relation to the discovery of other parts of the body which leads to the infant's formation of his concept of body image. At that time, through his discovery of body boundaries by sight and hand, the penis was not only alien but also particularly disturbing in its strong sensations because of its erotic sensitivity. … Loewenstein contends that this anxiety, this uncertainty whether or not the penis is part of the body, sets the model for what at a later stage of development and under different circumstances is felt as castration anxiety.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Instinct in Man; In the Light of Recent Work in Comparative Psychology

By Ronald Fletcher. (London: George Allen & Unwin Ltd. 1957. Pp. 348. 40s.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

This book by the Lecturer in Sociology, Bedford College, London, is a revised version of his Ph.D. thesis, and its main aim is 'to explore the possibilities of establishing a reliable basis of psychological theory which would prove useful in the context of wider sociological theory.' In Part I he surveys earlier work on instincts, from Darwin and William James through Lloyd Morgan and Hobhouse to McDougall, Drever, and Ginsburg. He discusses the criticisms levelled at their views and points out the considerable measure of agreement between their theories, particularly in regard to the discernibility and importance of instinctual experience and behaviour in man. In Part II he reviews the contribution of modern ethologists, summarizing the work of Lorenz, Tinbergen, Brun, and others, where again there are many differences of opinion but a considerable area of agreement.

The author then turns to a critical but fundamentally sympathetic examination of Freud's views on instinct. Although he has no practical experience of psycho-analysis he has grasped many of the more essential of Freud's insights and he seems to be devoid of 'emotional resistance'. His criticisms are based on logical and comparative grounds, and his presentation is in the main fair and clear although inevitably brief and selective, and is amply supported by quotations. Only in one minor instance, his definition of 'undoing' as, in effect, a variant of repression, does he go astray; so far as my memory serves the term 'undoing' is never used in psycho-analysis except in connexion with the obsessional technique of 'doing and undoing'. Such an error is excusable in a sociologist who is not a psycho-therapist.

It would take too long to follow the author's arguments in detail, but his conclusions may be noted. First, the scope of Freud's contribution is limited solely to human life; 'Those generalizations which are made with regard to instinct in animals, or in organic life in general, are practically worthless.' His attempts at classification are 'inadequate' and the speculative concept of Death Instincts 'invalid'. Freud's description of instincts as having a source, impetus, aim, and object says nothing really new but supports the views of earlier writers and modern ethologists. The positive contributions outweigh the negative aspects, namely: (1) the emphasis 'upon the existence, the extent and the importance of instinctual experience in the development of human personality and in the complex activities of social life'; (2) the greater elaboration of Freud's account and his development of 'a set of completely fresh concepts which prove to be extremely useful in the task of interpreting the complexities of human personality'; (3)

responseSet.title: The Education of Young Children: By D. E. M. Gardner.  (London:  Methuen, 1956.  Pp. 118.  7 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Education of Young Children

By D. E. M. Gardner. (London: Methuen, 1956. Pp. 118. 7 s. 6 d.)

James Anthony

James Anthony

James Anthony  

There is a malicious extension of a Shavian aphorism that runs as follows: Those who can, do; those who cannot, teach; and those who cannot teach, teach teachers. But here comes Miss Gardner—the doyen of all teachers of teachers—to give this the lie. She has 'done' and she has taught, and with a wealth of personal experience behind her and the shining example of our one and only Susan Isaacs in front of her, she has produced this little book on the current approach to the teaching of little children. The new environment of learning is described in all its spontaneity, freshness, informality, and creativity; the world is caught with both hands and at first hand, not indirectly through the eyes of weary pedagogues. It is nice to think that psycho-analysis has played its part in infusing this wholesome spirit into modern education.

Much of the forthright personality of the author comes through in the writing, and in the end one is left with the impression of a teacher with a deep respect for the child and a sincere regard for his point of view.

357

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1: Mortimer Ostow.  'The Illusory Reduplication of Body Parts in Cerebral Disease.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1

Mortimer Ostow. 'The Illusory Reduplication of Body Parts in Cerebral Disease.'

Although the reduplication clearly serves to replace a defective or an absent structure, Ostow believes that the defence can be delineated more precisely.

Such patients have undergone a catastrophic experience: a segment of the real world, a part of the body, has ceased to exist for them. The primary response has already been noted by Linn as a withdrawal of attention from the horrifying percept. The attempt to regenerate the destroyed part by illusion Ostow regards as comparable to the attempt of schizophrenics to repopulate the world they have lost.

366

responseSet.title: Family and Kinship in East London: By Michael Young and Peter Willmott.  (London:  Routledge, 1957.  Pp. xix + 232.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Family and Kinship in East London

By Michael Young and Peter Willmott. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. xix + 232. 25 s.)

Margaret Bavin

Margaret Bavin

Margaret Bavin  

This book is the first of a series of surveys by the Institute of Community Studies of family relationships in an industrial society and its associated housing estates. The authors look at family life in Bethnal Green and 'Greenleigh', primarily through the eyes of middle-aged husbands and wives. They give a vivid picture of the social background of their subjects, and attempt an analysis of the present system of family relationships and how the past shapes family behaviour in the present. In their investigation of nearly a thousand families, the authors find how dependent these are on the reality of the relationships which bind together the three-generation family in a complex system of rights and obligations. The key to the structure is the 'Mum' in the oldest generation, the evidence showing the main emphasis to lie on the strong tie between the mother and married daughter.

The second part of the book deals with the changes in family relationships resulting from the move to 'Greenleigh' twenty miles away, where parents and children are on their own. Here the emphasis lies on the loneliness of the married daughter, and shows how the quality of life changes. The only basis of judgement is external appearance; this means appraising and being appraised in terms of material things, clothes, curtains, TV sets, and cars.

The authors conclude with the suggestion that directors of housing policies in the future should make it possible for individuals to have the freedom to live with or near a younger or older generation, thus taking into account the reality of the relationships which bind together the wider three-generation family.

This is a warm and fascinating book which is to be welcomed as a contribution to building up an objective picture of the family under the social conditions of today.

71

responseSet.title: Oscar Friedmann, 1903—1958
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Oscar Friedmann, 1903—1958

D. Winnicott

D. Winnicott

D. Winnicott  

Oscar Friedmann, who died suddenly in London on 28 December, 1958, was born in March, 1903 in Germany, the eldest son in a family of nine children and for some time the only boy. He appears to have had a happy family life in his first years. He matured early, and it was therefore a heavy blow to him that on his father's death, when he was ten, the father's brothers decided that he and his sisters and brother should enter an orphanage. As a consequence he was unable to help his bereaved mother or her children. This might have embittered him, but instead it set him a pattern for his lifework. He made the helping of children his special job. He was dominated by the aim to keep a child at home when a home exists, and to meet the child's need to be able to contribute to its upkeep.

It happened that after his father's death he was sent to a bad institution, and he therefore knew at first hand the full extent of childhood frustration and misery. Soon he was transferred to a better institution, where he formed a strong relationship with the Director, which lasted until the latter's recent death. Here again experience became the basis for a pattern of action.

Friedmann trained as a teacher and later became a social worker. He did social work mainly with delinquent boys, and became Director of a Borstal in 1932 at Wolzig, near Berlin. He established himself as one who had the power to help boys and girls in need, especially adolescents. He was thus fulfilling himself at a time when, as a result of the Nazi régime, he and the children of the Borstal were put into a concentration camp which was notorious for the active cruelty practised in it. Our colleague suffered much physical hurt, and as a result of direct assault had a permanent partial paralysis of one side of the face and some unsteadiness of one hand. Fortunately he was transferred from this camp to a prison. Released in 1933, he went of his own free-will to take charge of sixty of the boys from the Borstal. This was felt by the boys as an heroic act, and it is said that they never forgot it. In this work he had but little personal freedom, and the boys were in two rooms, and always under guard. He made out of this a life full of constructive activities for these boys.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.: George Devereux.  'The Significance of the External Female Genitalia and of the Female Orgasm for the Male.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.

George Devereux. 'The Significance of the External Female Genitalia and of the Female Orgasm for the Male.'

Amongst Micronesians there is a custom of artificially enlarging the female genitalia. This occurs in association with the practice of unilateral castration in the men. Devereux proposes that this hypertrophy of the clitoris and of the labia offsets castration anxiety in the male. The women's genitalia are made to approximate to those of the men. Observation of these people suggests that there is a close relationship between orgasm, enlargement of the labia minora, and urination.

362

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Philip Weissman, M.D. (New York). 'The Childhood and Legacy of Stanislavsky.' Pp. 399–417.

This paper is part of a longer study on outstanding creative personalities in modern drama in which their lives and work are studied psycho-analytically for the light shed on the development of creative imagination. Stanislavsky's early years were spent with a devoted mother and a father who made dramatic activity a feature of the life of his large family. Thus his early dramatic exploits did not make Stanislavsky his father's rival but rather compliant with his wishes. Cover memories suggest the early development of an 'artistic ego' which relegated the 'personal ego' of the child to secondary significance.

373

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Instinctive Behavior. The Development of a Modern Concept

Translated and edited by Claire H. Schiller. Introduction by Karl S. Lashley. Contributors: D. J. Kuenen, Konrad Lorenz, Nicholas Tinbergen, Paul H. Schiller, and Jacob von Uexküll. (New York: International Universities Press, 1957. Pp. xix + 328. $7.50.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

This book is intended primarily to supply American psychologists with fuller information about the work of European ethologists. It presents the development of their concept of instinctive behaviour by excellent translations of monographs in chronological order from Uexküll, 1934, to Lorenz, 1952. As Karl S. Lashley says in his Introduction, 'The study of animal behavior during the present century has developed along two distinct lines, the products of professional psychologists in America and of zoologists in Europe.' American workers have concentrated mainly on laboratory experiments on learning, whereas the Europeans have been concerned with problems of the whole behaviour of animals in their natural environment or in captivity. In a Preface, Tinbergen considers these differing approaches, each having its own 'merits and shortcomings'. He is of opinion that the lack of fusion between the two is not only due to 'difference in interest' but to 'sheer ignorance of each other's work', caused in part by language difficulties, not the least of these being differences in technical vocabulary. This volume should do much to resolve these problems. The book is illustrated and contains a useful bibliography.

151

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Family Constellation as a Character and Marriage Determinant

Walter Toman

Walter Toman

Walter Toman
WALTHAM, MASS.

Walter Toman  

SUMMARY

The effects of the basic determinants of family constellation (sex and rank of siblings, also the parents' siblings, and final losses suffered in the immediate family) on interpersonal relationships have been investigated theoretically and implemented by some clinical data as well as one clinical case.

responseSet.title: A Note on Freud's 'Instincts and their Vicissitudes'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Note on Freud's 'Instincts and their Vicissitudes'

Dorwin Cartwright

Dorwin Cartwright

Dorwin Cartwright
Frank Harary
ANN ARBOR, MICH.

Dorwin Cartwright  

SUMMARY

We have proposed an axiom system which is consistent and has as its primitives abstractions of the entities involved in the foundations of ego development. The kind of mathematics appropriate for this purpose is the theory of balance in signed graphs. At each successive stage in its development, new points are introduced into the initial ego and new stresses and strains are induced. In accordance with a procedural theorem derived from postulated tendencies toward balance, and toward clustering, these are resolved by the process in which the ego rearranges entities into subjective and objective clusters.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.: Brian Bird.  'A Study of the Bisexual Meaning of the Foreskin.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.

Brian Bird. 'A Study of the Bisexual Meaning of the Foreskin.'

This is a case presentation the aim of which is to demonstrate the unconscious phantasy which came to be associated with the foreskin. The patient was a boy of 7 who suffered from conversion hysteria. That he was not circumcised in contrast to his father and brother accentuated his feminine identification. He, like his young sister, had no glans penis but only a fold of skin comparable to her 'crack' which was also composed of folds of skin. This led to a bisexual phantasy—he was both boy and girl.

362

responseSet.title: Alfred Robert Winterstein
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Alfred Robert Winterstein

Richard Sterba

Richard Sterba

Richard Sterba  

Dr. Alfred Robert Friedrich Freiherr von Winterstein was born on 25 September, 1885. After his academic studies at the Humanistic Gymnasium, he developed interest in the study of law, but soon changed to philosophy and the history of art. He became aware of Freud's new psychology when he read The Interpretation of Dreams as a young University student; this and other works of Freud determined the direction of his interest for the rest of his life. In 1908 he established contact with Professor Freud, and was invited to attend Freud's Saturday evening lectures at the Psychiatric Clinic. These alternated with seminars in which the attending students had to give a presentation on a subject of their choice. Winterstein chose as his subject an interpretation of Shakespeare's Hamlet. When he started with an apology for his many quotations from Shakespeare, Freud remarked ironically: 'Bring as many quotations as you can, they will certainly be the best part of your paper.' This somewhat discouraging remark had no lasting effect on Winterstein's interest in psychoanalysis. In 1910, the year in which the International Psycho-Analytic Society was founded, he joined the Vienna Psycho-Analytical Society, and remained a member till his death in May 1958.

In 1911 he gained his Ph.D. in psychology. After his graduation, he continued his psychological studies with Wundt in Leipzig and later in Zurich with Bleuler, Jung, and Monakow, adding some study of medicine at both places. In Zurich he was for a short while analysed by Jung. During the First World War he had to join the Armed Forces, and for more than four years his analytic activity was interrupted, but he continued it afterwards by completing his personal analysis with Dr. Eduard Hitschmann. He was active as a therapist and analytic scholar till 1938. When the Nazis forced the Vienna Psycho-Analytical Society to dissolve, Dr. Alfred Winterstein and Vorstand August Aichhorn were the only members who remained in Vienna.

responseSet.title: Reports Presented at the 20th Congress of Psychoanalysis in the Romance Languages: (Brussels, 15, 16, 17 February 1958)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reports Presented at the 20th Congress of Psychoanalysis in the Romance Languages

(Brussels, 15, 16, 17 February 1958)

S. Nacht et P.C. Racamier. The Psycho-analytic theory of delirium.

Introduction

Delirium, like neurosis, is the solution to psychic conflict. The distinction psychosis-neurosis is at the heart of psycho-analytic research. Criterion of transference is out-dated. Definition of psychotic anxiety. The heuristic importance of the primordial state of delirium, and of its history. The concept of projection is central. The content of the delirium is less important than its function in this presentation: this demands a detailed study of the functions of the ego of greater depth than that required for the neuroses.

History

Psychiatric research was not only descriptive and structural. Psycho-dynamic and psycho-genetic studies preceded psycho-analytic work along with ideas on the creative function, defence, and significance of delirium. But the organo-genetic position still allows nothing but an interpretation of content for psycho-analytic explanation.

For Freud at the onset, delirium was a defence against the loss of reality (1894). According to his work inspired by the homosexual conceptions of persecution delirium (Schreber) the aggressive factor takes first place, from Brunswick to M. Klein, who gives it an all-important rôle. It was Tausk who first pointed out the narcissistic essence of delirium and introduced the study of the primary states of the ego. From a wide survey of modern works, we refer to the critical analysis of the conception of the ego and the psychoses by Federn, the psychotic transference (Fromm-Reichmann), and the traumatic primitive reality (Séchehaye, Rosen). For the authors as for I. Macalpine and R. Hunter, the 'primum movens' is pre-objectal.

Psycho-analysis of delirious activity

Multiple functions of delirium. Basically, solution of conflict. The primordial state is the most significant psycho-pathologically. Language coded as in a dream, the delirium camouflages the pre-oedipal or oedipal conflict.

The deterioration of the sense of truth, basis of delirium, arises from the failure of the anaclitic relationship, the mother being the prototype and the mediatrix of object reality.

responseSet.title: Young Man Luther, a Study in Psychoanalysis and History: By Erik H. Erikson (New York:  Norton, 1958.  Pp. 288.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Young Man Luther, a Study in Psychoanalysis and History

By Erik H. Erikson (New York: Norton, 1958. Pp. 288.)

Martin Grotjahn M.D.

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn, M.D.
Beverly Hills, Calif.

Martin Grotjahn  

Erik Erikson's book on the 'Young Man Luther' is and is not a book on history and on religion and on philosophy. It is a psycho-analytic study of a great man as he developed in his time. It is a clinical study of Martin Luther's torturous search for ego-identity while on his way to greatness through a series of ego-crises. In his book Erikson gives a perfect illustration of a psycho-analytic contribution to existential philosophy.

The book is also a good example for teaching and understanding the relationship between a writer and his subject, between an analyst and his patient; to understand implies to a certain extent to be understood. Thus, this book is as personal as it is mature. It gives faith and hope in our time. Erikson shows something that should be self-evident; the problem of our time is not how to shoot at the moon, but man's search to find himself, his meaning, his place in this world and the essence of his being here. Man's fight for his soul, as Luther would have said, is still the most important task we are trying to accomplish in this life.

343

responseSet.title: Dr. Carl Müller-Braunschweig, Born 8.4.1881, Died 12.10.1958.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dr. Carl Müller-Braunschweig, Born 8.4.1881, Died 12.10.1958.

Gustav Scheunert

Gustav Scheunert

Gustav Scheunert  

By the death, after a long illness of Dr. Müller-Braunschweig, German analysts have lost not only their last surviving member from the period before World War I, but also a figure of importance in their history, for Müller-Braunschweig was an Honorary Member of the German Psycho-Analytical Association, and after the Second World War founded the Berlin Psycho-Analytical Institute.

He began his career as a student of philosophy under several famous philosophers, being most attracted by the teachings of Kant. This preference he retained throughout his life, lecturing as late as 1953–54 in Berlin on 'Freud and Kant: Psycho-Analysis and a Philosophy of Morals'. After taking his Ph.D, he published in 1908 a paper on 'The Method of Pure Ethics, especially Kantian Ethics, presented in the form of an Analysis of the Concept of a "Practical Law".'

His first contact with Freudian psycho-analysis brought him in the following year, 1909, to a turning-point in his life. He had been about to take up his duties as a University Lecturer in Philosophy, but renounced this post in favour of the study of analysis, and in the interest of his studies he spent, at the age of 31, several terms as a medical student. His training analysts were Karl Abraham and Hanns Sachs. He later lectured at the Berlin Institute, founded in 1920 by Eitingon, and in 1921 published a paper: 'Zur Moral' (Imago7, 1921). He also wrote: 'Fragen der Metapsychologie, (z.B.Desexualisation und Identifikation), issued both in English (Psychoanalytic Review, 13, 1926) and in German (Imago, 12, 1926).

His main interest lay in the systematics of psycho-analysis and its relation to philosophy. He helped to formulate the training rules for stutents which are still in force to-day; and in 1933 became chairman of the Training Committee.

Satisfying as Müller-Braunschweig's earlier years had been (in 1935 he was elected a member of the Executive of the International Assocition), the years that followed under the Nazi régime brought bitter disappointment. In 1936 the German Psycho-Analytical Association lost its independence and was compulsorily merged in the 'Reich Institute for Psychological Research'; in 1938 it was dissolved. Yet Müller-Braunschweig continued to promote psycho-analysis without compromise or distortion, and he was therefore barred from all teaching. Though he suffered deeply from this shackling of his intellectual activities, he tenaciously pursued his work in private, passing on to his patients the knowledge that had been passed on to him.

responseSet.title: Logic and Psychology: By Jean Piaget.  (New York:  Basic Books, 1957.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Logic and Psychology

By Jean Piaget. (New York: Basic Books, 1957.)

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard  

This slim little book, based on three lectures delivered at Manchester University in 1952, succeeds in its intention of serving as a real introduction to Piaget's methodology for arriving at his chronological assessments of a child's increasing capacity for conceptualization. He demonstrates his rationale as being an application of the 'algebra of logic'. Piaget expresses himself with that admirable but unequivocal conciseness which so often characterizes those whose aim is to bring orderliness to that which is overt. Within these limitations, as we know, he has discovered and formulated many important data on the cognitive and behavioural responses of babies and children to his excellently devised test situations.

With the aid of this compact, pocket-size book, one is admirably inducted into a quicker understanding of Piaget's guiding principles and terminology than could be achieved in any other way.

357

responseSet.title: Psychopathic Personalities: By Kurt Schneider.  Translated by M. W. Hamilton.  (London:  Cassell, 1958.  Pp. 163.  18 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychopathic Personalities

By Kurt Schneider. Translated by M. W. Hamilton. (London: Cassell, 1958. Pp. 163. 18 s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This well-known book was first published in 1923 and has recently appeared in the ninth edition, of which this is a translation. It will be welcome to many psychiatrists who have known it only from quotations. It demonstrates the gap between a purely descriptive approach and the various types of dynamic orientations current in the English-speaking countries. The scope of the book is really wider than the title appears to indicate. Professor Schneider has no use for the term neurosis, for semantic and philosophical reasons. His concept of illness is strictly organic. While he denies that there is such a thing as a neurosis he does not deny the existence of neurotics, whom he regards as psychopathic personalities with abnormal reactions to external events or to inner tensions or conflicts. Considering that he has stated his case for more than thirty-five years with great clarity and from positions of great authority, it is indeed remarkable how little research it has stimulated. Professor Schneider ignores psycho-analysis, and this gives his book an unreal and old-world quality. The translation is admirable.

360

responseSet.title: Gesticulation and Speech
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gesticulation and Speech

William Needles M.D.

William Needles

William Needles, M.D.
NEW YORK, N.Y.

William Needles  

Gesticulation as an accompaniment of speech is a phenomenon which the analyst observes daily in the course of his work. Patients on the couch vary considerably in the degree of motility they display. At one extreme is the exceedingly rigid individual who, once having assumed his characteristic posture, tends to maintain it indefinitely; at the other is the type who throughout the session thrashes the air with his hands as he speaks, since even vehement speech does not afford him an adequate outlet for his emotions. In this latter instance the lower limbs are usually uninvolved; rarely one observes wriggling movements of the feet. Like everything else that takes place in the analytic situation this language of the body also calls for explanation.

Gesticulation as an accompaniment of speech is, of course, not limited to the analytic chamber. The chest-thumping demagogue is a familiar enough figure. And in social groups one frequently observes similar manifestations in individuals whose aggressivity is ill-concealed. Hamlet's admonition to the players, not to saw the air too much, is a reminder from another era of the universality of the phenomenon.

As regards its prevalence, aside from the internal, psychological elements involved, cultural, national, and geographic factors seem to play an important rôle. Thus, gesticulation is more pronounced in Mediterranean peoples—who, in other ways, allow themselves more direct gratification of instinct—than in their Nordic counterparts. While in our own society, on the other hand, one sees many an angry young man who does not gesticulate because he has been 'properly' brought up.

The function of gesticulation as an adjuvant or reinforcement to speech is evident. But within this general framework certain variations may be distinguished. Thus, gesticulation has a relatively tame rôle when one person, in giving directions to another on how to get to a certain place, resorts to pointing; or when an angler demonstrates how big the one that got away was. In other instances the gesture carries a magical quality of omnipotence; the gesticulator disposes of a weighty problem by a flourish of the hand, like a king waving his sceptre. The outstretched hands may be used to emphasize a verbal appeal for the love object to come nearer. More violent forms of gesticulation are seen in individuals who are frustrated and angry and for whom speech proves inadequate to express their hostility and resentment.

responseSet.title: On Bullfighting and the Future of Tragedy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Bullfighting and the Future of Tragedy

Martin Grotjahn M.D.

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn, M.D.
BEVERLY HILLS, CALIFORNIA

Martin Grotjahn  

The psycho-analyst may feel unqualified to discuss the analytic interpretation of bullfighting. He may never have seen a bullfight, might not accept an invitation to do so, and may abhor the whole idea. If forced to witness such a spectacle he may fortify himself with large quantities of dramamine and sleep through the whole performance. The horrible aspects of the fight obscure any interest in the symbolization of the ritual. This perhaps explains the relative sparsity of analytic attempts to interpret the flesta (2), (4). An analyst finds it easier to identify with the attitude of conscientious objection as represented by Ferdinand the Bull (3).

The Psycho-History of the Bull as a Symbol

The ancient history of bullfighting shows that in historical reality something was dramatized which, in later times, became a psychological representation in the unconscious of modern man. This relationship between the historical past and the psychological present has always been of interest to psycho-analysis.

Historically speaking, the cow is probably older than the bull. The cow was worshipped as the representation of the milk-giving good mother in many religions, as for instance in early Egyptian cults or in Hinduism to this day. The bull was introduced into the picture as the primal mother's phallic consort, and may have been her son, as was Horus, the son of Isis. The original bullfighting or bull grappling, known to us best from the paintings found in Crete, was a kind of gymnastic dance in which girls participated. The idea was to vault over the back of the bull while holding on to his horns, perhaps imitating, symbolically, the salto mortale, or the course of the moon or the sun. The emblem of the moon (or of the sun) was placed between the horns of the cow or of the bull. The horns also symbolized the crescent moon, another source of magic power (1).

In other rites, the bull was sacrificed as the symbol of the sacred king who was ritually killed after his thirteen months of kingship. His body was dismembered, the parts were thrown over the fields in order to fertilize mother Earth, as in the cult of Osiris, and to safeguard the harvest of the coming year. His penis was buried with special rituals and often embalmed for later use when the earth came to life again. The life and death of the king or the bull repeated the cycle of the year. The king, the god of Spring, or the bull had to die at the height of his strength in order to insure the fertility of mother Earth.

responseSet.title: Psycho-Analysis and its Relation to Certain Other Fields of Natural Science
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psycho-Analysis and its Relation to Certain Other Fields of Natural Science

Jeanne Lampl-De Groot

Jeanne Lampl-De Groot

Jeanne Lampl-De Groot, Dr.
AMSTERDAM

Jeanne Lampl-De Groot  

In this paper I shall endeavour to set forth some ideas on psycho-analytic theory, regarded in the light of findings in other branches of science. Freud developed his conception of the 'supplementary series' (Ergänzungsreihe), in the early stages of his study of neuroses. It is said that neuroses emerge from an interplay of innate dispositional factors and experiences, due to the environment, undergone during a lifetime. This conception may be extended to mental development in general.

We already know a great deal about the processes of development from infancy to the adult stage; an increasing tendency towards tracing the earlier phases, to study the very young child and the newborn infant may be observed in recent psycho-analytic research. In the course of these endeavours it is inevitable that we come up against innate factors (Anlage). Several psycho-analysts, for instance Kubie, Massermann, Szekely, Ostow, Brun, and many others, have therefore in recent years turned their attention to predispositional factors. It is my impression that there are three particular fields of science which demand our special attention:—

i. Modern cerebral anatomy and physiology, which assist us in achieving a better understanding of the physical basis of mind;

ii. Modern physics and mechanics, which stimulate our knowledge of the way in which the brain functions;

iii. Animal psychology, especially ethology, as this teaches us a great deal about instinctive behaviour and its development from the congenital mechanisms of the various types of animal life.

In these branches of science I am myself a layman, and am therefore able only to develop a few tentative trains of thought which doubtless will require verification, revision, or both.

We all know that modern neuro-anatomy, neuro-physiology, and neuro-surgery have taught us a variety of things about the construction of the central nervous system and its physical activities.

We know that the human brain contains approximately 10, 000, 000, 000 neurones—nerve cells—and countless nerve fibres as well as afferent and efferent fibre tracts, which conduct stimuli into the brain and out of it. There are also the associative tracts which connect the different parts of the cortex. We know that the stimuli which activate the sense centres are carried on to other parts of the cortex. The same is the case with the inner stimuli, which reach the brain by means of the blood supply and the nervous pathways. Scientists tell us that the living brain contains electric potentials which are constantly at work and are able to vary their speed and rhythm: Adrian, for instance, says: 'There is constant transformation of energy in the brain'. We have long been acquainted with the manner in which reflexes in the central nervous system operate (Pavlov); today, however, more particular research is being done into the origin and the working method of the so-called higher psychic functions, such as consciousness, thought, feeling, etc. Knowledge of these matters has been greatly stimulated by modern physics and mechanics, and especially by what is now known as cybernetics, a term which must be coupled with the name of Norbert Wiener.1 I will discuss this presently, but first would like to point out that, notwithstanding this imposing increase of knowledge, one constantly encounters among leading neuro-physiologists and neuro-anatomists the view that what actually takes place in the brain during the exercise of the higher functions is not known. Adrian, for instance, says: 'The real trouble comes from the feeling that there may be an important part of the picture which can never be fitted in, however long we may work at it'. Also: 'It is a far cry from the nerve cell or even the cerebral hemisphere to the thoughts and desires of mankind'. Sherrington says: 'Aristotle, 2000 years ago, was asking how the mind is attached to the body; we are asking that question still'.

responseSet.title: On Identification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Identification

Roy Grinker M.D.

Roy Grinker

Roy Grinker  

Dr. Roy R. Grinker, M.D., Chicago, comments:

Drs. Engel and Reichsman are correct in assuming that I was referring to a memorandum prepared by Dr. Engel and circulated among his colleagues. I had overestimated in reading their report the proof which I now see is only an hypothesis. They are to be congratulated for not accepting the finality of their statements and are planning to continue their most excellent and stimulating work. It is very rare in these days to find authors who reject a generous evaluation of their work—an evaluation which bespeaks my confidence in their scientific activities. It is my belief that their letter published above adds a great deal to an understanding of the complexities of the problems concerned with identification.

61

[[RunningHead]]

Chronic Schizophrenia

By Thomas Freeman, John L. Cameron, and Andrew McGhie. Preface by Anna Freud. Foreword by T. Ferguson Rodger. (London: Tavistock Publications. Pp. 158. 21 s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

Most psycho-analytical contributions to the problem of schizophrenia have come from the study and treatment of individual patients by the psycho-analytical method, which, however, had in many cases to be modified. The large majority of the patients reported on had been treated in private practice and did not as a rule deteriorate or become institutionalized. Some of them were 'ambulatory' schizophrenics whose hold on reality had not suffered severely. The authors of this book have applied themselves to the study and treatment of chronic institutionalized schizophrenics with typical behaviour and thought disorder. Though therapeutically less rewarding than acute cases, these patients are excellent subjects for the study of what is most characteristic in schizophrenia.

67

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Case Studies in Childhood Emotional Disabilities, Vol. 2

Edited by George E. Gardner. (New York: American Orthopsychiatric Association, 1956. Pp. 368. $5.00.)

James Anthony

James Anthony

James Anthony  

This is the second volume in a series that seems destined to reproduce itself ad infinitum. I see two reasons for this: in the first place, the meetings of the American Orthopsychiatric Association have become hardy annuals that should continue indefinitely to provide the type of case study included in this collection; and secondly, there appears to be, judging by the demand for Volume 1, a real need for model presentations of this nature for use in clinic teaching programmes.

The traditional clinic method in America, as Dr. Gardner points out in his preface, has a three-pronged approach to the case. The psychiatrist, the psychologist, and the social worker combine to illuminate different aspects of the patient's problem. This at least was the original intention. Gradually, the psychiatric, the psychological, and the social assessments began to encroach on one another, and the triple division of function represented in 'the varidisciplinary approach' as Dr. Gardner terms it (for want of a better name, I presume), was fused into a single operation. Everyone became a therapist and everyone talked the language of Freud. The account given by the social worker differed from that of the psychiatrist only in its concern with a different age range, and not because the disciplines were essentially different. The kudos of being the all-powerful therapist led everyone to want to be just that. To do else was to do less.

The present volume covers a wide variety of disorders ranging over neurosis, psychosis, and psychopathy. Encopresis, which has become one of the more fashionable ailments of the modern disturbed child, is once again to the forefront. The papers are skilfully and attractively presented. If at the conference they were all as good as these, it must have been a joy to attend. I suspect, however, that Dr. Gardner has skimmed the milk for the cream. The book, as it stands, is such an excellent guide to American clinic practice as constituted today that one is led to look beyond it to the philosophy underlying the techniques described. One notices at once on reading through the book that the case histories tend to pall after a while, in marked contrast to those described in the purely psycho-analytic literature. It does seem that applied psycho-analysis (which is just what this is) begins to sound glib and facile with repetition. The feeling of continuity that comes with daily working through of a case is absent, and the therapist seems only too liable to fall back on mechanical reconstructions of the Oedipus formula. If this sounds a little disloyal to one's own side, I will hasten to add that I would rather read the next fifteen volumes in this series than one page of the sterile effusions that emanate from some of the 'psychobiological' clinics whose vague 'minimal brain damage' aetiologies and 'manipulative' therapies scarcely serve to differentiate one child from another. With analytically-oriented therapy, there is a dynamic feeling in the air, and one is constantly aware that the unconscious is just around the corner to stir things up.

Perceptual Processes and Mental Illness

By H. J. Eysenck, Ph.D., G. W. Granger, Ph.D., and J. C. Brengelmann, M.D., Ph.D. (New York: Basic Books, 1957. $3.75.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

The authors assert that diagnosis based upon ordinary psychiatric interviewing is notoriously inaccurate, and so they set out to devise certain tests and to adapt tests which have been used somewhat differently in order to have at hand a relatively infallible method of diagnosis of psychiatric illness and in particular to differentiate between normal people, neurotics, and psychotics. The results turn out to be even more favourable than the authors expected. By such methods they hope to eliminate the necessity for unscientific and intuitive approaches upon which they pour disdain, albeit relatively gently. The only statement which, if taken seriously, would put every psycho-analyst up in arms is in the last paragraph of the book.

69

[[RunningHead]]

Remotivating the Mental Patient

By Otto von Mering and Stanley H. King. (New York: Russell Sage Foundation, 1957. Pp. 216. $3.00.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

In Western Europe and in the United States of America there has been a great change in attitude towards the long-stay mental hospital patient since the Second World War. Formerly, although there may well have been a good deal of custodial kindness, the problem of personal and social rehabilitation had scarcely been faced at all. This book, an American product, consists of chapters which describe the different methods of tackling the problem which appropriately is called remotivation, as it consists of something more than rehabilitation, more than welfare, but can be summarized as the subjecting of a group of patients to modified and appropriate forms of social 'pressure cooking' within the mental hospital. The sort of work which is described by the authors is being carried out in many mental hospitals in the United Kingdom and Western Europe as well as in America, and it is interesting to see how close is the resemblance between the facets of the therapeutic community in the several countries where such projects are now in operation.

Hitherto patients who suffer from personal or social failure of a gross kind have been herded into large wards, and in this impersonal milieu further regression has been facilitated, so that there has been either gross withdrawal or a reduction to the lowest common denominator of performance and adaptation. This process of human degradation can be reversed, so that a vicious spiral of pessimism and decline, of devaluation and degeneration can be converted into a favourable spiral of progress and revaluation which often continues to the point of fitness for discharge into the outside community. Basically the principles are simple, and consist of enlightened love and care given by trained personnel who do not cordon themselves off from the patient but are prepared to involve themselves in the project of remotivation. At the same time, less ill patients are encouraged to take an active part in rehabilitating and remotivating more ill ones, so that both sets of patients benefit.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Straight to the Heart

A Personal Account of Thoughts and Feelings while undergoing Heart Surgery. By George Lawton. With a special supplement by Ethel Lawton. (New York: International Universities Press, Inc., 1956. Pp. 371. $5.00.)

James Taylor

James Taylor

James Taylor  

This is a truthful account of a terrible experience. The author underwent heart surgery for aortic stenosis, a condition of a heart valve which leads to increasing breathlessness and water-logging of the whole body. He describes his feelings and physical state before and after the operation. He is now well, and suffers from very little physical disability, and is able to continue his work as a lay psycho-analyst. To someone who has suffered from a heart affection this book makes difficult reading. Constantly one finds oneself saying 'Did I really behave as badly as that?' which I feel is testimony to the honesty of the writer, his efforts to remember and put everything down, and the vividness of his writing. His description of the recovery period and its confusional state is exceptionally good. Here we have all the self-deceits, ruthless egotism, paranoid fears, sentimentality, and in particular sense of unreality which are characteristic of the heart patient. Here too we have the longing to know the truth which suddenly gives way to the refusal of truth when the truth gets too close. It seems hard to tax the author with failing to rise to the height of his subject, but it is unfortunately true that he quite honestly shows that he never consciously faced death. It would have been a finer and better book if he had been able to do so. The supplement by his wife gives her account of the trouble they went through together and shows what a tower of strength she must have been to him.

71

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Augusta Alpert, Ph.D., Peter B. Neubauer, M.D., Annemarie P. Weil, M.D. (New York). 'Unusual Variations in Drive Endowment.' Pp. 125–163.

This study describes unusual variations in drive equipment in three children in terms of quantity of drive energy, innately or maturationally determined; having regard to the quantitative balance between initial libidinal and aggressive drives; and to the distribution of both energies over the libidinal zones. Distribution is also described in terms of externalization of drive energy.

Case 1 combined hyperlibidinal and hypoaggressive drive energies, Case 2 hyperaggressive and adequate libidinal drives, and Case 3 hypolibidinal and hypoaggressive energies. These variants were followed in their bearing on the cathexis of the external world as well as on ego development. In Case 1, localization of both drives in the oral zone from birth and insufficient externalization reduced the aggressive admixture in all ego functions and seriously limited aggressive discharge. In Case 2, a surplus of aggressive drive energies discharged in motility prevented sufficient focusing of the external world. In Case 3, deficient drive quantity associated with insufficient externalization delayed cathexis of the external world.

The interaction of drive endowment with maturational sequences and ego achievements was studied and significant constellations found in timing and quality of achievement. Variations in quantity, balance, and distribution of drives are assumed to have significant bearing on the colouring of infantile neurosis, character disorder, and deeper pathology.

1Published by International Universities Press, New York, and Imago Publishing Co., London.74

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 11, 195611

Erna Furman. (Cleveland). 'An Ego Disturbance in a Young Child.' Pp. 312–335.

This is an account of the analysis of a 3-year-old child unable to relate to adults or children, or comprehend the meaning of everyday events around her. Withdrawn attitudes alternated with senseless hyperactivity and meaningless excited talking. Poor motor control combined with regressive sexual and aggressive behaviour. By careful and systematic interpretation of defences against her oral-phallic wishes and anxieties, it became possible to elucidate the extent to which the child's oral proclivities, complementary character difficulties in the parents, and an unsuspected seduction to fellatio had contributed to the disturbance. The analysis succeeded in freeing her sociability, and establishing her capacity to learn at age level. Considerable development in superego was also effected.

1Published by International Universities Press, New York, and Imago Publishing Co., London.76

responseSet.title: A Note on the Splitting of Interpretations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Note on the Splitting of Interpretations

James Taylor

James Taylor

James N. Taylor, Dr.
LONDON

James Taylor  

The patient who has been listening to the voice or inflections of voice of the analyst rather than to the matter of his interpretations is familiar to us all. I should like to draw attention to what one might call a refinement of this mechanism of defence.

Mrs. A. B. is in her late forties. She is what one may call an old war horse of analysis. She was for several years in analysis until her analyst left to go to another country. She bears an unceasing grudge against the analyst for this desertion and for other reasons as well. During her first analysis and following it she took up work of a psychotherapeutic nature without any recognized qualifications and she earns her living successfully. Previous to this she had a career as an actress.

At times, she lets drop remarkable statements such as: (i) 'I would rather die than say that' (this was said apparently without any relevance to material at the time and has remained totally unexplained; (ii) 'I am an opera singer' (in reference to a sketch which she used to perform—a skit on opera singers). Tackled about this, she readily admitted with one part of herself that she was not an opera singer, but firmly fixed beneath this was the absolute conviction that she was and could be; (iii) On the subject of dependence on me she said, 'If you are necessary to me, I am just as much necessary to you.' This had arisen out of an exhaustive review of her attitude to her own psychotherapeutic patients, in which she had acknowledged momentarily that her attitude of identification with her patients and her use of them for her own personal therapeutic purposes was to say the least of it not quite what ought to happen.

Recently she has taken on two new patients—one a man who at the second interview told her that his sister was in Broadmoor for infanticide. This was said apparently without any feeling and the 'infanticide' turned out to be child murder of children aged five and three. Mrs. A. B. took this quite calmly but on second thoughts began to have cold feet about it. She continues to see him and recounted with a laugh that the patient had been attending Maudsley Hospital for some time and that the psychiatrist there, who is well known, had expressed disparaging ideas about psychotherapy. I asked, 'Why the laugh?' and she replied, 'I would give anything to know.' I replied, 'Would you really—it shouldn't be too difficult, you know, ' and eventually an attitude of mind appeared in Mrs. A. B. in which analytic procedures are felt to be fundamentally futile. Along with this feeling was also the awareness that people who disparage psycho-analysis may be frightened of it. (Mrs. A.B.'s room is a small one and she sits alongside the couch and facing the opposite way to the patient. The patient therefore can see her face all the time. She has to turn her head to look at the patient.) I give this fact about her behaviour with a patient because I think that it illustrates what I should like to call her saving grace—her 'guying' of everything, in this case the analyst. It also neatly sums up her position.

responseSet.title: La Psychanalyse Et Les Ulcères Gastro-Duodénaux: By Dr. Angel Garma.  (Bibliothèque de Psychanalyse, Presses Universitaires de France, 1957.  Pp. 179.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La Psychanalyse Et Les Ulcères Gastro-Duodénaux

By Dr. Angel Garma. (Bibliothèque de Psychanalyse, Presses Universitaires de France, 1957. Pp. 179.)

Lilla Veszy-Wagner

Lilla Veszy-Wagner

Lilla Veszy-Wagner  

This valuable work summarizes the former findings and theories of Dr. Garma about the psycho-genesis and treatment of peptic ulcer patients (in this Journal: 31, 1950, 53, and 34, 1953, 102) completed by the presentation of seventeen highly instructive cases. Gastrectomy and vagotomy do not seem to eliminate the troubles of such patients. Either the complains persist or arterial hypertension ensues instead, or else their potency suffers by the traumatic effect of the operation. Dr. Garma's contention is that it is not the latent femininity of the patient (Draper), or his tendencies to passivity (Alexander), or his masochism (Bergler) which are primarily responsible for this illness, but that peptic ulcers represent inner aggressions expressed in the digestive tract, being the remorse caused by an unjust and severe superego. They are (re)activated by actual external aggressions (mainly genital frustrations) caused on the part of the patient's environment on which the patient is dependent. The internalized bad mother acts as harmful food. The orally regressed patient displaces his conflict to the digestive tract, where his hostility and anxiety struggle to meet the bad mother. This is usually exacerbated by the patient's indulging in a too strenuous activity which does not allow for an adequate emotional discharge. The future ulcer patients are usually children whose mothers, while good milk suppliers, held back their babies too long from eating meat; moreover, having spurred them to activity, spurned their oedipal desires.

An (oedipal) prohibition of digesting certain foods (e.g. meat) is present, besides the desire for the same in order to prove the capability to 'digest' the bad mother. The digestive tract of these patients expresses both their aggressive wishes to bite and the punishment for it. Their behaviour in love life is usually brusque, and they seek 'digestive orgasm' instead of genital. Also they seek 'dangerous' food on which to put their maternally imposed activity to work and with which they want to destroy the negative qualities they resent in their mothers.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Your Family

By Alexander Grinstein, M.D., and Edith Sterba, Ph.D. (New York: Random House, 1957. Pp. 312. $3.95.)

Hilda Abraham

Hilda Abraham

Hilda Abraham  

This book is not written for psychiatrists or psycho-analysts but to introduce psycho-analytical theories

346

to an intelligent lay public. As such it takes a worthy place in a long row of publications with the same or a more limited aim. It seems typical not only for our science but for most disciplines that some workers wish only to do research or other specialized work, whilst others want to interest a wide public in the work to which they are dedicated. Freud himself must have felt this when he published his Psychopathology of Everyday Life. Abraham wrote an article on 'The Cultural Significance of Psycho-Analysis' in a monthly magazine read by the German intelligentsia, and Federn and Meng published Das Psycho-Analytische Volksbuch with the same motive, to name only a few.

More limited is the aim of those authors who deal with certain aspects of our work and present them to special groups. Thus Anna Freud wrote Psycho-Analysis for Teachers, Edith Buxbaum Your Child Makes Sense, addressing herself to parents, and the present book, on a far more ambitious scale, is addressed to the family. It tries to deal with any question or problem arising for courting couples, during engagement, and during marriage. The planning and timing of conception and the problems of pregnancy are discussed, and the authors try to simplify their difficult task by introducing the concept of the 'Family Ego' to describe 'the functioning of the family as a psychological unity, as well as the awareness the family has of its own group identity …' This is a useful term, and it is suggested that pregnancy, childbirth, and the size of the family should be related to the Family Ego's growing strength and dependability.

The authors show imagination and sensitivity in describing a multitude of possible problems, snags, and pitfalls, but without giving examples suggesting that only a miracle will prevent things from going wrong at some or every turn. To the reviewer, one of the best chapters seems to be that on 'Developing Controls'.

responseSet.title: The Unconscious: A Conceptual Analysis.  By A. C. MacIntyre, Ph.D.  (London:  Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1958.  Pp. 100.  11s. 6d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Unconscious

A Conceptual Analysis. By A. C. MacIntyre, Ph.D. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1958. Pp. 100. 11s. 6d.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The author, who is Lecturer in Philosophy at Leeds University, has in this essay attempted a logical analysis of the concept of the unconscious. He finds Freud 'not over-careful about terminology'. He refers to Freud's spurious attempt to present a psychology based on neurophysiology. Like others before him, he notes that Freud borrowed from neurology a number of terms by which he described psychological mechanisms.

The concept of the unconscious, according to the author, stands and falls with that of repression, which he regards as unproven. Freud used the term 'the unconscious' in a new meaning which in the author's view derived from neurophysiology. It is, in his opinion, no more than an explanatory concept, like the notions of the ether or the electron. In treating unconscious motives both as purposes and causes, Freud was guilty of confusion. His great achievement lay not in the explanation of abnormal behaviour but in its redescription. It is not quite easy to follow the author in these arguments. He is at a disadvantage in not being a biologist, for whom cause and purpose of behaviour are more intimately related than they are to the logician. His use of the term 'description' is different from that of the scientist. It appears that he is referring to what Jaspers called the difference between 'verstehen', i.e. to discern understandable relationships between phenomena, and 'erklären', i.e. to answer the question why a certain event happened. If this distinction was as clear and clean as it seems to the philosopher, Freud's achievement would be largely in the area of 'verstehen'. Freud is criticized for not having distinguished motives and causes. This confusion the author believes to be rooted in the therapeutic situation. Wittgenstein is reported as having expressed the view that Freud's genius was notable in his 'descriptive' work rather than in his explanatory concepts. Wittgenstein apparently included interpretation in his concept of description. 'Freud was decoding rather than explaining.' However, the author finds Freud's 'creative untidiness' far more fertile than the precision of many others. In the concluding chapter he discusses the question of the validation of psycho-analytic hypotheses. He is not hopeful that this will be possible. He expects that if and when Freud's theories are sifted 'the unconscious as an entity' and area of the mind will disappear. He regards it 'as a piece of metaphysics, an attempt at a more-than-scientific unification of concepts'. He does not, however, object to the concept of 'unconscious' being a quality of mental activities. In this sense the concept was used before Freud.

responseSet.title: Personality in Young Children: Lois B. Murphy and Associates.  Vol. I.  'Methods for the Study of Personality in Young Children.'  Vol. II.  'Colin—A Normal Child.'  (New York:  Basic Books.  Pp. 424 and 267.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Personality in Young Children

Lois B. Murphy and Associates. Vol. I. 'Methods for the Study of Personality in Young Children.' Vol. II. 'Colin—A Normal Child.' (New York: Basic Books. Pp. 424 and 267.)

James Anthony

James Anthony

James Anthony  

'Early in life', says Lawrence Frank, in his foreword to these two volumes, 'children realize that it is not wise or safe to reveal what they think, how they feel, their wishes and daydreams, their sometimes disturbing fantasies.' This leads in time to a hidden preconscious and an unknown unconscious world of thought which can eventually be disclosed to the outside observer only through the means of special techniques. Those who study the child or treat him are preoccupied with this problem of getting across to such inaccessible regions of his mind. Child analysts have evolved a special play technique to do so, psychologists projective techniques, and Piaget has used a special method of clinical interrogation to reach the preconscious ideation of children. All have found that the indirect way in, the longest way round, is the shortest way in.

In these two volumes the author is very much concerned with eliciting the child's personal view of the world. This is the view that Piaget has been investigating over the past thirty years, and so it is rather surprising that the author finds no place for him either in her pages or in her index. This omission may be due to the fact that her intention is less to give the child's personal view of the world than to record a description and analysis of his personality development. Piaget has never shown any interest in the personality of his young subjects. Mrs. Murphy's effort, therefore, is to see the child concretely and eclectically and to understand what he is experiencing and what his behaviour means to him as well as to other children and adults.

348

responseSet.title: Perspectives in Personality Theory: Edited by Henry P. David and Helmut von Bracken.  (London:  Tavistock Publications, 1957.  Pp. 435.  38 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Perspectives in Personality Theory

Edited by Henry P. David and Helmut von Bracken. (London: Tavistock Publications, 1957. Pp. 435. 38 s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This is a useful collection of contributions from students of personality working in Britain, the United States and Canada, West Germany, Belgium, Holland, Switzerland, France, and Italy. Psycho-analytic theory is represented by Else Frenkel-Brunswick. The Symposium is opened by

357

an admirably balanced article by Gordon Allport, who points out that there is a basic difference in philosophical assumptions regarding the nature of mental life, the Lockean tradition being dominant in Britain and America, the Leibnizian and Kantian in Europe. Much of the basic ideas found in Anglo-American theories of personality is borrowed from continental sources, while Anglo-American research contributes much to the goal of precision and adds the emphasis on the environment. These observations are illustrated by the twenty-two contributions making up this interesting volume.

358

responseSet.documentRefHTML: Stengel, E. (1959) The Dynamics of Anxiety and Hysteria: By H. J. Eysenck. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. 311. 32 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 40:358
responseSet.documentRefXML: Stengel, E. (1959) The Dynamics of Anxiety and Hysteria: By H. J. Eysenck. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. 311. 32 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 40:358
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195940358BThe Dynamics of Anxiety and HysteriaBy H. J. Eysenck. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. 311. 32 s.)E.Stengel
responseSet.title: The Dynamics of Anxiety and Hysteria: By H. J. Eysenck.  (London:  Routledge, 1957.  Pp. 311.  32 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dynamics of Anxiety and Hysteria

By H. J. Eysenck. (London: Routledge, 1957. Pp. 311. 32 s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The author attempts to use learning theory and Pavlovian principles for the elucidation of certain neurotic manifestations. He is all for mathematical precision, but he does not seem to care what he is precise about. The title is puzzling. It is not clear whether the author means neurotic anxiety, which is frequently present in hysterics, or anxiety neurosis without hysterical symptoms, or the anxiety of the obsessional, etc. The reader will search in vain for a clarification of these questions. It is one of the inconsistencies of Eysenck's work that while he is highly critical of the vagueness of the psychiatrist's diagnostic concepts, he treats them, when it suits him, as if they had the reality of a piece of muscle or of an inanimate object to be analysed in the laboratory. He never tells us what kind of hysterics he has experimented on, or what kind of neurotic anxiety he was dealing with. Presumably, the patients were diagnosed at the Maudsley Hospital, where diagnostic criteria and language vary greatly. Some years ago this reviewer characterized Eysenck's work as the scrupulous handling of material collected without sufficient scrupulosity. It now seems that this opinion was rather rash. He (the reviewer), was then greatly impressed by the apparent scientific rigour of the author's methods of which he (the reviewer) understood nothing. While this deplorable ignorance still persists, Eysenck's methods, especially their mathematical and statistical aspects, have been heavily criticized for being far from impeccable; according to expert critics the handling of the material has not been sufficiently scrupulous either. All this is very confusing and disappointing for the clinician, who tends to be too readily impressed by the symbols of pure science. The best he can do in such a case is to suspend judgement and to hope that some stimulus will nevertheless come from this work.

358

responseSet.title: Psychotherapy and Culture Conflict: By Georgene Seward, Ph.D.  With case studies by Judd Marmor, M.D.  (New York:  Ronald Press, 1956.  Pp. ix + 299.  $6.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy and Culture Conflict

By Georgene Seward, Ph.D. With case studies by Judd Marmor, M.D. (New York: Ronald Press, 1956. Pp. ix + 299. $6.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

Dr. Seward, a psychologist, writes about the effect of culture upon personality, and upon the difficulties which occur when an individual is confronted with a clash of cultural values, in particular because his own early acculturation has produced in him an adjustment with a certain degree of inflexibility. Dr. Seward illustrates his argument with extracts from the case histories of some of the patients of Dr. Judd Marmor. Psycho-analytic concepts are used.

The main thesis is that though there is a basic instinctual root to personality, its stem, flower, and fruit vary from culture to culture in accordance with the environmental atmosphere in which and by which that root has been nourished. Moreover, the norms which are deemed desirable vary from one ecological setting to another, so that what in one culture would be called paranoid, in another would pass for normal. Four interesting case studies are recounted. The areas of cultural conflict chosen for discussion are varied, and include 'color and conflict', 'Jews between two worlds', 'women as a minority', 'social class and personality'. These type situations are used in such a way that the reader can choose other examples for himself, can apply the technique of study of culture conflict, and reach an understanding of the particular problem in hand.

The authors bring convincing evidence that psychotherapy will at best be less effective than it should be, and at worst not effective at all, if cultural factors are not given due weight and consideration in the treatment of patients who are displaced from the cultural or social milieu in which they developed during their early years.

359
