responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.: Heinreich Racker.  'Counterresistance and Interpretation.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2.

Heinreich Racker. 'Counterresistance and Interpretation.'

Sometimes analysts refrain from making interpretations which they fear might disturb the patient. Racker believes that this behaviour on the analyst's part is due to anxiety within himself—a counter-resistance. Overcoming these counter-resistances which are the counterparts of resistances within the patient leads to the production of new material.

361

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Peter Blos, Ph.D. (New York).  'Preoedipal Factors in the Etiology of Female Delinquency.'  Pp. 229–249.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Peter Blos, Ph.D. (New York). 'Preoedipal Factors in the Etiology of Female Delinquency.' Pp. 229–249.

The identity of delinquency and perversion outstandingly corresponds with the clinical picture of female delinquency while it constitutes only one special variant in the more diverse etiology of male delinquency. The adolescent girl in turning away from the mother represses instinctual drives which were intimately related to her bodily care, the total scope of pregenitality. The adolescent boy who returns to pregenital drive satisfactions during transient regressive episodes is still in relative consonance with his progressive sex-appropriate development or certainly not in any fatal opposition to it.

There are two types of female delinquents: one has regressed to the pre-oedipal mother and the other clings defensively to a foothold on the oedipal stage. Offences at each of these levels are essentially different in dynamics and structure. Of the latter type, the author states that in addition to experiencing oedipal defeat at a father's hands, the girl identifies with a mother who is dissatisfied with her husband, shares her disappointment, and thus forges a destructive and indestructible relation with her. The delinquent behaviour is motivated by the need for the constant possession of a partner who serves her to surmount in phantasy an oedipal impasse and to take revenge on the mother who had hated and ridiculed the father. In these girls there is no wish for a baby and, if one occurs, it is an act of revenge or competition. In the girl who has failed entirely to liberate herself from the mother, the male only serves to gratify insatiable oral needs; pseudosexuality is a defence against regressive pulls which are also represented in obsessive wishes for a child.

It seems that a special kind of interaction must be at work between parent and child to prevent the progressive establishment of an independent life in the child—a sadomasochistic pattern which has permeated the instinctual life and adversely affected ego development. Primal ambivalence rooted in the biting stage of oral organization constitutes its nucleus.

responseSet.title: Body Image and Archaic Thinking
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Body Image and Archaic Thinking11

Andrew Peto M.D.

Andrew Peto

Andrew Peto, M.D.
NEW YORK

Andrew Peto  

I

I wish to discuss in this paper a particular type of transference phenomenon which shows features of deepest regression. Similar regressions have been described by several authors in the past twenty years (I refer here in particular to Isakower's observations (11) and to Lewin's studies (14) and have been observed or adumbrated in papers dealing with schizophrenia and fetishism (Bak, Greenacre and Mahler) (1), (6), (16).

In recent years I have had under my treatment several patients who showed the transference phenomena I am going to describe. They were young people between 15 and 25, with high intelligence ratings and (with one exception) excellent high school and university records. They had in their history 'breakdowns' which were diagnosed by academic psychiatrists in most cases as psychotic episodes. The precipitating causes of the 'breakdown's were the usual factors we find in juvenile psychotic episodes or in grave personality disturbances: intellectual stress, separation of parents, a physical illness of moderate severity, etc. The patients were referred to me after ECT, narco-analysis, prolonged insulin treatment with the aim of causing hypoglycemic precoma had proved to be ineffective. Most of them went through some kind of so-called psychotherapy.

The anamnestic data or the course of the analysis revealed in most cases at least one period of prolonged severely abnormal behaviour in childhood, which may have manifested itself as (i) grave depression and inertia in the first or second year of life; (ii) withdrawals of several months' duration, probably accompanied by hallucinations around the climax or passing of the oedipus conflict; (iii) confusional states with temper tantrums, sleeplessness, periods of extreme phobic reactions.

The transference phenomena under discussion appeared in the majority of cases during the second or third year of the analysis. My impression was that, by and large, the greater the ego's relative integrative function, the later the transference took this course. In one male patient they came to the fore towards the end of the third year; in a rapidly deteriorating paranoic schizophrenic boy of 15 they manifested themselves—though for a very short period only—in the third month of his treatment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Selected Contributions to Psycho-Analysis

By John Rickman. International Psycho-Analytical Library, No. 52. (London: Hogarth Press and Institute of Psycho-Analysis. Pp. 411. 1957. 30s. net.)

W. Ronald Fairbairn

W. Ronald Fairbairn

W. Ronald Fairbairn  

The lamented death of John Rickman on 1 July, 1951, at the age of 60, deprived the psycho-analytical movement in Great Britain not only of one who had played an important part in the foundation and organization of those institutions which preside over the teaching and practice of psycho-analysis in that country, but also of one whose sprite-like, if perhaps at times somewhat erratic, genius never failed to add colour and interest to the meetings of the British Psycho-Analytical Society and the councils of the Institute of Psycho-Analysis. The quality of his genius being what it was, the intellectual reputation which he established among his contemporaries was not built upon the painstaking elaboration on his part of any system of original ideas at all comparable to that which has earned special recognition for the work of his colleague, Melanie Klein. It was based rather upon his penetrating flashes of intellectual insight and rapier-thrusts of critical judgment, which were such as to preclude any intellectual stagnation or complacency among those exposed to his influence. His own originality thus functioned in no small measure as a means of provoking and encouraging original thought in others. Fortunately, ample opportunity for the exercise of such a function was provided not only at scientific meetings and in private conversation, but also in the course of his editorial duties, to which so much of his time was devoted, particularly during his tenure of the editorship of the British Journal of Medical Psychology between 1935 and 1948. In general it may be said that the influence which he exercised within the field of psycho-analytical thought was mediated by his personal influence rather than by his published writings, which, for the most part, assumed the form of occasional contributions to the journals; and for this reason alone it seems doubtful whether a permanent record of his name will be left in the history of psycho-analytical thought. The appearance of the present volume as No. 52 in the series constituting the International Psycho-Analytical Library should, however, serve to ensure for his contributions an established place in psycho-analytical literature.

responseSet.title: On Identification
responseSet.authorMast: George L. Engel and  Franz Reichsman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Identification

George Engel M.D. and Franz Reichsman M.D.

Franz Reichsman

George L. Engel, M.D.
Professor of Psychiatry and Associate Professor of Medicine.
Franz Reichsman, M.D.
Assistant Professor of Medicine and Psychiatry.

Franz Reichsman  

SIR,

In the paper 'On Identification', Grinker (1) makes the following statement:

Engel and Reichsman ("Incorporation, Introjection and Identification.' Engel, G. L. and Reichsman, F. Unpublished material) have made a major contribution to the problem of identification in their psychosomatic studies of Monica, an infant with an oesophageal atresia and a gastrostomy. During the earliest months of life, when objects were not differentiated from self and mental representations were not yet formed, the infant was only an incorporative being. At this time acid secretion in the stomach occurred mainly in relationship to the feeding-wakeful state. Later, object- and self-representations became differentiated, and Monica's stomach secreted acid either in response to food or when in the proximity of human beings with whom she had a pleasurable transaction. She seemed to introject these objects psychologically, using an alimentary model, as she developed mental representations of them. After self- and object-representations were clearly discriminated, qualities of the real object of their representations were acquired by Monica without alteration of gastric secretion. This, Engel and Reichsman call identification. Under conditions of stress, such as in the transference situations during psycho-analysis, when the boundaries of the self become regressively blurred, it is suggested that the processes of incorporation and introjection may be revived and earlier identifications reactivated.

We appreciate this generous evaluation of our work, but wish to call attention to the fact that it implies a greater degree of validation of these concepts than we have claimed (2), (3). Because the material referred to is unpublished, we feel it necessary to clarify our position.

The unpublished comment referred to by Grinker was a memorandum prepared by one of us (G.E.) in January 1955 to clarify for our research group our understanding of incorporation, introjection, and identification at that time. It was not intended to publish this material, but mimeographed copies were circulated among a number of colleagues for their reactions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reports of the Scientific Activities of the Component and Affiliate Societies of the International Psycho-Analytical Association22

Pearl King B.A.

Pearl King

Pearl King  

EDITORIAL NOTE

Since the International Psycho-Analytical Association now consists of twenty component societies as well as nineteen Affiliate Societies and three officially recognized Study Groups, the unabridged Reports received from them amount to a great deal of material. Furthermore, the amount of detail given, e.g. about training schemes, varied very greatly, and while some Reports were confined to internal matters, others contained much information about public relations.

In editing the Reports we had to consider what information would be of most value to members. Provisionally, it seemed that information of two types would be of particular interest: first, regarding changes of policy in any Society during the past year and, secondly, a brief account of each Society's scientific activities. It was hoped that in this way any member of the International interested in a particular topic could make direct contact with any other members who had read papers on this topic. The Bulletin could thus help in the cross-fertilization of psycho-analytic ideas. Information concerning membership and offices of the Societies is contained in the Roster, and therefore will not be repeated in these Reports.

We hope that the Business Meeting of the next Congress may give some guidance on the proper character and function of the Bulletin.

NORTH AMERICA

THE AMERICAN PSYCHOANALYTIC ASSOCIATION

36 West 44th Street, New York 36, N.Y.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2: I. Peter Glauber.  'Freud's Contributions on Stuttering:  Their Relation to some Current Insights.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2

I. Peter Glauber. 'Freud's Contributions on Stuttering: Their Relation to some Current Insights.'

Freud's account of the patient Frau Emmy von N. in the Studies on Hysteria provides most of the material which the author examines in the light of more recent analytical knowledge. He refers to a number of factors which probably led to the patient's speech defect. Those named include repression of the oedipal wish, regression to orality, and separation anxiety in all its varied forms. Other references which Freud made to stuttering are also commented on. Freud was not optimistic about the value of psycho-analysis in speech disorders. Glauber, in the light of his own experiences, does not feel that this pessimism is justified.

362

responseSet.title: Creative Passion of the Artist and its Synesthetic Aspects
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creative Passion of the Artist and its Synesthetic Aspects11

Felix Deutsch M.D.

Felix Deutsch

Felix Deutsch, M.D.
BOSTON, MASS

Felix Deutsch  

The main body of this essay was written several years ago with the intention of having it critically appraised by Ernst Kris before publication. It is based on the writings on art by Freud and others (4), (12), (19), (22), (31), (35), (36), (40), (41), (45), (46), (49), but mainly by Ernst Kris (26), (27), (28), (29), (30). It contains reflections on the motivation for artistic creativity, particularly of sculptors, and their urge to recreate living forms as if resolving their own unconscious conflicts in this manner.

Ernst Kris had the passion of the scientist—the intellectual passion which requires originality. 'Creative scientists spend their lives', as Michael Polanyi (37) had only recently pointed out, 'in trying to guess right. They are sustained and guided therein by their heuristic passion. Even as the artist's sensibility merges into his creative powers. Such is the heuristic function of scientific passion.'

Twenty-five years ago I had a discussion with Ernst Kris on my observations of the facial expressions of dying patients in the hospital ward. He had at that time just published his critical appreciations of the work of Franz Xaver Messerschmidt, and particularly of how this paranoid artist succeeded in representing the physiognomy of anxiety, anger, frustration, greed, etc., in the faces of his figures. They were all self-portraits, asking, as it were, 'Who am I?' but also of those outside of him, who laugh at him, are angry with him, afraid of him, etc. These sculptures of facial expressions made a deep impression on me. They were the source of my later interest in postural behaviour (9). I owe Ernst Kris the encouragement for those studies which guided me into the meaning of artistic creativity (7), (10). From the outset, he reminded me of Freud's words: 'Before the problem of the creative artist, analysis must, alas, lay down its arms' (13).

Some twelve years ago in a lecture on 'Art and Regression' (29), I heard him again refer to this remarkable sculptor who worked more than 200 years ago. 'In the first bust of his series', as Kris said, 'he represented one face sleeping, yawning, and smiling. The face was always his own. He suddenly was convinced that he had discovered secrets of Nature, long hidden by the Gods. Therefore, evil demons were persecuting him. The persecutions were both of an anal and an oral nature, thus the mouths of all his busts (there are more than forty of them) are closed so that nothing can enter.' 'The defence against the intruder through the mouth, suggested by his delusion, are ideas regularly condensed by the primary processes.' 'In order to close the lips he sometimes put a band over them. At other times, as if in spite, the lip is made to protrude; and in two cases, in heads which he refused to look at when he was visited by his interviewer, the lips are pulled into long extended shapes which, as Messerschmidt said, was meant to frighten the spirits. Here clearly, the oral intrusion of the persecutor is depicted.

responseSet.title: Expression in Symbolic Logic of the Characteristics of the System Ucs or the Logic of the System Ucs
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Expression in Symbolic Logic of the Characteristics of the System Ucs or the Logic of the System Ucs11

Ignacio Matte-Blanco M.D.

Ignacio Matte-Blanco

Ignacio Matte-Blanco, M.D.
SANTIAGO DE CHILE

Ignacio Matte-Blanco  

Introduction and formulation of the problem

The discovery of the characteristics of the system Ucs is the most creative and fundamental of Freud's discoveries, because it is on these characteristics that his greatest contributions to psychology, especially all those pertaining to dreams, are based. We have indirect evidence that he valued them particularly. In his preface (1, p. xxxii) to the third English edition of The Interpretation of Dreams (1931) he mentions that 'it contains, even according to my present-day judgement, the most valuable of all the discoveries it has been my good fortune to make. Insight such as this falls to one's lot but once in a lifetime.'

On the other hand we know from Jones (4, p. 34) that there were in Freud's writings three things of which he thought highly; one was the last chapter of this book, another his essay on 'The Unconscious'. Now both these rest, so to speak, on the foundations given by the characteristics of the system Ucs, and in both the study of these characteristics occupies a prominent place. Finally, in his New Introductory Lectures(3, p. 99) he comments: 'It is constantly being borne in upon me that we have made far too little use for our theory of the indubitable fact that the repressed remains unaltered by the passage of time. This seems to offer us the possibility of an approach to some really profound truths. But I myself have made no further progress here.' This reference to one of the characteristics is worded in a manner that leaves no doubt as to his estimate of its importance. It is obvious that what he says about this particular one could be applied to all. Yet recent analytic researches are, on the whole, sadly uninterested in this fundamental topic.

It is worth remarking that the terminology employed on this subject seems to have changed in the course of years. In The Interpretation of Dreams(1, pp. 588–609, esp. p. 597) Freud distinguishes between the primary and the secondary process; and in his work on 'The Unconscious' (2, pp. 118–122) he included the first as one of the 'Special Characteristics of the System Ucs', while earlier he seems to have employed the term primary to designate them all.

responseSet.title: On the Continuation of the Analytic Process after Psycho-Analysis (A Self-Observation)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Continuation of the Analytic Process after Psycho-Analysis (A Self-Observation)

Maria Kramer

Maria Kramer

Maria K. Kramer
CHICAGO

Maria Kramer  

In Analysis Terminable and Interminable (3, p. 352), Freud stated as a condition for a successful training analysis: 'We hope and believe that the stimuli received in the learner's own analysis will not cease to act upon him when that analysis ends, that the processes of ego transformation will go on of their own accord and that all further experiences will be made use of in a newly acquired way.1 This does indeed happen and, in so far as it happens, it qualifies the learner who has been analysed to become an analyst.'

In this paper I propose to show, with examples gained through self-observations, that the processes of ego-transformation do indeed continue of their own accord, i.e. not activated by conscious volition. On the basis of these observations, I have made the assumption that the capacity to integrate unconscious conflicts in the form of insight can become an independent ego function, which for purposes of this communication I shall call autoanalytic. I assume this function to be initiated by psycho-analytic therapy which introduces conscious awareness of a repressed conflict as a new solution to psychic conflicts in preference to defensive activity previously employed. The non-volitional nature of the autoanalytic function was demonstrated to me repeatedly by the emergence of analytic insights when I was not actively seeking them. Such observations are confirmed by Reik (6) who described insights as coming spontaneously as if by inspiration. 'I had no intention of interpreting the dream and never turned my attention to it consciously. My interest in it was thus felt in spite of myself.'

Analytical knowledge and theory has always been based on empirical data. The difficulty of acquiring such data with regard to post-analytic processes has stood in the way of enlarging our knowledge in this area. Consequently, any assumptions about post-analytic developments are largely speculative. There are two opportunities to acquire the necessary data. One is given by follow-up studies on patients, the other by the use of data acquired by self-observation of analysts. Attempts at follow-up studies on patients are being made. I shall approach the problem by using my own experiences. In trying to do so, I have found a number of difficulties which probably explain the dearth of such observations in the published literature. If we felt free to use the material gained by self-observation, a large amount of empirical data would be available among analysts who, having been analysed, are going through the experience of post-analytic development. The objection might be raised as to the validity of such observations because of lack of objectivity. This seems to me an inadequate reason for discarding a vast source of material which could be validated by comparison of the different observations obtained. Furthermore, it is not unlikely that knowledge gained by this method could then be validated by other means. But this is not the only difficulty encountered in using self-observations. I found the following ones myself. In trying to formulate my ideas for publication, the very personal nature of the material discovered, which could not be protected by anonymity as is possible with data gained from patients, seemed to be an insurmountable difficulty. I was ready to give up the attempt. Later, I realized that it might be possible to describe the dynamic processes without specific reference to personal details.

responseSet.title: The Points of View and Assumptions of Metapsychology
responseSet.authorMast: David Rapaport and  Merton M. Gill
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Points of View and Assumptions of Metapsychology11

David Rapaport Ph.D. and Merton Gill M.D.

Merton Gill

David Rapaport, Ph.D.
STOCKBRIDGE, MASS.
Merton M. Gill, M.D.
BERKELEY, CALIF.

Merton Gill  

I. ON THE PRESENT STATE OF METAPSYCHOLOGY

Freud first used the term metapsychology to indicate that his psychology deals with what is beyond the realm of conscious experience (6, p. 246). Later, however, he defined metapsychology as the study of the assumptions upon which the system of psycho-analytic theory is based (12, p. 222). While metapsychological statements are scattered throughout Freud's writings, the seventh chapter of The Interpretation of Dreams, the 'Papers on Metapsychology' (fragments of a never completed work; see Jones (21), pp. 184–187), and the 'Addenda' to The Problem of Anxiety are our sources for reconstructing Freud's metapsychology.

These sources, however, do not fulfil the programme implied in Freud's definition. They do not state systematically that minimal set of assumptions on which the psycho-analytic theory rests. Indeed, in all these sources propositions stating observations, theories, and underlying assumptions are closely interwoven. Systematic studies in metapsychology, however, will have to distinguish between empirical propositions, specific psycho-analytic propositions, propositions of the general psycho-analytic theory, and propositions stating the metapsychological assumptions.2 In formulating the assumptions which follow here, we have avoided using specific psycho-analytic concepts. We are not yet in a position to present formal definitions of the terms used in stating these assumptions. We are, however, aware that without such definitions a set of assumptions is of limited value and that, indeed, some of the assumptions presented here are little more than covert definitions. Thus the formulation of the definitions will probably modify this statement of the assumptions.

At some point in the development of every science, the assumptions on which it is built must be clarified. Freud meant metapsychology to do just that for psycho-analysis. This justifies our attempt to state explicitly and systematically

(Received 23 March, 1958)

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Harold F. Searles, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>CHESTNUT LODGE, ROCKVILLE, MARYLAND</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Oedipal Love in the Counter Transference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Oedipal Love in the Counter Transference

Harold Searles M.D.

Harold Searles

Harold F. Searles, M.D.
CHESTNUT LODGE, ROCKVILLE, MARYLAND

Harold Searles  

This paper will advance three hypotheses which, as will be shown, are interrelated: (a) in the course of a successful psycho-analysis2, the analyst goes through a phase of reacting to, and eventually relinquishing, the patient as being his oedipal love-object; (b) in normal personality-development, the parent reciprocates the child's oedipal love with greater intensity than we have recognized heretofore; and (c) in such normal development, the passing of the Oedipus complex is at least as important a phase in ego-development as in superego-development.

Since I began doing psycho-analysis and intensive psychotherapy nine years ago, I have found, time after time, that in the course of the work with every one of my patients who has progressed to, or very far toward, a thoroughgoing analytic cure, I have experienced romantic and erotic desires to marry, and fantasies of being married to, the patient. Such fantasies and emotions have appeared in me usually relatively late in the course of treatment, have been present not briefly but usually for a number of months, and have subsided only after my having experienced a variety of feelings—frustration, separation-anxiety, grief, and so forth—entirely akin to those which attended what I experienced as the resolution of my Oedipus complex late in my personal analysis—specifically, about five years ago.

As I shall detail later on, with the first few patients toward whom I found myself having such feelings, I reacted with much anxiety, embarrassment, and guilt. My training had been predominantly such as to make me hold rather suspect any strong feelings on the part of the analyst toward his patient, and these particular emotions seemed to be of an especially illegitimate nature. My observations of colleagues' work with their patients, observations made in the course of listening to case-presentations or during informal chats with colleagues about our work or, in the past five years, while doing supervision, strongly suggested to me that these colleagues were not insusceptible to experiencing such feelings themselves. But it was only a rare one who openly acknowledged the presence of them in himself; so I remained, until very recently, largely convinced that I have an unusual propensity for exploiting analytic patients for the purpose of grappling with my own unresolved Oedipus conflicts.

responseSet.title: Aspects of Defence in Neurosis and Psychosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aspects of Defence in Neurosis and Psychosis

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman
Glasgow

Thomas Freeman  

The purpose of this paper is to examine certain aspects of the defensive process in psychoneurotic states and in psychotic reactions. To achieve this end excerpts from the treatment of a number of psychoneurotic and psychotic patients will be presented. Three of these patients were undergoing psycho-analytical therapy, whereas the remainder were treated by analytically orientated psychotherapy. It is, of course, quite artificial to isolate particular defence mechanisms from the general defensive activity of the ego, but this is essential for the present investigation. The clinical phenomena which are to be described developed within the framework of the transference situation. It is here that object relationships and the interplay of impulse and defence can be most readily observed.

In view of the importance which is currently attached to the concepts of introjection and projection, it seems appropriate to study them once again in the light of observations derived from both psychoneurotic and psychotic states. Such an enquiry cannot be undertaken without the inclusion of the concept of identification, which has such close links with introjection and projection. Introjection describes a dynamic process. It is the psychical correlate of the incorporative aim of the oral drives. From a topographical standpoint it operates in relation to the ego. Projection also relates to the ego and dynamically it is concerned with the extrusion of any influences which might disturb the ego in its relations with the environment and the super-ego. Identification has been described by Fenichel (7) as an instinctual vicissitude. The changes which occur in the aim and object of the drive lead to identification. In the introduction to the structural formulations Freud (18) pointed out that '… the ego is a precipitate of abandoned object cathexes and that it contains a record of past object choices'. Identification leads to the characteristic traits by which the ego will later be recognized. It is hardly necessary to add that Freud (18) regarded identification as the original mode of object relationship. He distinguished between this primary form and those secondary identifications to be found in normal and abnormal conditions. Identification is always thought of as a structural concept but Glover (21) has suggested that introjection too can be regarded in structural as well as dynamic terms. Here the distinction will be maintained, identification being reserved for the structural change which occurs within the ego, and introjection for the means whereby this is achieved. Foulkes (11) followed this trend by reserving identification for the ego and introjection for the id. For him introjection designated '… the act of inclusion into the ego complex by way of instinctive pattern' while identification was limited to '… the fact of inclusion into the ego system'. In favour of this distinction is the fact that introjection and projection imply the presence of a boundary, however indistinct, marking the individual off from his environment. Identification in its primary form does not demand this condition. While identification is always based upon introjection, this does not apply to primary identification. It is hoped that this view, initially proposed by Fenichel (7), will be amply supported by the clinical observations to be reported later.

responseSet.title: An Investigation Into the Psycho-Analytic Theory of Depression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Investigation Into the Psycho-Analytic Theory of Depression

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld, Dr.
LONDON

Herbert Rosenfeld  

In this paper I am attempting to give a detailed account of the work of the majority of those analysts who have made considerable contributions to the psychopathology of depression1 One purpose of this study has been, first to discover the main trends in the development of the theory of depression by clarifying the views of each author, and afterwards, through a detailed comparison of these, to arrive at some picture of the differences and similarities between the various theories advanced. In order to achieve this, it was necessary first to make detailed abstracts of the work of each author and by a process of condensation to arrive at the relevant factors. During this survey of the literature, I was struck by the fact that certain points constantly came up for discussion, the most outstanding being: the role of constitutional factors; the importance of aggression or increased ambivalence; the importance of oral elements; the role of processes of introjection and identification; the importance of narcissism; the nature of the early object relations; the importance of processes of splitting of objects and the self into good and bad parts; the origin and the nature of the depressive superego; the importance of certain infantile phases or periods, and their relation to the development of adult depression; the relationship of paranoia and paranoid anxieties to depression; the relationship of mania and depression. This last aspect I decided to omit almost completely from the discussion, since it is too large a subject for inclusion; I have therefore limited myself to depression per se. In describing the details of the work of various analysts, I have particularly concentrated on those contributions which relate to the points I have just listed.

Freud first showed his psychological interest in the theory of melancholia in 1897 in a letter to Fliess, but it was only in 1910–1911 that the first papers by psycho-analytic writers on depression were published. I am thinking of the work of Maeder, Brill, and Abraham.

responseSet.title: The Concept of Narcissistic Mortification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Narcissistic Mortification11

Ludwig Eidelberg M.D.

Ludwig Eidelberg

Ludwig Eidelberg, M.D.
Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, School of Medicine, State University of New York

Ludwig Eidelberg  

To those who have happened to read some of my previous papers, another paper on the subject of the Narcissistic Mortification may be something of a surprise. After many years of preoccupation with the study of this phenomenon, it is embarrassing to be forced to admit that an important aspect of the problem has eluded my attention completely. In mitigation of such an 'offence', one may plead that if we stumble on something new there is rarely anybody around who would be able to explain it clearly and distinctly, and in consequence, a description of a phenomenon hitherto unknown must appear confused, blurred, and even contradictory. In addition to the 'conscious' difficulty connected with an accurate portrayal of the Narcissistic Mortification, I have had also to overcome my own unconscious resistances. Probably, the recognition that the Narcissistic Mortification has nothing to do with our own desires was too difficult for me to accept, although I had recognized long ago that my patients, in addition to repressing their infantile wishes, eliminate from their conscious minds other phenomena, the so-called Narcissistic Mortifications.

Using a descriptive approach, it was thought that a Narcissistic Mortification took place whenever the subject became an object of another subject. An attack by a robber was used as illustration in which the robber had the power to force the subject to do what he wanted. The consequent Narcissistic Mortification was defined as a sudden loss of control over external or internal reality, or both, by virtue of which the emotion of terror is produced, along with the damming up of narcissistic libido or destrudo. Unfortunately, according to this definition, the Narcissistic Mortification was described metapsychologically as caused by the damming up of narcissistic libido or destrudo. Furthermore, the phrase, 'loss of control', does not indicate sufficiently clearly that this loss of control is the result of the power of somebody else to overwhelm the subject. This statement appears to be false, and therefore in need of correction. For example, should this robber return after a few minutes, and give me back the money he had taken, I would, of course, while rejoicing in his change of heart and the return of my property, keep on resenting his power to take it away in the first place, and would want to get even. In addition to the restitution, a punishment for his impudence would help to eliminate the humiliating experience of having to yield to his threat. It should have been obvious from the beginning that the experience of terror which accompanies a Narcissistic Mortification can not be caused only by the dammed-up wishes of the subject, but must result from the power of another, and stronger, subject, who succeeds in inflicting a Narcissistic Mortification on the weaker individual. We must learn, finally, to differentiate between unpleasure due to the damming up of narcissistic and object libido and destrudo, and an unpleasure felt when the subject, against his will, becomes the object of the instinctual gratification of an aggressor. We must understand that a neurosis is caused not only by the unconscious infantile demands of the patient, but also by the unconscious Narcissistic Mortifications, both internal and external.

O." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>J.</nfirst><nmid>O.</nmid><nlast>Wisdom</nlast></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">J. O. Wisdom</cs></ln><ln>LONDON</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: On a Differentiating Mechanism of Psychosomatic Disorder
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On a Differentiating Mechanism of Psychosomatic Disorder11

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. O. Wisdom
LONDON

J. Wisdom  

1. Introduction

It was Freud, as might be expected, who took the initiative in studying psychosomatic disorder, and indeed it was in this field that he began his psycho-analytic work. But he confined himself mainly to conversion-hysteria (where symbolism is in evidence) and did not devote himself in a comparable way to the general field of enquiry (where symbolism is highly doubtful).2 This was opened up in the second quarter of this century by later psycho-analysts, Alexander (1943) and Deutsch (1939a), (1939b) who have provided two of the very few theories in the field. Then, rather more than a decade ago, came the theory of Selye (1952), which was endocrinological so far as mechanism was concerned. In 1952 I (1953) tried to form a specific theory on the general problem. And the next year Wolff (1953) produced a theory centering on an idea like Selye's, which Wolff, however, made more definite and infused with psychological content. In addition there is Groen's (1957) non-specific framework.

In the sense in which theories are understood in science, i.e. groups of testable hypotheses possessing a high degree of specificity and power of explaining mechanisms in some detail, there are very few psychosomatic theories. There are, however, several highly important ideas that have been put forward, which have not been developed, at any rate as yet, into theories capable of answering specific questions about mechanism. Though not theories, these ideas are of central importance, in that they are very likely to have a fundamental bearing on the enquiry, and should therefore find a place in a well developed theory. Ideas that may prove to be indispensable are: Head's (1911–1912) body-schema; internal objects, as introduced by Freud but taken in the more specific form they assume with Melanie Klein; Garma's (1950) biting mother-imago (which is evidently an application of Klein's concept); Alexander's (1935) directional attitudes;3 and Kardiner's (1941) 'action-syndrome'.

responseSet.title: The Problem of the Identical Twin as Reflected in a Masochistic Compulsion to Cheat
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Problem of the Identical Twin as Reflected in a Masochistic Compulsion to Cheat11

Pierre Lacombe M.D.

Pierre Lacombe

Pierre Lacombe, M.D.
NEW YORK

Pierre Lacombe  

Ne me demande rien; le jour où je suis né J'avais commis mon crime et j'étais condamné.

VICTOR HUGO (Les Jumeaux)

As Freud (3) has stated in An Outline of Psycho-analysis, 'the determining causes of all the varying forms of human mental life are to be looked for in the interplay between inherited dispositions and accidental experiences.' The importance of studying identical twins, in an effort to evaluate the relative influence of those inherited and environmentally conditioned factors, is therefore self-evident.

Authors such as Heinz Hartmann (4) and more recently Dorothy Burlingham (1) have observed a certain number of pairs of identical twins and reported on their study. But the published material of actual psycho-analyses of identical twins is rather limited.

The paper by Douglas W. Orr (7) deals with 'A Psychoanalytic Study of a Fraternal Twin', not of an identical one. Herbert Cronin (2), in his 1933 paper, 'An Analysis of the Neuroses of Identical Twins', comes to the conclusion that for the pair analyzed 'twinship was a distinct handicap … and from it arose the intrapsychic conflict precipitating the situations that led up to their neuroses.'

On the other hand, in his 1946 paper, 'The Psychoanalysis of Identical Twins with Reference to Inheritance', Endre Petö (8) feels that 'the results are anything but ambiguous.' Further psycho-analytical observations seem, therefore, very desirable before considering any conclusion.

The purpose of this paper is to submit such clinical data yielded in the analysis—fragments of which are reported hereafter—of an identical twin affected by a severe obsessional neurosis with a masochistic compulsion to cheat, and to see what light such analytical data can shed on the problem of the identical twin.

I. THE FIRST SESSION

Jack, 41, comes to analysis on the advice of his wife's analyst. He complains of only one symptom, an intermittent pain in his penis and a burning ejaculatory sensation which has no physical cause.

Jack, rather short and stocky, has a personable mien. He is a business man of some wealth, working in his father-in-law's flourishing enterprise.

responseSet.title: Chess, Oedipus, and the Mater Dolorosa
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chess, Oedipus, and the Mater Dolorosa11

Norman Reider M.D.

Norman Reider

Norman Reider, M.D.
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA

Norman Reider  

The psycho-analytic study of play and games has been particularly rewarding, but no game is so full of possibilities for such study as that of chess. Chess is the royal game for many reasons. It crystallizes within its elaborate structure the family romance, is replete with symbolism, and has rich potentialities for granting satisfactions and for sublimation of drives. Not without reason is it the one game that, since its invention around A.D. 600, has been played in most of the world, has captivated the imagination and interest of millions, and has been the source of great sorrows and great pleasures.

My introduction has two purposes. First, it indicates the numerous facets inherent in a study of chess: the fascination and the extent of the addiction to the game; the psychological factors involved in its historical development; its social and therapeutic value; its legal involvements; its relation to love and aggression; the problem of genius in chess; the characterological problem of its players and their style of play; and ego functions as manifested in play, especially the distinctions between the psychological meanings of the game, its pieces and rules, and the psychology of the players. My intention is to develop these themes more extensively in later essays and to stimulate the interest of others in these problems. Secondly, this exposition is also meant to convey some of the quality and flavour of the game, especially to non-players, or to those who have never been addicted to or fascinated by chess, so that they may be prepared for the complexities of the legends.

THE ATTRACTION OF THE GAME

Throughout the ages chess has been praised in poetry, prose, painting, and even music, as witness a few testimonials from devotees. 'Chess is the art of the intellect' (42).3 'Chess is so ancient that by that distinction alone it seems taken beyond the category of games altogether; and it has been said that it probably would have perished long ago if it had not been destined to live for ever' (6, p. 1). 'There must have been a time when men were demigods or they could not have invented chess. Could it indeed have been invented? I am almost tempted to believe that chess is a discovered fragment of inexhaustible, ever-creative nature' (69). 'Chess is catholic and common to every country. It possesses a history, language, literature, poetry, prose, science and art of its own' (73, p. 5). 'I have always had a slight feeling of pity for the man who has no knowledge of chess, just as I would pity the man who has remained ignorant of love. Chess, like love, like music, has the power to make men happy' (74, p. 4).

responseSet.title: Liebestod Fantasies in a Patient Faced with a Fatal Illness
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Liebestod Fantasies in a Patient Faced with a Fatal Illness

Bernard Brodsky M.D.

Bernard Brodsky

Bernard Brodsky, M.D.
NEW YORK

Bernard Brodsky  

How a person faces the real possibility of his own demise is a question that many analysts have probably thought of but about which little has been written. The case history to be presented concerns a cultured attractive young woman who, learning she had a fatal illness, attempted to ward off her fear of death with the fantasy of eternal reunion with a dead brother.

Early in life her mother's aloofness had drawn her towards her warm and brilliant father. When she saw that he did not measure up to her overvaluation and as she became aware of his preference for her brother, the longing for her father had been replaced by an intense feeling for the brother. Her love for him was reciprocated, even though envy of his masculinity contaminated her affection. There developed a far-reaching community of interest in poetry, literature, and music. They were deeply attracted to Wagner, especially to Tristan and Isolde, and when the two children heard the Liebestod—or love death—they were moved to tears. She was Isolde and he Tristan. It will be recalled that Tristan had an illicit love affair with Isolde, the betrothed of King Mark, his uncle. Isolde is then wedded to King Mark, but clandestine meetings with Tristan continue and are revealed to the king by a jealous friend. In a struggle with this friend Tristan is mortally wounded. Tristan dies in Isolde's arms, and Mark's forgiveness is too late. Isolde laments her lover and also dies, being united with him in death. But her dying is no death, and in her final aria she recounts her passionate response to his living beauties as if seeing them in an undying vision. Wagner describes her as 'verklärt'—transfigured. This fantasy of ecstatic reunion after death had the most profound effect upon the patient. Enthralled with the Love-Death, the two children vowed that if one were to die before the other, he would give a signal from the other world. Thus was death denied in advance.

The patient also felt that a story of Thomas Mann, The Blood of the Volsungs, which she read as an adult, had a special meaning for her: Siegmund and Sieglinde, devoted twins, go to see Die Walküre, in which Wagner's twins, also Siegmund and Sieglinde, sleep together. The twins of Mann's story then go home and proceed to act out the incest they have just seen presented in fantasy by Wagner.

responseSet.title: The Unconscious Motives of War: By Alix Strachey.  (London:  Allen and Unwin.  Pp. 283.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Unconscious Motives of War

By Alix Strachey. (London: Allen and Unwin. Pp. 283. 25 s.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

This work is in three parts. Part I, entitled 'Undercurrents of Individual Psychology', is by far the largest and consists of an attractive, beguilingly simple account of the unconscious mind, personality structure, and emotional development. It is a pleasant and admirably clear introduction to psycho-analytical concepts which should be not only illuminating to the lay reader but also of use to the analytically sophisticated who, in the course of their work, have to explain psycho-analytical concepts using as few technical terms as possible. In reading this book they will learn how to do it.

Part II, 'Danger Points in Group Psychology', outlines the role of the unconscious in social behaviour and then proceeds through a consideration of the group as a regressive force to a description of how that group known as the Sovereign State evokes such regression and, in the absence of democratic institutions, militates towards those episodic fulminations of outwardly directed violence known as war. The regressive phenomena which cause all the best that has been achieved so slowly and painfully to disintegrate catastrophically occur more readily under the impact of authoritarian leadership. War has become so unrestricted and no longer can be confined to those who choose to be involved in it that something will have to be done, and that urgently, if those things which we value, and even our physical beings, are to survive. This leads on to the third part of Mrs. Strachey's book.

In Part III, 'Recommendations', the author recommends changes in the State which, in brief, consist of an increase in the sort of democracy which we know in Britain and America—to mention but two of the Western nations. This consists in a curbing of autocratic leadership and in peripheralizing rather than centralizing government, restricting the powers of those leaders in whose personalities despotism is malignantly ingrained. Pari passu with this process of decentralization she recommends that broad contacts should be fostered by education, foreign travel, and supra-national organizations. In this way the discrete, paranoid, autocratically led grouping would be more difficult to achieve, its dynamic would be decreased and its danger minimized. By fostering the small family unit and by bringing up children sensibly the author thinks that a considerable measure of true prophylaxis will be attained. She also recommends that the educators and governors of the people should be given opportunities for gaining psycho-analytical insight, which is tantamount in certain cases to removing the fuse from a time bomb.

responseSet.title: Alfred Adler, Apostle of Freedom: By Phyllis Bottome.  (London:  Faber, 1957.  Pp. 300.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Alfred Adler, Apostle of Freedom

By Phyllis Bottome. (London: Faber, 1957. Pp. 300. 25 s.)

Katherine Jones

Katherine Jones

Katherine Jones  

It is notoriously difficult to write a good biography, and in the field of psychology those that can claim the stamp of excellence can be counted—to quote a famous slip—on one finger. Mrs. Bottome's book on Alfred Adler does not come into the category. It is indeed less a biography than a paean of praise, and to find out the plain facts of Adler's life—surely the first requirement

66

in a biography—we have to turn to one of the appendices (Prof. Birnbaum's). The more dramatic qualities of the man, however, such as 'the beautiful cadenced voice', 'the hooded penetrating eyes looking out from under heavy brows as if he understood the soul of man', and the combination of 'a fiery temper with the patience of an angel' are to be found in Mrs. Bottome's observations.

Adler (we now follow Mrs. Bottome) was not only a distinguished psychologist but equally outstanding in the fields of education and ethics. Born and brought up in one of the poorer suburbs of Vienna, the second child of Jewish parents, he became 'a Christian, to escape the isolation of Jewishness'. He decided early to become a doctor, owing apparently to the sudden death of a younger brother, so that he might have 'the mastery over death', as Prof. Birnbaum puts it. How soon the young medical student must have been disappointed! He was successful as a general practitioner, contracted a marriage with a Russian lady which after some estrangement was resumed towards the end of his life, and had four children. The war of 1914 changed Austria and with it his life. He went to America, where. however, he was not a success. In the 1920's he came to England to read papers at Congresses, but went back to the United States in the 1930's, where he held appointments as Visiting Professor at Columbia University and Long Island Medical College. By 1934 he was back in England and lectured at various schools, at University College, London, and at Christ Church, Oxford. A lengthy course of lectures was arranged for him by Mr. Bottome in Aberdeen in April–May 1937. He collapsed while taking a walk in that city and never regained consciousness. His life-span extended from February 1870 to May 1937.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Youth and Crime

Edited by Frank J. Cohen. (New York: International Universities Press, 1957. Pp. xiv + 274. $6.00.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

This book consists of a series of articles by people representing the various disciplines who are concerned in the matter of dealing with delinquency in young people. The approach to the subject is simple and earnest, too superficial to be of great interest or importance to psycho-analysts but nevertheless worthy and timely in view of the great increase in juvenile delinquency. Despite a certain therapeutic naïveté, as exemplified by the idea that individual techniques might be equally applicable to groups, the whole direction of the book is worthy and the motives of the numerous contributors wholly laudable in that time and again it is made apparent that shortage of money, or staff, and in particular of suitably knowledgeable workers in the field of delinquency hampers progress.

70

responseSet.title: Pain and Pleasure: By Thomas S. Szasz, M.D. (London:  Tavistock Publications Ltd., 1957.  Pp. xvi + 301.  30s.; New York:  Basic Books, 1957.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pain and Pleasure

By Thomas S. Szasz, M.D. (London: Tavistock Publications Ltd., 1957. Pp. xvi + 301. 30s.; New York: Basic Books, 1957.)

R. De Saussure

R. De Saussure

R. De Saussure  

This book, on pleasure and pain, constitutes an attempt to extend Freud's excessively physico-physiological conception. It is full of originality o outlook and deserves the most thorough study.

The author, Hungarian in origin, studied medicine in Cincinnati. He completed his training at the Chicago Institute of Psychoanalysis, and subsequently taught there for five years. Before writing the present work he published a series of brilliant papers in a number of American psychiatric journals. The book is made easy to read by the clarity of the writing, the excellent lay-out, the abundant bibliography and the author and subject indexes.

Taking over an idea of Bertrand Russell's (A History of Western Philosophy, 1945), Szasz distinguishes private facts (acquired through introspection) from public facts (observed by others), and suggests that the data of psychology are related to the former. The traditional distinction between psychogenic pain and organic pain has long hindered medicine and psychiatry. One way of clarifying this problem, or at least of by-passing it, would be to distinguish affects as experienced by the self from the same facts seen and judged by an external observer. The analytical situation is particularly suitable for the study of both these aspects.

Szasz classifies pain as an affect, in the psycho-analytical sense of the term, and consequently it has also to be studied in relation to the ego. Like anxiety it is first and foremost an alarm signal; but whereas anxiety tells us about external danger or libidinal strivings, pain tells us about bodily disorder. It is a communication from one part of the organism which informs the nervous centres of too intense a stimulation or of a loss of body substance.

Initially, pain permits the infant to notify the adult of his need for help (the social or communicative meaning of pain). Later on pain can be directed towards the doctor or any other person. It can even change its meaning and express feelings of grievance and outrage. It can symbolize a whole gamut of claims on the environment. If a child complains of a headache because he does not want to go to school, the task of the psychotherapist is not to establish the degree of pain (a subjective experience of the ego) or to deny its existence, but to discover what message he conveys and why he does it in that particular way.

responseSet.documentRef: Rapaport, D. (1959) Leo Berman, M.D—(13 April, 1913–27 December, 1958). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  40:334-338
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Rapaport, D. (1959) Leo Berman, M.D—(13 April, 1913–27 December, 1958). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 40:334-338

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195940334-338Leo Berman, M.D—(13 April, 1913–27 December, 1958)DavidRapaport
responseSet.title: Leo Berman, M.D—(13 April, 1913–27 December, 1958)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Leo Berman, M.D—(13 April, 1913–27 December, 1958)

David Rapaport

David Rapaport

David Rapaport  

Leo Berman was at the time of his death a psycho-analyst engaged in private practice and research; instructor (1950–8) at the Harvard University Medical School; visiting psychiatrist in charge of group work at the Beth Israel Hospital (1952–8); training analyst (1952–8), control analyst (1954–8), Secretary of the Education Committee (1952–7) of the Boston Psychoanalytic Institute; Member of the Editorial Board, American Journal of Orthopsychiatry (1954–8); consultant of the Joint Commission on Mental Illness and Health (1956–8). He obtained his medical training at the University of Basel, Switzerland (M.D., 1937), and his psycho-analytic training at the Boston Psychoanalytic Institute (1941–5).

I

Leo Berman died young in the middle of unfinished work carried on to the last in spite of wracking pain. He was not yet generally known outside his own geographical community, the Group for Advancement of Psychiatry, and group therapy circles, nor had he written much. Yet his work made a lasting impression on his associates, friends, students, and readers of his papers, and contributes to the development of the mainstream of psycho-analytic thought. I am writing about him here not only to express what many of us felt about him as a friend, but also to give voice to the intents that animated his work and life.

Leo Berman and his work are representative of the problems, trends, and aspirations of his generation of psycho-analysts. While his individuality, fierce integrity, courage, and dedication led to a unique synthesis of these problems, trends, and aspirations, his unassuming ways and the untimely interruption of his life-work tend to obscure his contributions to them. The very fact that Leo Berman's work remained uncompleted makes it a more faithful mirror of his generation than any completed life-work could be. Completed work—with the exception of the greatest—usually brings to a head the efforts of past generations, and thus tends to cloud what the current generation's struggle is about.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud

Translated from the German under the General Editorship of James Strachey, in collaboration with Anna Freud, assisted by Alix Strachey and Alan Tyson. Vol. XII (1911–1913). The Case of Schreber, Papers on Technique, and Other Works. (London: Hogarth Press and Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1958. Pp. vii + 373. £36 the set of 24 vols.; sold only in sets.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

This volume opens with the famous paper on Schreber's Memoirs, 'Psycho-Analytic Notes on an Autobiographical Account of a Case of Paranoia (Dementia Paranoides)'. According to the Editor, Freud as early as 1895 was writing to Fliess about paranoia as a neurosis of defence whose chief mechanism was projection; but it was only in this 1911 paper that he set out in full his views on the connexion between this disorder and repressed passive homosexuality, involving regression to a narcissistic phase of development. These views were never substantially modified in later papers, but the discussion of them in this paper fore-shadowed a number of subsequent contributions to metapsychology, e.g. 'On Narcissism' and 'Instincts and their Vicissitudes'. The Postscript shows Freud's awakening interest in mythology and in 'totems', here mentioned for the first time. Interest in the Schreber paper has never died out, but has recently been stimulated anew by Baumayer's summary of the actual case records and family history (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 37, 1956, 61–74).

Next come six papers on Technique, two written in 1914 being included because they belong to the series. They represent Freud's nearest approach to a systematic account of his technique which, in the main, must otherwise be inferred from his clinical papers. The Editor mentions a number of factors that may have induced this reluctance of Freud's to write about technique. Nearly all the translations are modified versions of those published in C.P. II, 1924, but 'The Dynamics of Transference' by James Strachey is completely new. At the end of the series is a useful appendix, a 'List of Writings by Freud dealing mainly with Psycho-Analytic Technique and the Theory of Psycho-Therapy'. The paper 'Remembering, Repeating and Working-Through' contains the first mention of the 'compulsion to repeat' and of 'working-through'. Freud thought that 'Observations on Transference-Love' was the best of this series.

The next paper, 'Dreams in Folklore', by Freud and Oppenheim is of special interest because, although probably written in 1911, it was overlooked until Oppenheim's daughter brought it to light in 1956, and it was published in New York in 1958. The collection of dreams cited enabled the authors 'to establish the fact that folklore interprets dream-symbols in the same way as psycho-analysis, and that … it derives a group of dreams from needs and wishes which have become immediate.

responseSet.title: Thrills and Regressions: By Michael Balint.  With a Chapter on 'Distance in Space and Time' by Enid Balint.  (No. 54, International Psycho-Analytical Library.  London:  Hogarth Press and Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1959.  18s. net.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thrills and Regressions

By Michael Balint. With a Chapter on 'Distance in Space and Time' by Enid Balint. (No. 54, International Psycho-Analytical Library. London: Hogarth Press and Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1959. 18s. net.)

Alastair Macleod

Alastair Macleod

Alastair W. Macleod
Montreal.

Alastair Macleod  

At first glance Dr. Michael Balint's book, to which Enid Balint has contributed a chapter entitled 'Distance in Space and Time', appears to be an easy book to review. He writes of exciting, thrilling things, of fun fairs and switchbacks, of children's games and dreams of flying, of air and water and of sand and words. He writes of people who love their independence and the friendly open spaces, who seek dizzying spills and frightening thrills, who seek to master the skills of an acrobat. For these people he has coined the term 'philobats'.

He speaks as well of timid people, of people who cling to people and things, of people who hate the horrid empty spaces, of people who dread being caught off balance. He coins another term to describe such people. They are the 'ocnophils'.

But closer reading reveals that his book deals with weighty matters, that his arguments are well thought out and that he has touched explicitly or implicitly on so many matters of on-going concern to analysts that a full review could well become a book in itself!

In essence Dr. Balint has written a book on ego psychology with particular reference to the economic aspects. The subject-matter represents a continuation of the author's abiding interest in two directions of research: the tracing of the erotic instinct from its earliest biological beginnings to its highest manifestations in human beings and the tracing of the development of the object-relations of the individual. He reiterates his belief that the development of object-relations cannot be fully explained on the basis of its being completely secondary to the development of instinctual aims. Instinctual aims and object-relations, although continuously influencing each other, are none the less fundamentally different processes.

342

[[RunningHead]]

Research Reports of the American Psychiatric Association, No. 7: Regional Research Conference, Nov. 1955

Stress; Experimental Psychology; Child Psychiatry. (Washington: APA, 1957. Pp. 88. $2.00.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The present volume of this valuable series contains interesting discussions which aim at the integration of biological, clinical, and psycho-analytic observations and theories. It is often insufficiently realized how much Cannon's concept of homeostasis and Freud's psychodynamics have in common. The paper 'Experimental Approach to Symptom Mechanisms in Psychiatric Patients' contributes to the knowledge of the physical concomitants of anxiety. The last third of the volume is taken up by a discussion of Kanner's 'early infantile autism' which is of great interest to psycho-analysts. Among those taking part were Kanner, Dancey, Clifford Scott, Eisenberg, and Keeler. Kanner assumes that those children are born with defective 'affective potentialities' and that emotional 'refrigeration' on the part of their parents further enhances this defect. Clifford Scott raises the question of the importance of the way the mother deals with the child's excretions.

359

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2: Thomas M. French.  'The Art and Science of Psycho-analysis.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 6, 1958, No. 2

Thomas M. French. 'The Art and Science of Psycho-analysis.'

In this paper the author proposes that it is time for the intuitive art which is part of psycho-analytic therapy to be made explicit and thus become available for communication. Psycho-analysis must be converted from an intuitive art to a scientifically orientated procedure. He suggests that this can be done by checking the intuitive inferences which the analyst makes by means of his critical faculties. The confusion which arises from the number of possible interpretations of a given piece of material can be offset by concentrating upon what French terms the 'focal conflict' which occurs in every analytical session.

The focal conflict must be differentiated from the nuclear conflict of the patient's neurosis. The former is generally preconscious. The advantage of interpretation at the level of the focal conflict is that it adds no new factor to complicate the patient's emotional situation and the analyst's picture of it. When the analyst comes to interpret he should predict the outcome and wait for confirmation or otherwise. The analyst must pay attention to the ego aspects of the patient's mental life as well as to the instinctual side. This can be well achieved by concentration upon the focal conflict which is usually taken up with the current life situation. At the same time it makes it possible for interpretations to become the object of study and thus limits the extent to which intuition can operate without control.

361

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1.: Bernhard Berliner.  'The Role of Object Relations in Moral Masochism.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 27, 1958, No. 1.

Bernhard Berliner. 'The Role of Object Relations in Moral Masochism.'

The term 'moral masochism' is used in this paper to describe those forms of masochism which appear as a 'norm of behaviour' in contrast with sexual or perverse masochism.

Berliner's presentation starts with Freud's distinction between self-torment and masochism. The prerequisite of masochism is an object-relationship. His thesis is that this object does not enter the picture only after the masochist's passive aim has been established, but has been a reality in his life from the beginning, and has been instrumental in bringing about the whole masochistic process. The essence of the masochist's problem is that he has to love someone who hates him; and such a person is always to be found in his history, though the degree of hatred necessary to traumatize is variable.

365

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957: Charles Brenner, M.D. (New York).  'The Nature and Development of the Concept of Repression in Freud's Writings.'  Pp. 19–46.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, 12, 1957

Charles Brenner, M.D. (New York). 'The Nature and Development of the Concept of Repression in Freud's Writings.' Pp. 19–46.

There were four principal stages at which Freud made important innovations in the concept of repression or significant additions to it: the stage of introduction of the concept, 1894–6; the period 1900–6; the period 1911–15; the period 1923–9. The author makes a meticulous and exhaustive examination of Freud's writings in each of these periods, showing the contribution made to the theory of repression from his changing views on the nature of neurosis, and of normal psycho-sexual development; from the development of his metapsychological thought; and from his elucidation of psychic structure. Consideration is given to his final views on the question of primal repression, on the conditions which favour a return of the repressed, and to his views on the inter-relatedness of repression and other defence mechanisms.

367

responseSet.title: Some Regressive Phenomena Involving the Perceptual Sphere—The Phenomena of Objects Coming Closer and of Objects Receding from View
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Regressive Phenomena Involving the Perceptual Sphere—The Phenomena of Objects Coming Closer and of Objects Receding from View

Melitta Sperling M.D.

Melitta Sperling

Melitta Sperling, M.D.
NEW YORK

Melitta Sperling  

This paper deals with phenomena of pathological visual perceptions observed during the psycho-analytic treatment of patients who were not psychotic and did not suffer from epilepsy or any organic disorder where similar phenomena are not infrequent. I am concerned specifically here with the phenomenon of objects coming closer (and becoming larger) and of objects receding from the patient. These objects were either real or imagined ones. These pathological perceptions proved to be regressive phenomena which had a definite psychic structure, content, and function, and were accessible to analysis and interpretation. It was found that these phenomena occurred in situations of acute emotional conflict when the danger of a break with reality seemed to be imminent. I should like to illustrate these points with clinical observations.

Case 1. A 36-year-old woman had come for analysis because of a skin condition from which she had suffered for the past nineteen years without any relief, although she had been continually under the care of competent skin specialists. This patient suffered, among other symptoms, from an unusually severe constipation. The experience to be dealt with here occurred toward the end of the second year of her analysis, when her skin condition had improved and the analysis was bringing to the fore her deeply repressed castration complex. A wealth of material clearly indicated that castration was felt by her as an anal loss, as if faeces were slipping away from her and disappearing. This patient also suffered from an intense fear of mice. She could go into a faint on seeing a mouse. Once she even had a fainting spell when someone mentioned having seen a mouse in the room where she had been.

The patient was, at this point, in an acute conflict as to whether to discontinue the analysis, rationalizing this with her marked improvement, or to stay and to have her fantasies exposed in the analysis. A dream highlighting this conflict was summed up by the patient as saying: 'I am crazy to leave and I am crazy to stay.' It was during this critical phase of her analysis that she reported the following experience: She woke up one night with anxiety, and had the sensation that the objects in the room were moving away from her. She became panicky and woke her husband, asking him to hold her hand and to talk to her. She felt as if she were losing her mind. There was no change in the size of the objects except that they were rapidly moving away from her and the room seemed to expand.

responseSet.title: Envy and Gratitude: A Study of Unconscious Forces: By Melanie Klein.  (London:  Tavistock Publications, 1957.  Pp. 91.  12s. 6d.  New York:  Basic Books.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Envy and Gratitude: A Study of Unconscious Forces

By Melanie Klein. (London: Tavistock Publications, 1957. Pp. 91. 12s. 6d. New York: Basic Books.)

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld  

In this book, Melanie Klein has radically revised our knowledge of two fundamentally important sources of human behaviour—envy and gratitude. She considers that envy is an oral sadistic and anal sadistic expression of destructive impulses operative from the beginning of life, and that it has a constitutional basis. This view has much in common with that of Karl Abraham, who considered envy an oral character trait and also stressed the constitutional basis of the strength of oral impulses. But Mrs. Klein has thrown an entirely new light on the importance of envy, both as an impulse and a character trait, by discovering the fundamental role which it plays in normal and abnormal infantile development. In particular, she shows the link between envy and the development of gratitude which, she believes, is essential in building up the loving relationship with the object.

Envy is defined by Mrs. Klein as 'the angry, grudging feeling that another person possesses and enjoys something desirable, the envious impulse being to take it away and to spoil it. Envy implies the subject's relation to one person only and goes back to the earliest exclusive relation with the mother and the breast. Jealousy, on the other hand, while based on envy, involves a relationship to at least two people. It is mainly concerned with love that the subject feels is his due and has been taken away by a rival.'

In distinguishing between greed and envy, which are so frequently combined, Mrs. Klein emphasizes in greed the destructive introjection, whereas in envy she stresses the projection and projective identification. She writes: 'Envy puts badness, primarily bad excrements and parts of the self, into the mother, and first of all into her breast, in order to spoil and destroy her.' This statement implies a link between envy and anal sadistic and urethral impulses.

Gratitude is regarded as a major derivative of the capacity for love. It is rooted in the emotions and attitudes that arise in the earliest stage of infancy, when for the baby the mother is the one and only object. The capacity for love and gratitude is assumed to be innate. How these innate feelings of gratitude develop, is traced to the full gratification at the breast which causes the infant to feel he has received from his loved object a unique gift which he wants to keep. Gratitude is closely linked with a trust in good figures. 'This includes, first of all, the ability to accept and assimilate the loved primal object without greed and envy interfering too much.' 'The more often gratification at the breast is experienced and fully accepted, the more often enjoyment and gratitude and accordingly the wish to return pleasure are felt.' This recurrent experience of gratification and gratitude in the earliest relation to the mother plays an important role in the capacity to make reparation and in all sublimations.

responseSet.title: The Contribution of Psycho-Analysis to the Biography of the Artist—A Commentary on Methodology
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Contribution of Psycho-Analysis to the Biography of the Artist—A Commentary on Methodology11

David Beres M.D.

David Beres

David Beres, M.D.
NEW YORK

David Beres  

I. PSYCHO-ANALYTIC BIOGRAPHY AND LITERARY CRITICISM

Biographical reconstruction is an inherent aim of the psycho-analytic process, and psycho-analysis as a genetic psychology is in essence a science of biography. In the clinical situation the focus is the biography of the patient, the reconstruction of the repressed events of his early life; in the application of psycho-analytic methods to the study of the life of a great man there is a similar interest in the reconstruction of aspects of his life that would not be available from an ordinary biographical study.

There is no unanimity in literary criticism about the value of biographical studies of artists, and in some quarters there is an actual aversion to the biographical approach. But whatever differences of opinion may exist, it is a fact that modern literary criticism includes among its techniques biography, psychology, and also psychological biography. Evidence of this is to be found in the review pages of any literary journal and in the increasing number of psychological studies by literary critics.2

Psycho-analysis cannot avoid its portion in the general interest in biography, and offers, as I propose to show in this paper, an instrument of particular sharpness and precision with which to scrutinize its psychological aspects. There is much to be learned from the life of a great person and there is an undeniable fascination in the study of such a life. The psycho-analyst, like any other scientist, is a man with curiosity, directed in his case to the working of the mind. To the curiosity he adds psychological insight and scrupulous application of the tools of his skill.

It is an inevitable step from the study of the dream and the fantasy to the study of the creation of a work of art. The biography of the artist is a prelude to the study of imagination, because in the artist and in his work we are vouchsafed insight into thought processes and fantasy formation to a degree rarely possible elsewhere.

The analyst, like any other biographer, must guard against identifying himself with the object of his study and keep in mind his aim—the elucidation of the relation of the artist's life to his work. Freud was well aware of this danger, and in Leonardo da Vinci he warned the biographer against idealizing his subject in order to gratify an infantile fantasy, such as the revival of the infantile concept of the father (23, p. 130). Of course there is also the opposite danger, ironically evident in some popular biographies of Freud, in which the biographer displaces his unconscious hostile feeling on to the subject of his biography.
